title: Diff-TCAD: Dual-Path-Conditioned Diffusion Surrogate for Real-Time TCAD Device Fields
authors: Xulin Zhang, Yupeng Hu, Cheng Zhuo, Qi Sun
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: We propose Diff-TCAD, a diffusion-based surrogate model that generates spatially resolved, strongly coupled multi-physics fields from device conditions, reducing reliance on costly PDE-based TCAD simulations. In Diff-TCAD, a dual-path conditioning mechanism injects geometry and bias through channel-wise feature modulation and context attention, enabling smooth and continuous control over operating points. A diffusion backbone with few-step sampling delivers low-latency inference while jointly predicting multiple fields in a single model. Experiments on semiconductor device benchmarks show substantial speedups over commercial TCAD tools while preserving high field-level accuracy, enabling large-scale, near-real-time surrogate device analysis.

title: Unlocking the Ocean’s Hidden Diary: Reconstructing Environmental History from the Chemical Clues in Fish Otoliths
authors: Mohith Raaj A., Isaac Jeddiah J. K., Subha K.
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: Attempting to analyze the fish ear bones, or "otoliths," to understand the ocean’s past typically involves a laborious process that demands a certain level of knowledge in the subject matter or a significant time consumption. In our present study, however, we sought to heavily rely on machine learning to simplify our process in an attempt to hasten our workload in analyzing the fish ear bones’ composition to establish deeper insights into underlying ocean conditions through the analysis of chemical composition, such as calcium or magnesium, to make further inferences regarding certain conditions in the ocean environment. In fact, machine learning may be useful in monitoring our ocean environments through a deeper analysis of otoliths, at an accuracy rate of 88%, especially in identifying areas of hypoxic conditions or areas in our ocean that have lower levels of oxygen in the water.

title: Attention Modulation of P300 and MMN: A Pilot Study Using a 2-Electrode Oddball Paradigm
authors: Ibrahim-Rida Kassem, Katia Sawaya, Tarek Houri
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: The P300 component is a well-established electrophysiological marker of attention and memory updating. However, individual variability in how conscious attention modulates P300 amplitude remains poorly understood. This pilot study examined P300 and Mismatch Negativity (MMN) during automatic (passive) versus conscious (active) auditory oddball tasks using a simplified 2-electrode setup (Fz, Cz). EEG was recorded from six healthy individuals during passive and active auditory oddball paradigms. Automatic artifact rejection removed epochs exceeding 150 µV, flat signals, or inverted polarity. P300 was identified as the maximal positive peak between 300-600 ms. MMN was calculated as the negative difference wave (Deviant minus Standard) between 100-250 ms. Active P300 (n=5) showed mean amplitude of 18.85 ± 10.18 µV at 440 ± 5 ms. Passive P300 (n=3) showed mean amplitude of 6.22 ± 2.16 µV at 445 ± 10 ms. MMN was reliably detected in both conditions (Passive: -3.63 ± 2.15 µV; Active: -2.72 ± 1.19 µV). Notably, all participants with usable paired data (n=2) showed P300 enhancement during conscious attention (mean effect: +4.11 µV). Detection rates exceeded 97% across usable conditions. This pilot study demonstrates the feasibility of a simplified 2-electrode oddball paradigm for assessing attention modulation of P300 and MMN. Preliminary findings suggest consistent P300 enhancement with conscious attention. Results support further investigation with larger samples and provide practical insights for simplified EEG protocols.

title: Tunable High-K Profile Control in 28nm Metal Gate Etching
authors: Junming Wang, Zhe Li, Ryan Ruan, Chun Gao, Taojun Zhuang, Weijun Luo, Yunpeng Yin
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: High dielectric constant (high-k, HK) materials have been developed for logic gate devices to meet the stringent demands of gate length scaling. The primary challenges in etching the widely used high-k material HfO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> include achieving a controllable etch profile for optimal device performance and ensuring high selectivity to silicon oxide to prevent silicon recess. Although extensive research has been conducted on the etching mechanisms of HfO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> layers, limited attention has been paid to the influence of etching complex gate stacks on the HfO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> etching process. Therefore, it is essential to investigate HfO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> etching characteristics within the context of complete gate stack integration to better understand variations in etching performance across different applications. In this study, we first examined conventional high-k profile control parameters, such as etching time, temperature, and BCl<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>/Cl<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> gas ratio. These parameters are sometimes limited by equipment hardware or trade-offs. Subsequently, the impact of precursor process steps, including polysilicon etching, TiN main-etch (ME), and TiN over-etch (OE), were examined, as these steps were found to significantly influence the final high-k etch profile. Finally, we present our insights and practical strategies for addressing productivity challenges arising from these pre-etch processes.

title: Deep Learning-Driven Adverse Drug Reaction Surveillance Using Online Health Data
authors: M. Umamaheswari, P. Ranjana
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: The accuracy and dependability of Adverse Drug Reaction(ADR) detection across various data sources, such as social media, pharmacovigilance reports, and molecular datasets, have greatly increased due to deep learning. Graph neural networks, BERT-CNN hybrids, Bi-LSTM with attention, and LLM-based frameworks are examples of models that regularly beat conventional machine learning techniques. Because transformer-based systems can handle noisy and unstructured text, social media-based ADR detection considerably benefits from them. The prediction of uncommon and intricate ADR connections is improved by graph-based and multi-omic fusion models. By enhancing clinical interpretability, LLM-driven ADR summary contributes to patient-level pharmacovigilance. Despite excellent performance, issues with data imbalance, cross-platform generalization, and real-time deployment still exist. The key element for enhancing detection performance is the integration of heterogeneous data sources. Overall, it has been established that pharmacovigilance is becoming a more accurate and proactive safety monitoring system thanks to deep learning. Clinical medication safety will be significantly advanced by ongoing research on scalable, real-time, and cross-domain ADR detection algorithms

title: A Novel AI Approach to Investigate Curcumin’s Potential to Delay Alzheimer’s Onset Using Genetically Modified Drosophila
authors: Zoe Fan, Shuwen Jin
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: This study examines the efficacy of curcumin, a natural polyphenolic compound derived from the turmeric plant, in helping to reduce inflammation and Alzheimer’s disease (AD) symptoms. Three experimental groups of Drosophila were used for testing: GAL4 Drosophila (control group), Drosophila acquiring AD-like symptoms, and Drosophila acquiring AD-like symptoms after curcumin treatment. Two tests were conducted to measure the health of the flies: a climbing assay test and a petri dish performance assay. Both tests use Drosophila’s movement to measure health. This study has developed an AI-based solution with identified behavioral indicators that calculates the speed and height of Drosophila. The Drosophila given curcumin in their diet was relatively more active than the others. The developed AI, OpenCV, calculated results from movement across all three testing groups. The AI OpenCV method used in this project was applied in a novel way by using its image analysis capabilities to measure and compare behavioral or cognitive pattern changes related to curcumin’s potential to delay Alzheimer’s progression, rather than for traditional object detection tasks. The Drosophila given curcumin showcased a significant increase in health, living 30% longer. The Drosophila that acquired Alzheimer-like symptoms with standard fly food died relatively quickly. They showed weaker performance in both assays due to slower mobility and relatively lower health levels. In both tests, the Drosophila that acquired Alzheimer-like symptoms with curcumin in their food moved approximately 5.5 times quicker and had progressively improved health. The results of this study demonstrate that curcumin powder increases speed and movement by 5.5-fold greater than untreated models. Statistical analysis showed highly significant differences between curcumin-treated flies and Drosophila acquiring Alzheimer-like symptoms (p = 0.0086), indicating curcumin’s positive effects. Comparing the control group and the group acquiring Alzheimer-like symptoms also showed high significance (p = 0.0075), confirming successful breeding of Drosophila. There was an insignificant difference between the curcumin group and the control group, suggesting curcumin restored normal health levels. This study illuminate’s curcumin’s efficacy and efficiency in delaying the progression of Alzheimer’s due to its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. This study also demonstrates the immediate effect of an AI-based solution to support the Alzheimer's drug and nutrition study.

title: Signal Processing-Guided Multimodal Encoder for Intelligent Bearing Fault Diagnosis
authors: Mohammed M. Farag
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: This paper presents a novel multimodal convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture with adaptive signal fusion mechanisms for bearing fault diagnosis using vibration signals. The proposed architecture employs three specialized processing branches—temporal, spectral, and spectrogram—that extract complementary discriminative features from raw vibration data through domain-specific signal transformations. A dual-channel signal processing approach is introduced to simultaneously analyze raw signals and their analytical envelopes computed via Hilbert transform, capturing both carrier frequency characteristics and amplitude modulation patterns indicative of fault conditions. The architecture incorporates two fusion strategies: concatenation-based feature combination and adaptive gated fusion with learnable attention. Comprehensive experimental validation on two industry-standard bearing fault datasets (Case Western Reserve University and Paderborn University) demonstrates superior performance compared to individual branch architectures. The multi-branch architecture achieves validation accuracies and F1-scores exceeding 99% across both datasets while maintaining computational efficiency suitable for real-time deployment. Extensive ablation studies reveal that concatenation fusion consistently outperforms gated fusion, with the spectrogram branch demonstrating the highest individual performance on complex fault signatures. The proposed architecture provides interpretable fault diagnosis through comprehensive branch visualization and feature embedding analysis, establishing a robust foundation for industrial condition-based maintenance systems.

title: MambaMon: Linear-Time State Space Models for Large-Scale Distributed System Monitoring
authors: Tejas Pravinbhai Patel, Chandrashekhar Medicherla, Arun Kumar Elengovan, Chaitanya Kulkarni
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Real-time anomaly detection in distributed systems remains computationally prohibitive for Transformer-based approaches, whose quadratic O(n<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) attention complexity is untenable for the long log sequences produced by modern microservice deployments. We present MambaMon, an architecture that leverages selective structured State Space Models (SSMs) to achieve linear-time O(n) complexity while improving detection accuracy, and that addresses three production requirements simultaneously: ultra-long sequences (8K+ tokens) that capture system-wide temporal dependencies, sub-100 ms inference latency, and a resource footprint suitable for edge deployment. Across three large-scale datasets—HDFS (11M+ events), BGL supercomputer logs (4.7M events), and Amazon Retail production logs (2.3M events)—MambaMonattains a 93.7% average F1-score, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines by 3.8% while delivering 2.7× faster inference and 4.2× faster training. Our hierarchical SSM design with bidirectional state propagation and adaptive temporal gating captures multi-scale system behavior; a sparse self-attention layer handles the remaining long-range, cross-service dependencies. A 30-day production deployment at Amazon Retail observes 94.2% anomaly detection with 67 ms P99 latency, validating MambaMon’s viability for mission-critical monitoring at scale.

title: Towards Robust Detection of Climate-Induced Rainfall Anomalies Using Mahalanobis Distance and Feature-Enriched Time Series
authors: Nikhil Sanjay, Vedhesh Dhinakaran, Kopperla Bharath Reddy, N. Neelima, T. V. Smitha, Vivek Venugopal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: The frequency of hydro-meteorological extremes that is on the rise in South Asia requires the implementation of effective anomaly detection systems for successful disaster preparedness. This paper introduces a multivariate anomaly detection methodology utilizing Mahalanobis distance for identifying compound anomalies of rainfall, soil moisture, and temperature in Baksa district, Assam, from April 2023 to October 2024. A new 14-feature dataset combining VIC model rain estimation, in-situ soil moisture, and AWS temperature measurements was created. Temporal and hydrological memory features were designed, and data imputed and standardized. Mahalanobis distance combined with the Mahalanobis-Taguchi System allowed for multivariate anomaly detection with consideration for inter-feature correlations and scale differences. Validation with ASDMA disaster logs, climatological deviation thresholds, and ENSO modulation identified a detection accuracy above 82%. The method surpassed conventional univariate approaches and provides a scalable solution to real-time monitoring of regions affected by monsoons.

title: Intelligent Fire Prediction and Detection Management Using Real-Time Grad-CAM Analysis
authors: Mahmoud Y. Shams, Mohamed Wasef, Amal Yasser, Roheet Bhatnagar, Esraa Hassan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: This paper presents an innovative approach to fire prediction and detection utilizing artificial intelligence models. The first model focuses on predicting the occurrence of fires based on key natural factors such as the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI), Land Surface Temperature (LST), and Thermal Anomalies. By anticipating potential fire outbreaks using these indicators, this model aims to enhance preparedness and minimize damage. The second model employs Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) to analyze visual data in real-time, determining the presence of fire with high accuracy. This model's rapid and versatile fire detection capabilities make it crucial for applications ranging from forest protection to public safety. Together, these models represent a significant advancement in intelligent fire management solutions. The Fire Prediction Model has an accuracy of 84.00%, while the Fire Detection Model has a higher accuracy of 98.00%.

title: Energy-Efficient Power Control and Movable Antenna Positioning in 6G Cellular Networks via Reinforcement Learning
authors: Ayaz Ahmad
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: This paper considers power control and antenna positioning in downlink 6G cellular networks with the base station (BS) quipped with movable antenna serving multiple ground users. The objective is to maximize energy efficiency (EE) defined as users’ sum-rate divided by total transmission power plus circuit power while satisfying the BS power budget constraint, the feasible region constraint for antenna movements, and minimum rate requirement per user. The problem of joint power control and antenna position optimization is formulated as an episodic Markov decision process. A feasibility-aware deterministic reinforcement learning (RL) framework based on actor–critic method with a safety layer is proposed to solve it. This framework effectively solves the problem while ensuring all the constraints. The simulation results demonstrate fast and stable learning with improved EE and sum-rate improvements compared to fixed antenna baseline across diverse BS power budgets and users’ QoS thresholds.

title: Hybrid Quantum-Classical Generative Adversarial Network for Synthetic 12-Lead Electrocardiogram Signal Generation
authors: Aron Jose A, Shamila Ebenezer
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: The development of effective deep learning systems for cardiac disease diagnosis requires extensive and diverse electrocardiogram (ECG) databases, which remain scarce due to patient confidentiality regulations and costly medical annotation procedures. This paper introduces a novel hybrid Quantum Generative Adversarial Network (QGAN) that generates realistic 12-lead ECG signals trained on the MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database. A parameterised quantum circuit (PQC) serves as the generative core, exploiting quantum superposition and entanglement to model biomedical signal distributions that traditional generators cannot efficiently replicate. The system pairs a PennyLane-based quantum generator with a 1D Convolutional Neural Network discriminator implemented in PyTorch, trained via the parameter-shift rule. Experimental results demonstrate that the Quantum PQC achieves a Fréchet Distance of 12.3 ±2.1, outperforming Classical CNN (15.8 ±3.2) and Classical LSTM (18.4 ±4.1) baselines while using fewer parameters. Generated 12-lead traces accurately reproduce P-waves, QRS complexes, T-waves, and inter-lead spatial relationships, validating quantum-augmented synthesis as a viable, privacy-preserving approach for cardiovascular AI training.

title: A High-Performance Fully Integrated RF Power Amplifier Output Matching Network Featuring an ESD Discharge Path
authors: Bingsheng Huang, Lingzhi Xu, Xiaopeng Yu, Wenxuan Zhang
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: This work presents a high-performance, fully integrated RF power amplifier (PA) output matching network (OMN) featuring an ESD discharge path between the output node and the ground node. The proposed OMN incorporates several harmonic suppression branches and an impedance matching network, utilizing only five on-chip inductors in total, with a single inductor placed in the main RF signal path. In a practical 2.4GHz fully integrated PA implementation, the proposed OMN achieves harmonic suppression of nearly −70dBc and a power-added efficiency (PAE) greater than 40% at the 1-dB compression point (P<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1dB</inf>), while occupying an area of only 0.27mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. The entire OMN is implemented in a 0.13μm CMOS technology.

title: Narrative Time and Historical Causality: A Computational Framework for Temporal and Causal Reasoning in First-Person Sources
authors: Prince Yaw Gharbin
publish: 2026 22nd International Conference on Intelligent Environments (IE)
abstract: Historical documents (letters, diaries, etc.) from the First World War contain tangled timelines, fragmented memories, and subtle causal links that complicate computational analysis. This paper introduces a Temporal-Causal Reasoning (TCR) framework that unifies event extraction, transformer-based causal classification, semantic role labelling, temporal normalization, and Allen Interval Algebra. A key novelty is the dual-layer temporal-causal event graph that models subjective (personal) causal reasoning alongside objective historical causality, enabling direct comparison between lived experience and verified timelines. Applied to first-hand trench accounts, TCR improves narrative flow, logical consistency, and interpretability. By integrating temporal sequencing and causal inference, the framework produces historically grounded narratives that support digital history, automated historiography, intelligent environments, and explainable storytelling systems.

title: The Effect of Generative AI Use on Programming Self-Efficacy in Computer Engineering Students
authors: A. M. Mutawa, Shouq Alsubaihi, Sai Sruthi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: The fast adoption of Generative AI (GenAI) tools like ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude has completely changed the way people learn to program. Even while these tools provide real-time guidance, we still don't know much about how they affect students' self-efficacy. This exploratory research examined computer engineering students (N = 24) after a foundational Java programming course. The 12-item Introductory Programming Self-Efficacy Scale (IPSES), specifically adapted for Java programming, was used to measure self-efficacy. We used the statistical package to analyze data on GenAI use and demographic characteristics using multiple linear regression models. The regression analyses indicated that GenAI usage did not significantly predict self-efficacy (p-value > 0.05) after controlling for other variables. On the other hand, the study suggested a significant difference between students' computer knowledge and programming self-efficacy. The results provide preliminary evidence that GenAI tool usage does not generally enhance programming confidence. Instead, its relationship is influenced by gender, and computer knowledge adoption trends. The findings offer preliminary insights; nonetheless, the limited sample size requires additional research with larger as well as more varied groups.

title: Forecasts of Hurricane Helene Using AI Machine Learning Models
authors: Kristen Lou, Tristan Ballard
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Accurate hurricane forecasting is vital for protecting communities and saving lives. This paper forecasts Hurricane Helene, a Category 4 storm that caused widespread damage across the southeast U.S. in 2024, using AI weather prediction (AIWP) models. The AIWP models (Nvidia’s FourCastNet, Google’s GraphCast, Microsoft’s Aurora, and Huawei’s Pangu-Weather) - billion-parameter deep learning models - were used to forecast Hurricane Helene’s speed, pressure, landfall, trajectory, and intensity, with 1-day, 2-day, and 3-day forecast windows. The prediction focused on key parameters, including the maximum 10-meter wind speed, minimum mean sea level pressure (MSLP), and the hurricane’s landfall and centroid locations. We proposed a new linear interpolation algorithm to accurately predict the time when the hurricane will hit the coast, based on the AIWP model-predicted hurricane positions just before and after landfall. We compared the AIWP forecasts to both the ERA5 reanalysis dataset and the National Hurricane Center (NHC) observed data. Our results suggest that the AIWP models, having been trained primarily on the ERA5 dataset with limited local hurricane data, faced greater challenges in accurately predicting Hurricane Helene (2024) compared to Storm Ciarán (2023). The maximum wind speed and minimum MSLP of the hurricane were both under-predicted by the AIWP models and even by ERA5, when compared to the NHC ground truth data. However, they did perform reasonably well in predicting the hurricane’s overall trajectory and landfall time and location, which is crucial for early warning systems and disaster preparedness. The AIWP models’ ability to accurately predict the hurricane’s path and movement underscores their potential for supporting future hurricane forecasting. The study suggests that further refinement and integration of more localized data may be needed for the AIWP models to be used for operational extreme weather forecasting.

title: IoT-Enabled Smart Agriculture for Automated Irrigation and Equipment Rental
authors: Rajeshwari G L, Harshitha B, Keerthana Gowda C M, Ganavi S K, Suhas S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: The Smart AgriTech architecture aims at defeating traditional farming with resource management automation. The system conducts field operations by means of an ESP32 microcontroller coupled with different sensors like soil moisture, DHT11 (temperature and humidity), carbon monoxide (CO), and infrared (IR) detectors. One of the technical advancements of the system is dual-threshold irrigation which is programmed between 30% and 40%. The system operates in the loop, which prevents over-irrigation and ensures efficient irrigation management. Instead of recording field operation activity, it provides secure, web-based booking of farming equipment. This allows farmers to avoid server conflicts and resolve common problems associated with equipment booking. Farm machinery becomes shared digital resources making it easy for local farmers to schedule it. Data obtained from the farm fields is transferred to the ThingSpeak cloud for analysis and from there to the ThingsView mobile app so that the farmer knows field conditions at any point in time. For dependable communication in rural zones with low internet coverage, a GSM-based alarm system alerts farmers of CO gas leakage, water supply in the field, and unauthorized entry in the form of SMS messages. The proposed system substantially reduces manual monitoring while boosting water efficiency and equipment usage.

title: Guardrailed Uplift Targeting: A Causal Optimization Playbook for Marketing Strategy
authors: Deepit Sapru
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: This research proposes a marketing decision framework that achieves optimal customer targeting by merging heterogeneous treatment effect estimation with concrete business guardrails. The aim is to maximize revenue and retention while abiding by constraints like budget, revenue protection and customer experience. The framework first uses uplift learners to estimate Conditional Average Treatment Effects (CATE), then solves a constrained allocation problem to allocate whom to target with which offer. It aids in decision-making for retention messaging, event rewards, and spend-threshold assignment. Through both offline simulations and online A/B test validations, the methodology outperforms the propensity and static baselines. It offers a reusable playbook for causal targeting at scale.

title: Neuromuscular Control Assessed by Entropy during Isometric Contractions in Physically Active and Sedentary Young Adults
authors: Dario Santos, Mauricio Montero, Joaquín Alvarez, Lucía Barrios, Joaquín García, Florencia Melgarejo, Joaquín Rebuffo, Macarena Trujillo, Andrea Mattiozzi, Franco Simini
publish: 2026 IEEE Medical Measurements &amp; Applications (MeMeA)
abstract: Traditionally, neuromuscular capacity has been assessed using Maximum Voluntary Isometric contraction (MVIC). MVIC, a measure that does not fully capture the complexity of motor control. The aim of this study was to quantify maximal isometric force, Rate of Force Development (RFD), and force-time signal entropy Approximate Entropy (ApEn), in the quadriceps and hamstring muscles, comparing physically active and sedentary young adults. Sixty young healthy volunteers (36 active and 24 sedentary) were evaluated using the portable device DINABANG®, which gives real-time measurements of force and joint angle. MVIC was assessed at $\mathbf{6 0}^{\boldsymbol{\circ}}$ knee flexion, while ApEn was calculated during sustained submaximal contractions performed at 60% of MVIC using a trapezoidal reference signal. Data normality was evaluated using the Shapiro-Wilk test. Between-group comparisons were conducted using independent samples t-tests for normally distributed variables and MannWhitney U tests for non-normally distributed data, with statistical significance set at $\mathbf{p}\lt \mathbf{0. 0 5}$. Physically active participants exhibited significantly greater MVIC and RFD compared with sedentary individuals. In contrast, no significant differences in ApEn were observed between groups, regardless of physical activity level. These findings indicate that entropy provides complementary and non-redundant information relative to traditional force-based measures. The similarity in ApEn values suggest that this metric primarily reflects neural motor control mechanisms and the temporal organization of the motor command rather than peripheral adaptations induced by physical training. Entropy analysis may therefore represent a valuable biomechanical tool for the early detection of neuromotor dysfunction in clinical contexts where changes are not reflected by MVIC.

title: Design and Development of Polarization Agile Sinuous Antenna for Direction Finding Applications
authors: N. M. Mary Sindhuja, E. Jerlace Selin, S. Viveka
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Sinuous antennas for high-precision direction finding systems have been developed in response to the need for wideband, polarization-agile antennas in defense and aerospace applications. Over ultra-wide bandwidths, conventional frequency-independent antennas like spiral and log-periodic structures show limitations in terms of compactness, impedance stability, and polarization purity. This work presents the design and development of a four arm polarization-agile sinuous antenna operating between 2 and 18 GHz to address these issues. Stable input impedance and dual circular polarization over several octaves are guaranteed by the antenna geometry, which is based on log-periodic scaling and self-complementary principles. An exponentially tapered microstrip balun is integrated with the radiating structure to convert the sinuous radiator's theoretical 188.5 Ω impedance into the conventional 50 Ω feed. Full-wave electrom is used to optimize the design, which is implemented on a FR4 substrate.

title: Source and Curvy-Mask Optimization with Continuous Tone Quantization
authors: Wenjing He, Jiale Liu, Yijiang Shen
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Source-mask optimization (SMO) typically mitigates the gap between the imaging capacity of ArF lithography and the process requirements at 28 nm technology nodes and beyond. This paper extends such mitigation by enabling concurrent optimization of the illumination and curvy masks with complied manufacturability. To this end, co-optimization of illumination and continuous transmission masks (CTMs) is performed where the CTMs are then divided into main feature and sub-resolution assist features (SRAFs) regions with various orders. A quantization term is defined so that any mask value can move away from where it is without any particular bias to either allowed tones. By gradually mixing-in the quantization term into SMO in a staged way, the mask tones are penalized to assumed values of being opaque or transparent quasistatically. Simulation results show improved lithography performance and mask manufacturability.

title: AI-enabled three-layer method to strengthen cancer patient safety plans at specialized centers in Peru
authors: Cristina Aldana, Luis Vilcahuaman, Kevin Vila
publish: 2026 IEEE Medical Measurements &amp; Applications (MeMeA)
abstract: Peruvian oncology referral centers use patient safety plans, safety walkrounds, incident reporting and indicators, but operational gaps remain in taxonomy consistency, signal integration, prioritization and timely closure of corrective actions. Objective: To propose a three-layer, AIenabled method that strengthens oncology patient-safety planning and execution across sites under human-in-the-loop governance. Methods: Layer 1 transforms institutional plans and annexes into computable management structures and a gap register, enabling auditable accountability through explicit owners, timelines, indicators, denominators and closure evidence. Layer 2 consolidates incident reports, walkround findings and indicator series into a dynamic risk profile, standardizing classification and prioritization to detect recurrent, high-impact risks by service and process stage. Layer 3 converts prioritized risks and plan gaps into measurable actions managed through workflow governance, tracking status and verifying closure with documentary evidence. The Patient Safety Committee validates classifications, prioritization and proposed actions, ensuring transparency, regulatory alignment and prevention of uncontrolled automation. The method closes the operational gap between written plans, observed safety signals and measurable actions, enabling cross-site comparability and KPI-driven monitoring of recurrence reduction and closure performance. The approach supports cross-site benchmarking and sustained organizational learning

title: Beyond Agent Design: A Systematic Framework for MCP Server Runtime Orchestration
authors: Vijayakumar Venganti, Deepak Kole, Siva Prasad Nandi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) has rapidly emerged as a standard interface for connecting AI agents to external tools, databases, and services. While considerable research has addressed agent design, prompt engineering, and tool selection, the runtime infrastructure layer that hosts MCP servers remains unstudied. This paper argues that production failures in agentic systems arise primarily from runtime and orchestration mismatches rather than from deficiencies in agent logic. We present a systematic decision framework comprising a six-dimension workload characterization rubric, a four-tier runtime taxonomy (serverless/FaaS, container-based, VM/bare metal, and managed orchestration), a decision matrix mapping workload profiles to runtime tiers, and a comparative evaluation across four production-relevant metrics. Two illustrative case studies demonstrate that applying the framework reduces latency variability by up to 73% and operational incident rate by over 60% compared to ad hoc runtime selection. This work establishes runtime orchestration as a first-class design concern in production agentic systems and provides practitioners with actionable, cloud-agnostic guidance for MCP server deployment.

title: Empowering Data-Driven Smart Cycling Initiatives: a Comprehensive Analysis of Existing Datasets for Bike Maintenance
authors: Gabriel Eggert, Daniel G. Costa
publish: 2026 22nd International Conference on Intelligent Environments (IE)
abstract: Bike-sharing systems have become an essential component of sustainable urban mobility, although scalability, safety, and cost-efficient service management remain important challenges in smart cities. When managing shared bike fleets, predictive maintenance has proven effective to reduce costs and prevent disruptions, but real-world applicability remains understudied, largely due to limited access to comprehensive maintenance records. This paper addresses this gap by collecting and analyzing maintenance-related data from multiple international bike-sharing operators, allowing us to explore different patterns of component degradation across diverse climates, fleet configurations, and usage behaviors. For that, this work identifies and compares available open and proprietary datasets, highlighting their major characteristics and assessing their potential to support advanced predictive models. Building on these insights, opportunities for smart cycling applications grounded in artificial intelligence and data science are discussed, outlining how datadriven maintenance strategies can enhance operational efficiency, reduce environmental and economic costs, and contribute to safer, more sustainable smart cities.

title: Design and Implementation of a Voice-Controlled Robotic Arm
authors: Odgerel Ayurzana
publish: 2025 International Symposium on Computer Science and Educational Technology (ISCSET)
abstract: This paper presents the design, development, and implementation of a voice-controlled robotic arm featuring three joints and five degrees of freedom. The system integrates a smartphone application, developed using MIT App Inventor, with a microcontroller to enable wireless control via Wi-Fi. The application utilizes Google's cloud-based Speech-to-Text API to convert spoken commands into text, which is then processed and transmitted to the robotic arm. The arm comprises six servo motors and multiple joints, enabling precise multi-directional movements and object manipulation. Inverse kinematics was employed to determine the rotational angles of the robotic arm's joints. It is capable of executing commands such as rotating left or right, moving up or down, gripping, and releasing objects. A usability study involving six participants demonstrated an average command recognition accuracy of 89 %, with performance influenced by the clarity of English pronunciation. These results indicate the system's potential for accessible, lowcost robotic control in various applications.

title: Shrinkage of Pattern CD by Using Inductively Coupled Plasma for Logic MOL Dielectric Layers Etch Application
authors: Junkai Yan, Litian Xu, Guo Li, Jiwei Lin
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Plasma etch technology has become the standard patterning technology to bridge the Photo resolution gap between 193 immersion and EUV lithography. We introduce the art of small-contact etch process by new in-situ patterning approaches to form 20nm hole CD with 70nm pattern CD of incoming multi-layer photo resist mask. Small contact holes are achieved by mean of not only excess fluorocarbon deposition on the sidewall but also free of etch stop. The mechanism of the reduced etch stop by quasi-ALE is also investigated during the shrinkage of contact hole.

title: AI-Driven Pseudo-3D Mesh Reconstruction from 2D Fetal Ultrasound for Accessible Prenatal Visualization
authors: Omar Mohamed Elwan, Karim Mohamed AboalAzm, Mazen Ebrahim, Tamer M. Nassef
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Most prenatal ultrasound examinations worldwide are still performed using standard two-dimensional devices, forcing clinicians to mentally combine thin slices into a three-dimensional understanding. Although learning-based 3D reconstruction methods exist, they typically require tracked freehand sweeps, multiple aligned views, or paired 3D ground truth that is difficult to obtain in many clinics and public datasets. This paper presents a low-resource pipeline that converts a single 2D fetal ultrasound frame into a pseudo-3D scalar field and extracts a surface mesh using Marching Cubes, followed by lightweight cleanup and visualization. The method prioritizes interpretability and reproducibility of geometry extraction under single-frame constraints. Evaluation uses single-frame-valid measures: IoU between the extracted mask and rendered mesh silhouette, mesh size/quality statistics, and runtime. The work supports SDG 3 (Good Health and Well-Being) by improving access to prenatal visualization and SDG 10 (Reduced Inequalities) by targeting settings where 3D/4D ultrasound hardware is uncommon.

title: A Data-Driven Approach to Predicting Menstrual Irregularity in Women with PCOS and Thyroid Disorders
authors: Tharini Kuzhali Senthilkumar, Aram Bahrini, Rastin Mastali Majdabadkohne
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: Menstrual irregularity is a common health concern among women, frequently associated with endocrine disorders such as polycystic ovary syndrome and thyroid dysfunction. This study examines the relationship between polycystic ovary syndrome status, thyroid-stimulating hormone, luteinizing hormone, follicle-stimulating hormone, insulin resistance, and lifestyle indicators in predicting menstrual irregularity. Using a publicly available dataset of 468 women, of whom 271 had regular cycles and 197 had irregular cycles, three ensemble classifiers, including Random Forest, XGBoost, and Gradient Boosting, were evaluated via five-fold stratified cross-validation. Thyroid-stimulating hormone values were classified into clinical hyperthyroid, normal, and hypothyroid categories and encoded as binary engineered features, expanding the feature set to 16. A key methodological finding was that applying the synthetic minority oversampling technique prior to cross-validation inflated the mean cross-validation AUC from an honest 0.5449 to a misleading 0.7308, an overestimation of 0.1859. Following correction, the honest testset AUC was 0.6208, with sensitivity of 50.0% and specificity of 64.8%. A three-tier risk stratification system produced a clinically meaningful gradient, with actual irregularity rates of 29.0%, 41.9%, and 56.2% across Low, Moderate, and High tiers, respectively. By integrating predictive modeling with hormonal, metabolic, and clinical data, this study demonstrates a risk-stratification approach for identifying women at elevated risk of menstrual irregularity and highlights the value of transparent, leakage-free methodology in clinical machine learning.

title: A Study on LDD Optimization for HCI Reliability Enhancement in 55nm 3.3V IO NMOS Devices
authors: Wenfeng Ding, Ling Tang, Tao Xiong, Liyun Wu, Zhuo-JieWu
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: HCI (Hot Carrier Injection) effect is a significant concern for device reliability, particularly in NMOSFET. As device dimensions shrink and channel lengths become shorter, the impact of HCI on device performance becomes increasingly pronounced. Based on the HCI-induced failure issue observed in 55nm 3.3V I/O NMOS devices, this study aims to optimize junction characteristics by adjusting the LDD (Lightly Doped Drain) profile to modify the doping distribution, thereby improving HCI. This paper elaborates on the structure and function of LDD, discusses optimization strategies for LDD design, and explains the mechanism behind HCI improvement using TCAD simulations. The optimized design achieves a 192-times enhancement in HCI lifetime compared to the initial baseline.

title: An Attention-Guided Deep Learning Framework for Brain Tumor Detection Using Multimodal MR Imaging
authors: Ibrahim Abdelhaliem, Oluwatunmise Akinniyi, Joseph Aina, Jose Dixon, Shiva Mehravaran, Md Mahmudur Rahamn, Gehad A. Saleh, Fahmi Khalifa
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Precise identification of brain tumors is essential for prompt and effective clinical treatment. Recently, AI-powered diagnostic platforms have gained prominence as impactful solutions, streamlining classification tasks and supporting swift, unbiased clinical decisions. In this work, we developed AI-based approach for classifying brain tumors. It introduces a multi-modal MRI framework that combines Diffusion-Weighted MRI (DW-MRI) with T2-weighted MRI (T2-MRI). To align spatial dimensions across imaging types while preserving critical visual details, T2-MRI scans are adjusted using a High-Frequency Information Retention (HFIR) method. The proposed model utilizes a dual-path 3D Convolutional Neural Network (3D CNN) to capture deep, modality-specific features. To further refine the representation of tumor characteristics, an Adaptive Region Attention (ARA) mechanism is incorporated, directing focus toward shared, highly discriminative regions across both modalities, thereby enriching tumor analysis. The model was tested on a custom-labeled brain MRI dataset comprising three tumor categories. Results highlight its effectiveness, with an accuracy of 92.86%, a sensitivity of 80.00%, and a specificity of 94.12%. Comparative statistical evaluations validate the model’s superiority over current leading techniques, reinforcing its reliability and advanced performance for accurate brain tumor classification.

title: ArabTutor: An Arabic Intelligent Tutoring System Powered by GPT-4 for Primary Education
authors: Wala’a Shehada, Huthaifa I. Ashqar
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: This paper presents ArabTutor, an intelligent tutoring system built on OpenAI’s GPT-4 architecture, aimed at enhancing educational accessibility for Arabic-speaking primary school students. The system supports three core subjects (Arabic Language, Mathematics, and Science) and enables students to interact using Modern Standard Arabic (MSA). ArabTutor provides concise, contextually accurate answers, maintains a short conversation history, incorporates subject selection, and includes a feedback-logging mechanism. Implemented with Python and Gradio, responses are logged to CSV files. A key feature is a "flag" function to report potentially incorrect responses for review. Unlike earlier Arabic tutoring systems that relied on static or rule-based agents, ArabTutor leverages GPT-4 to deliver flexible, adaptive explanations. Evaluation based on 30 representative questions (10 per subject), conceptually extended to a broader set of 90 items across subjects and grade levels, reviewed by educators, showed 100% accuracy in Mathematics and Science and 90% in Arabic Language. The single error, hamza-type misclassifications, highlights challenges in fine-grained orthography. ArabTutor demonstrates the feasibility of LLM-powered tutoring for Arabic education and contributes toward closing the gap in Arabic-language AI applications. Code and data for reproducing our experiments are available in our GitHub repository (https://github.com/walaashe/ArabTutor).

title: Implementation of Sentiment Prescriptive Analytics in a Web-Based Library User Satisfaction System Using Deep Learning
authors: Jerry I. Teleron, Vanessa Gay R. Liabor, Niel Carlo D. Asgar, Virginia P. Mollaneda
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: This study presents the development and evaluation of a web-based Library User Satisfaction System that integrates deep learning-based sentiment analysis and rule-based prescriptive analytics to support data-driven decision-making in academic libraries. The system was developed using the Design and Development Research (DDR) approach, which includes analysis, design and development, and evaluation phases. A Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) model with an attention mechanism was implemented to analyze textual feedback and classify sentiments into positive, neutral, and negative categories. In addition, a rule-based prescriptive analytics module evaluates survey scores across library service dimensions—resources, facilities, technology, and services—to identify areas requiring improvement and generate recommendations. The model was trained using 10,000 labeled feedback comments, achieving 100% accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score on the validation dataset. The system also provides an interactive dashboard for monitoring satisfaction indicators and sentiment distribution. Results indicate that the technology dimension received the lowest satisfaction score, highlighting the need for improvements in technological services. Overall, the system demonstrates the potential of combining BiLSTM-based sentiment analysis and prescriptive analytics to transform user feedback into actionable insights for improving library services.

title: Investigating The Correlation Between Mechanical Plastic Deformation And The Characteristics Of Zinc Whisker Growth On Galvanized Steel Plate
authors: Asmita Bhandari, Bivushi Timilsina, Samia Sultana, Michelle Gervasio
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: Zinc whiskers are spontaneous metallic filaments that can form on galvanized steel surfaces and pose significant reliability concerns for electrical systems due to their potential to break and fall onto sensitive circuit components. Despite documented failures associated with zinc whiskers, the role of mechanically induced stress in promoting whisker formation remains insufficiently understood. This work focuses on investigating a correlation between the characteristics of zinc whisker formation on galvanized steel and the level of controlled uniaxial tensile deformation. Dogbone-shaped tensile specimens were machined from galvanized steel. Specimens were annealed at 150°C for one hour prior to being elongated to prescribed levels of strain using a universal testing machine. The specimens were characterized using SEM to document initial surface conditions and were then aged at 30°C under ambient humidity to promote whisker growth, and periodically imaged with SEM to identify whisker initiation and evolution. Preliminary results indicate that higher levels of tensile deformation lead to an increased number of zinc whiskers, suggesting a strong influence of mechanically induced stress on whisker nucleation and growth.

title: ALD TiN Step Coverage Improvement for Ultra-High AR
authors: Guobiao Xue, Siqi Wang
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: ALD TiN is widely used in advanced logic, memory and packaging area due to its outstanding electrical and barrier properties, w/ low resistivity and ultra-high step coverage ability, such as capacitors’ electrodes and barrier layers with ultra-high aspect ratio (AR). Here we deposited ALD TiN thin film by our self-designed chamber, investigated the film’s basic properties and step coverage capability, and consequently analyzed the key ALD TiN process parameters’ impact and mechanism on deposition response in ultra-high AR trenches (AR >50). With optimized process conditions, we successfully achieved repeatable >90% step coverage performance and the film showed extremely good conformality.

title: Efficient Voice Identification System Utilizing Wav2Vec2.0 and HuBERT Based on the Quran Reciters Dataset
authors: Aly Moustafa, Salah A. Aly
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Current authentication and trusted systems depend on classical and biometric methods to recognize or authorize users. Such methods include audio speech recognitions, eye, and finger signatures. Recent tools utilize deep learning and transformers to achieve better results. In this paper, we develop a deep learning constructed model for Arabic speakers’ identification by using wav2vec and HuBERT audio representation learning tools. The end-to-end Wav2vec2.0 paradigm acquires contextualized speech representations learning’s by randomly masking a set of feature vectors, and then applies a transformer neural network.We employ MLP classifier that is able to differentiate between invariant labeled classes. We show several experimental results based on the Quran Reciters Dataset that safeguard the high accuracy of the proposed model. The experiments ensure that an arbitrary wave signal for a certain speaker can be identified with 98% and 97.1% accuracies in cases of wav2vec2.0 and HuBERT, respectively.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Deep Neural Networks for Reliable and Selective Decision-Making in Medical Image Classification
authors: Krishna Kishor Tirupati, Naresh Kumar Methuku, Pradeep Kumar Chilukury, Sai Reddy Busi Reddy
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Artificial intelligence systems used in safety critical areas tend to make overconfident predictions without knowing their own uncertainty, making them unreliable for decision making in real life. This work proposes an uncertainty-aware deep learning framework for robust medical image classification based on the DermaMNIST dataset, and employs the Monte Carlo Dropout technique within a convolutional neural network to model the uncertainty of the prediction. Besides traditional classification, the approach allows calibrated confidence estimation and selective prediction. Experimental results obtain accuracy of 0.7362 with macro F1-score of 0.4311 and good calibration of Expected Calibration Error of 0.0363. Uncertainty analysis shows just how much incorrect predictions have uncertainty which almost double (0.0020109) the uncertainty of correct predictions (0.0010636). Furthermore, selective prediction increases reliability significantly in accuracy of prediction with 0.9302 at 40% coverage and 1.0000 at 10% coverage. These findings signal the benefits of uncertainty-aware models in trustworthy, risk-sensitive, AI decision-making.

title: A Probabilistic Framework for Scene-Context Facial Emotion Recognition
authors: Luis Eduardo Arenas-Deseano, Juan Manuel Ramirez-Cortes, Pilar Gomez-Gil, Hayde Peregrina-Barreto
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The incorporation of scene contextual information may provide substantial improvements in facial emotion recognition performance. A face-only model could assign an emotion that matches facial appearance but not the social setting in which the subject appears. In this work, we incorporate scene context as an explicit prior in a dual-stream architecture for emotion recognition. The contextual stream uses MobileNetV2 (224×224×3, width multiplier 1.0) to classify seven scene categories: Customer Service, Fitness, Funeral, Office, Party, Restaurant, and Store. A lightweight facial model processes the face in parallel. Both outputs are fused through a confidence-gated reweighting step that favors emotions compatible with the detected scene and attenuates incompatible ones. To reduce rapid variations across consecutive frames, both model outputs were filtered with an exponential moving average before the final fusion stage. The behavior of the proposed framework was examined through four indicators, namely PSC, IDC, ISR, and VSC, in order to quantify how the contextual prior affected the final prediction. For the sequence test, the fused output had reduced entropy compared to the face-only prediction indicating that, after incorporating context into predicting, the probability mass became more concentrated than before. As expected, the submatrices of 3D correlations also showed this trend: the post-fusion arrangement of affective categories was less spread out and aligned more closely to the detected scene than they were before fusion. The full system was written in Python 3 and evaluated on Jetson Orin and Raspberry Pi hardware, sustaining execution rates above 30 FPS during online inference. Taken together, these observations indicate that scene-dependent priors help reduce emotion assignments that are difficult to justify from the surrounding social context.

title: A Differential Geometric Control Approach for Series Resonant Converter
authors: Arsalan Rasoolzadeh, Sayed Amir Hashemi, Bahram Jahanbakhshi Pordanjani, Majid Pahlevani
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: Resonant converters are extensively utilized in applications requiring high power density and conversion efficiency. Nevertheless, their control design remains a significant challenge due to the nonlinear characteristics that vary with both switching frequency and operating conditions. This challenge becomes particularly pronounced during transient events, where an aggressive control response may force the converter to lose zero-voltage-switching (ZVS) operation, thereby increasing switching losses and compromising system reliability. In this work, a differential geometric control approach is proposed for Series Resonant Converter (SRC) that leverages both frequency modulation and phase-shift control to achieve a fast and smooth transient response while maintaining ZVS of the power switches under all operating conditions. A Lie algebraic scheme is proposed to derive the control law, which is shown to ensure Globally Asymptotically Stable (GAS) across all converter operating conditions, including variations in output voltage setpoints, input voltages, load currents, and switching frequencies. A series of simulations and experimental tests are carried out to validate the proposed method, confirming its capability to maintain accurate ZVS operation during transients as well as under steady-state conditions.

title: Not All Extracted Information Is Credible: Toward Credibility-Aware Coverless Image Steganography in Distributed Cloud Services
authors: Bobiao Guo, Ping Ping, Yingchi Mao, Q. M. Jonathan Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computers
abstract: Image steganography has emerged as a promising channel for transmitting task assignments, authentication credentials, and scheduling metadata across distributed cloud nodes without raising suspicion. However, existing steganography methods primarily focus on robustness against attacks while overlooking the credibility of the extracted information. In distributed cloud environments, blindly accepting incorrect or manipulated data can propagate faults across nodes, trigger inconsistent system states, or even cause cascading service failures. To address this, we propose C2IS, a Credibility-Aware Coverless Image Steganography framework designed for distributed cloud services. C2IS enables cloud nodes not only to extract hidden information, but also to assess its credibility before execution or propagation. Specifically, we propose a polar harmonic transform-based feature extraction and selection strategy that extracts both coarse-grained and fine-grained features. The coarse-grained features, characterized by their high stability under various attacks, are utilized to carry secret information. Meanwhile, the fine-grained features quantify the degree of feature perturbation to assess information credibility. Furthermore, we develop a double median thresholding-based hash mapping algorithm, which binarizes inter-image feature differences using the median, thereby significantly enhancing the diversity of the generated hash sequences. Extensive experimental results show that our method achieves a complete hash sequence length of up to 13 bits, enabling higher hiding capacity. Meanwhile, under various attacks, it exhibits stronger robustness than state-of-the-art methods and can accurately determine whether the extracted information contains errors, providing a trustworthy means of covert communication for distributed cloud services.

title: QS-BTrust: A Quantum-Secure Privacy-Preserving protocol with Revocation for Trusted Broadcasting in Integrated Vehicular Networks
authors: Mahima Mary Mathews, V Panchami
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Integrated Vehicular Networks (IVNs) require the exchange of large volumes of safety-critical broadcast messages, where ensuring message origin authenticity and data integrity can be more critical than message confidentiality. Many existing authenticated broadcast mechanisms either rely on classical public-key cryptographic techniques that are vulnerable to quantum adversaries or suffer from significant verification and revocation overhead under dense traffic conditions. This paper proposes QS-BTrust, a quantum-secure and privacy-preserving authenticated broadcast protocol that combines Physical Unclonable Functions (PUFs), post-quantum digital signatures, and Hashgraph-based authentication tag management. QS-BTrust enables immediate message authentication with constant-time verification complexity, supports pseudo-indistinguishable identities to achieve unlinkability, and enables revocation without introducing additional message overhead, even in dense traffic scenarios. The security of QS-BTrust is analyzed under the Dolev–Yao (DY) adversary model to establish protocol-level robustness against active network attacks and is formally validated using ProVerif to verify broadcast authenticity and resistance to message forgery. The protocol is further analyzed under both the Random Oracle Model (ROM) and the Quantum Random Oracle Model (QROM), demonstrating robustness against replay, impersonation, message forgery, Sybil, and quantum-assisted attacks. Security guarantees are evaluated using adversarial success probability, unforgeability, and impersonation advantage as attack metrics. Simulation-based performance evaluation further shows that QS-BTrust achieves lower verification overhead under high message loads compared to representative broadcast authentication schemes, while providing quantum resistance. These characteristics make QS-BTrust well suited for large-scale and critical vehicular communication environments.

title: A Finite Elements-Based Method of Modeling The Eddy-Current Losses in Ribbon Ring-Shaped Cores With Gaps
authors: Roman Szewczyk, Anna Ostaszewska-Liżewska, Przemysław Zackiewicz, Adam Pilśniak, Marcin Polak, Adrian Radoń, Aleksandra Kolano-Burian
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: Finemet-type nanocrystalline alloys are promising materials for inductive wound choke cores, with their magnetic properties tunable through controlled recrystallization of amorphous ribbons. Eddy current losses can be effectively changed by adjusting the ribbon’s effective conductivity, which depends on stochastic, interlayer contact characteristics. The magnetodynamic behavior of such cores can also be regulated by introducing and optimizing air gaps in the magnetic circuit to control the magnetic flux fringing effect. Modeling of a Finemet-type ribbon ring-shaped core with a double gap, using an open-source toolchain combining NETGEN, ELMER FEM, and GNU-OCTAVE, showed strong agreement between simulation and experimental measurements. The results establish a validated framework for extending the applicability of the Steinmetz law and reveal that the effective conductivity exhibits a linear frequency dependence when plotted on a log-log scale.

title: STAR-SRAM: 16-bit Floating-Point SRAM-Based Digital Computing-in-Memory Macro in a 28 nm
authors: Chuan-Tung Lin, Jonghyun Oh, Mingoo Seok
publish: IEEE Journal of Solid-State Circuits
abstract: A digital computing-in-memory (DCIM) macro emerges as a promising building block in a deep neural network (DNN) accelerator. To better support DNN workloads, circuit designers aim to improve three main metrics for macros: energy efficiency, compute density, and weight density. Improvements in those metrics directly translate into reduced energy consumption, shorter inference latency, and a smaller silicon footprint at the accelerator level. This article introduces STAR-SRAM, a DCIM macro designed for 16-bit brain floating-point (BF16) arithmetic. The macro is designed using a top-down approach. We started the design process with model-based parameter optimization, in which we determined the optimal design parameters that maximize the figure of merit (FoM) of a DCIM macro. Then, we developed several circuit techniques that further improve the FoM, including pre-multiply symmetric alignment, approximate multiplier circuits, and sparsity-aware wordline, clock, and input gating techniques. Fabricated in 28-nm CMOS, STAR-SRAM achieves the state-of-the-art FoM with up to 43.06 TFLOPS/W, 1.89 TFLOPS/mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, and 400 Kb/mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>.

title: Distributed Brillouin fiber optic temperature sensing for hydrogeology
authors: Maxime Romanet, Jordan Labbe, Alexandre Matic, Maxime Zerbib, Philippe Amiotte-Suchet, Xavier Bertrand, Thomas Karbowiak, Kien Phan Huy, Hélène Celle, Jean-Charles Beugnot
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: In this paper, we demonstrate application of Brillouin-based distributed fiber optic sensors for hydrogeology, conducted at an experimental site located at Port Douvot, near the city of Besanc¸on in East of France. The flow measurement is achieved using an active heating method and distributed fiber sensor instrument with spatial resolution of 1 meter, a temperature resolution of 0.2 °C, and an acquisition time of 1 min 30s. Brillouin-based instruments offer the advantage of frequency-dependent temperature measurements, simplifying the system setup.

title: Soft, Scalable Spacer-Knitted Piezoresistive Pressure Sensors with Tunable Localized Sensitivity Enabled by Heat-Expandable Yarn Inlays
authors: Aftab Ahmed, Rahul Bhaumik, Rachele Didero, Michael Haller
publish: IEEE Sensors Letters
abstract: Soft textile pressure sensors provide superior comfort for wearable systems but often suffer from limited compressibility, tunability, and complex post-processing. A fully textile-based spacer-knitted piezoresistive pressure sensor is fabricated in a single step using a standard V-bed flat knitting machine with heat-expandable yarn inlays. The expandable inlays form highly compliant spacer regions, yielding effective compressive moduli of 54 and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{77}\,\text{kPa}$</tex-math></inline-formula> for the two- and four-inlay configurations. Sensor sensitivity is tunable through sensor size and internal architecture, ranging from <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$-1.067$</tex-math></inline-formula> to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$-0.251\,\mathrm{\%\;kPa^{-1}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> for sensor sizes between 2.5 and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{7.5}\,\text{cm}$</tex-math></inline-formula> and from <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$-0.678$</tex-math></inline-formula> to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$-0.180\,\mathrm{\%\;kPa^{-1}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> by varying spacer inlays and resistive yarn plies, while maintaining linear responses up to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{60}\,\text{kPa}$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Stable operation over <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10{,}000$</tex-math></inline-formula> loading cycles and preserved electrical integrity after 10 washing cycles confirm suitability for long-term wearable and non-wearable pressure sensing applications. The real-world applicability of the proposed spacer-knitted pressure-sensitive structures is further demonstrated by a knee cuff and a 2×2 sensor matrix.

title: Temporal Information and Feature Fusion for RGBT Tracking
authors: Di Yuan, Haiping Zhang, Xuyang Li, Qiao Liu, Zhenyu He
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Robust multi-modal sensing is essential for reliable object tracking in complex environments. Although RGB-Thermal (RGBT) sensing leverages complementary spectral modalities to enhance tracking performance, existing methods often rely on static template matching, thereby neglecting the inherent temporal continuity of sensor streams. This limitation compromises tracking stability under challenging sensing conditions, such as abrupt illumination changes, severe occlusions, and thermal crossover. To address these challenges, this paper proposes TFTrack, A new type of closed-loop video stream RGBT tracking framework has adopted a deep-collaborative two-component time design: a dynamic template library, which provides spatial priors for trajectory modeling by storing the prediction box results of historical templates; and autoregressive time label propagation, which compensates for the continuity deficiency of pure template methods. Unlike traditional sparse sampling paradigms, TFTrack exploits continuous temporal informatics by integrating historical appearance features and motion trajectories within a dynamic template library, ensuring consistent perception across time. Furthermore, to maximize multi-sensor synergy, we develop an Input Feature Guide Attention Fusion (IFGAFusion) module. By dynamically recalibrating spatial importance weights under the guidance of input features, this module effectively fuses heterogeneous data streams from RGB and Thermal Infrared (TIR) sensors. Extensive qualitative and quantitative experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance. Notably, TFTrack operates at 30 FPS on the LasHeR benchmark with a precision rate of 74.7% and a success rate of 59.6%, satisfying the stringent real-time and reliability requirements for practical vision-based sensing systems.

title: Enhancing Scalability in MARL-Based BVR Engagement: A Role-Aware Transformer Approach With Adaptive Attention
authors: Haoran Han, Jian Cheng, Maolong Lv
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) has gained increasing popularity in the beyond visual range (BVR) engagement of unmanned combat aerial vehicles (UCAVs). To enhance the scalability of the MARL-based BVR engagement, this paper presents the role-aware transformer, upgrading the architecture from the perspective of attention, head, and mask, respectively. We first introduce distinct attention matrices to fuse the self, friend, and enemy observations of each UCAV, overcoming the information confusion caused by the identical embedding matrices. Then, addressing the hybrid action space of the UCAV, we construct role-aware heads. The embedding associated with the self observation is utilized for maneuvering decision, while that of enemy observation is utilized for missile hit probability prediction. Self-play scheme is adopted for data collection, where reinforcement and supervision losses are applied to optimize the maneuvering and firing decision, respectively. Lastly, to address the distribution drift of attention calculation when dealing with an enlarged engagement scale, we introduce the role-aware mask mechanism. This method enables adaptive attention by deactivating unimportant scores and thus mitigates the distribution drift caused by the enlarged scale. The experimental results demonstrate the strong effectiveness and scalability of the proposed method.

title: CSD-GAN: Content and Style Decoupled Generative Adversarial Network for Unsupervised Multimodal Remote Sensing Image Change Detection
authors: Zhifu Zhu, Xiping Yuan, Shu Gan, Weidong Luo, Cheng Chen, Xuan Li
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Multimodal change detection (CD) effectively improves the flexibility and timeliness of emergency response by exploiting complementary information from different sensors. However, significant differences in imaging modalities lead to a lack of comparability across multimodal images, posing a severe challenge to change identification. Existing studies have alleviated this issue to some extent by introducing generative adversarial networks (GANs) to align multimodal images into a unified image domain. Nevertheless, these methods usually adopt a coupled encoding of semantic content and visual style, which easily leads to mutual interference and semantic drift between the two, thereby degrading the quality of modality alignment and the subsequent CD accuracy. Moreover, insufficient hierarchical feature modeling and the lack of effective background noise suppression further constrain their performance in practical applications. To this end, we propose a novel content and style decoupled GAN (CSD-GAN) for unsupervised multimodal CD. This method first employs a carefully designed explicit decoupling strategy to separate content and style representations, effectively suppressing semantic drift and significantly enhancing modality alignment quality. Subsequently, a spatial and semantic feature refinement (SSFR) mechanism is tailored to the distinct characteristics of shallow and deep features, thereby providing richer feature support for both modality alignment and CD. Finally, a dual-domain sparse difference perception (DSDP) module is developed to suppress background noise via cosine similarity and masked sparsity strategies, while exploiting cross domain interactions to mine multimodal complementary information, substantially enhancing the model's responsiveness to true changes. Systematic experiments conducted on five representative datasets demonstrate that CSD-GAN achieves competitive detection performance compared with existing methods.

title: Flexible Optical Macrocurvature Sensor Mounted on a Robotic Hand for Static Posture Recognition
authors: Walter O. C. Flores, Marcia Muller, José L. Fabris, Uilian J. Dreyer, José J. A. Mendes Júnior, André E. Lazzaretti
publish: IEEE Sensors Letters
abstract: This work reports a flexible optical sensor mounted on a robotic hand for static posture recognition. The sensor consists of a standard telecom optical fiber containing macrobends encapsulated in silicone elastomer. Spectral transmittance in the 400–800 nm range was used as the sensor response. A dataset containing sensor responses to 32 binary configurations of the 5 fingers, acquired over 4 consecutive days, was presented to the machine learning model. Classification was performed using a support vector classifier with a linear kernel and evaluated using a single-day training and cross-day testing protocol to estimate inter-day generalization. When training with Days 2, 3, and 4, test accuracies of 99.2%, 99.8%, and 96.7% were obtained, respectively; training with a single sample per class (Day 1) yielded an accuracy of 87.9%. Under the adopted protocol and experimental conditions, the results show that macrocurvature-induced spectral changes can be used to discriminate among static postures of a robotic hand.

title: Super-Aligned Carbon Nanotube-Based Resistor Array for High-Speed and Polarization Infrared Scene Simulation
authors: Peng Zhang, Zhihui Yang, Shuting Yang, Zhiyu Chen, Yiming Li, Dong Li, Zeyu Deng, Hongmei Zhong, Zhong Zhang, Yang Zhao
publish: Journal of Microelectromechanical Systems
abstract: The resistor array generates infrared radiation by Joule heating to simulate complex infrared scenes, helping evaluate the performance of the infrared imaging system. However, it remains challenging to simultaneously achieving resistor arrays with high-speed response and polarized emission. Here, we present a super-aligned carbon nanotube (SA-CNT) based RA, leveraging its ultralow thermal mass for high-speed response and intrinsic optical anisotropy for polarized emission without external optics. We fabricated a RA comprising <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2\times 15$</tex-math> </inline-formula> pixels that exhibits both high-speed thermal response and polarization-dependent emission. Scaling predicts that reducing the pixel pitch to the state-of-the-art <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$46~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m could enable sub-2 ms response time and frame rates exceeding 500 Hz, which remains a projection, as thermal dynamics are not strictly linear under scaling. Polarization measurements reveal pronounced anisotropic thermal emission consistent with Malus’s law, yielding a polarization radiance ratio of 1.6. Our approach opens up new ways for next-generation infrared scene simulation system. [2026-0040]
