title: A Hybrid Framework for Mid-Term Household Energy Consumption Forecast
authors: Mehrnaz Miri, Mario Gianni, Dominik Wojtczak
publish: 2026 22nd International Conference on Intelligent Environments (IE)
abstract: Smart meters have enabled collecting detailed energy usage data, which can facilitate more accurate forecasting at both the individual building and household levels. In this paper, we introduce a hybrid energy consumption forecasting framework that combines the strengths of Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) neural networks with Prophet. By leveraging LSTM's ability to capture temporal dependencies and Prophet's strength in modeling seasonality and trends, our framework aims to identify complex consumption patterns that traditional methods might miss. We further enhance the prediction accuracy of our model by incorporating weather data and lag features. Validation across different socioeconomic household profiles shows that the hybrid model outperforms standalone LSTM, Prophet, and SARIMA models in high income households associated with more stable consumption behavior, while its accuracy declines relative to LSTM in profiles where usage is more reactive, usually found in lower socioeconomic segments. Overall, The work aims to improve energy management strategies, enabling more targeted and effective interventions in households where socio-economic factors influence consumption patterns.

title: Significant Difference in the Level of Acceptability Among the Accreditation Task Force Toward the Web-Based System for Engineering Programs Accreditation of Bicol University, Albay, Philippines
authors: Fe de Leoz-Ataiza, Zendy D. Mañago
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: The study aims to come up with a web-based system for engineering program accreditation for the BU College of Engineering to help faculty members who encounter difficulties, particularly in accessing and retrieving documents.Web applications, as the name implies, are software programs hosted on remote servers and accessed via web browsers. In contrast, cloud-based applications offer enhanced functionalities and broader compatibility. A web-based application was chosen over a cloud-based one because the development cost is less. The format design of the system is based on the 10 areas of the survey instrument of the AACCUP. The documents gathered and collected were uploaded to the web-based system. These documents are made accessible to accreditors, the chairman, and faculty members, guided by a user’s manual and approval of accounts by the system administrator’s user guide. The WordPress (an open-source content management system) and BlueHost (a web hosting domain) were used to develop the system. Furthermore, the system was designed using the Agile software development method, using programming languages, specifically, HTML, CSS, Javascript, and PHP. There are two major types of users of the system: 1) public users composed of the accreditors, chairman, and faculty members; and 2) administrators of the system. Access to the Web-Based System for Engineering Programs Accreditation requires a registered user account to be approved by the system administrator. The software is evaluated using the eight (8) criteria of ISO 25010 software quality evaluation. Using a Likert scale consisting of four points with 4 as the highest rating with adjectival rating of Strongly Agree (SA) and 1 as the lowest for Strongly Disagree (SD), results show that the evaluation of the respondents for functionality, reliability, usability, efficiency, maintainability, portability, security, and compatibility ranges from 3.80 to 3.92. The over-all result of the evaluation of the system, a weighted mean of 3.87 suggests that the overall performance is relatively high. In this case, the result indicates a favorable assessment across multiple dimensions, indicating a strong adherence to the ISO 25010 model’s standards and requirements.

title: An Immersive Virtual Tour Framework for Educational Institutions Using 360° VR and AI-Based Chatbot
authors: Mahmoud.R.Morgan, Alaa M. Nagy, Ahmed Radi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Traditional methods of showcasing educational institutions often fall short. Static photos and brochures lack immersion, hindering students' ability to envision themselves within the academy environment. Physical visits for basic information can be inconvenient and environmentally impactful. This project addressed these challenges by pioneering a virtual tour system integrated with a chatbot guide for the El Shrouk Academy website. Cutting-edge technologies were used to enhance accessibility by proposing a 360° VR tour which transcends geographical and physical barriers for website visitors. Students everywhere can virtually explore facilities, classrooms, and common areas, gaining a deeper understanding of the learning environment. Proposed VR tour, coupled with an audio guide, fosters more engaging experience compared to static visuals to allow prospective students to connect with the academy's atmosphere. Also, AI-based chatbot technology powers a virtual guide within the VR tour and throughout the website to readily answer users’ inquiries, potentially reducing the need for physical visits for basic information gathering. Dedicated faculty members profiles integrated within the VR environment further enhance user experience by allowing students to learn more about the esteemed faculty members. This project goes beyond showcasing an institution; it represents a pioneering vision for the future of education as it embraces sustainability and offers a convenient and immersive way for prospective students to explore educational offerings. This project serves as a model for other institutions seeking to revolutionize their student recruitment strategies and enhance the accessibility and engagement of their virtual presence.

title: Design and Evaluation of a Fairness-Aware Student Success Prediction System Using Learning Analytics Data (OULAD)
authors: Betty Nkem Okwedadi, Ekereuke Udoh, Edita Gashi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Student retention remains a persistent challenge in higher education, with non-completion rates exceeding 30% across OECD countries and even higher in developing regions. Machine learning (ML) and learning analytics (LA) offer early-warning capabilities by predicting at-risk learners based on demographic, behavioural, and assessment data. However, most systems optimise solely for accuracy, overlooking fairness, transparency, and interpretability. This research addresses that gap through the design and evaluation of a Fairness-Aware student success prediction system using the Open University Learning Analytics Dataset (OULAD). The system integrates predictive modelling, fairness auditing, and explainability into one unified framework. Four machine learning classifiers—Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, Random Forest, and XGBoost—were benchmarked, with XGBoost achieving the highest performance (AUC = 0.947). Predictions were expressed as calibrated probabilities, categorised into risk bands to guide proportionate human intervention. Fairness was quantified using Demographic Parity Difference (DPD) and Equal Opportunity Difference (EOD), while SHAP and LIME provided interpretability at both global and individual levels. Results show that post-mitigation fairness gaps (Demographic Parity and Equal Opportunity differences) were reduced to near-zero levels with less than 1% accuracy loss. The study extends fairness analysis beyond traditional demographics to contextual fairness, incorporating behavioural proxies—such as late-night study patterns and inactivity streaks—that reflect socio-economic and time-related pressures among international and working learners. This work contributes a reproducible framework and an interactive dashboard prototype that together demonstrate how predictive accuracy, fairness, and transparency can coexist to support equitable, responsible, and globally inclusive learning analytics.

title: A Multivariate ARIMA-Driven Analytical Framework for Structural Inflation Forecasting in the United States: Integrating Macro-Financial Indicators to Enhance Predictive Stability
authors: Venkat Vishwanath, Vishnupriya P, S. Aiswarya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: One of the pillars of economic stability is inflation which has a direct impact on interest rates, investment strategy, and the purchasing power of the consumers. Due to the need to stabilize the economy by having accurate forecasts, this paper aims at developing a strong multifactor time series model within the ARIMA framework. We will not use the traditional univariate techniques, but we will incorporate twelve exogenous macroeconomic variables such as the rate of unemployment, GDP growth, and producer price indices to represent the multidimensional structural linkages that cause inflation. Data is also preprocessed thoroughly and testing is done so that it was stable according to the Augmented Dickey Fuller (ADF) test and ideal parameters are obtained after analyzing the Autocorrelation Function (ACF) and Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF). The prediction accuracy of this model is very high, where the value of R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> is 0.998 and MAE and RMSE are low. This study aims at further developing simple univariate time series models and incorporating numerous economic drivers. Our results indicate that certain measures including the Home Price Index and the Producer price index of Construction Material are the most suitable measures of capturing short term market changes.

title: Study on Furnace-based ALD SIN Formation Process and Film Quality Improvement
authors: Luo Yafang, SunYan, Lin Kai, Liu Xiaomeng
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Silicon nitride (SIN) film deposited by furnace-based atomic layer deposition (ALD) wildly used in DRAM manufacturing and advanced logic fabrication. The increasing demands for enhanced film properties and excellent film-forming control capability, especially imposed by the progressively shrinking feature sizes in the logic device, request more solution need to be preparing for next generation processing tools. This paper introduces an optimized tool configuration that enables concurrent improvement of film uniformity and suppression of chlorine impurities. Experimental results demonstrate that the film deposited maintains the advanced performance characteristics, while achieving improved uniformity up to 28%-38% and in-film impurities reduced 29%-51% compared to original system. These comprehensive optimizations will effectively support the fabrication of advanced logic nodes, substantially reduce yield degradation associated with ALD SIN film uniformity, systematically eliminate factors contributing to electrical parameter variability, and thereby enhance the yield of chip fabrication.

title: Formally Schedulable ACC and AEB on ARM Cortex-M3: Rate Monotonic Analysis and Adaptive Variance-Weighted Dual-Sensor Fusion
authors: Aldrin G, Basil Raymund A, Manoj G, Abith Itty Jacob, Berbiyo Roy E, Allen Benshiel J
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: Embedded driver assistance applications require provable temporal correctness, where a deadline miss in a safety-critical control path cannot be empirically verified until system failure. Low-cost ACC and AEB prototypes have measured latencies but lack mathematical guarantees of meeting real-time deadlines. Moreover, they use minimum-selection fusion, where degradation occurs silently when one channel is transiently noisy. This paper presents a low-cost embedded prototype of adaptive cruise control (ACC) and emergency braking using an STM32F103C8T6 microcontroller running FreeRTOS, integrating ultrasonic and infrared sensors with an inverse-variance weighted fusion strategy. Schedulability is analyzed under fixed-priority assumptions using measured execution times, yielding a total utilization of 25.3% compared to the Liu–Layland bound of 74.4%. Evaluation across five obstacle scenarios shows a 38.5% reduction in distance RMSE over minimum-selection fusion (p < 0.01, n = 30). A direct ADC guard path achieves a full stop from 5cm in 900ms. Both the schedulability proof and fusion algorithm are portable to any rate-monotonic RTOS.

title: Advancing ATE Efficiency: Integration of EXA Scale and Smartest 8 for APBB Applications
authors: Felix Chen
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: The growing complexity of APBB devices demands automated test solutions that offer higher multisite parallelism, deeper vector handling capability, and enhanced power-timing coordination. To address these challenges, we present a unified workflow built on EXA Scale hardware and SmarTest8 software, supported by a structured set of reusable test components. This combination achieves substantial improvements in test-time performance, engineering efficiency, and production stability. While the latest platform features—such as deep vector memory, optimized execution models, and improved diagnostic tools—provide the foundation, practical efficiency also relies on a consistent development framework that streamlines reusable logic across diverse test modules. Our results demonstrate more than 20 percent overall test-time reduction, smoother correlation, and faster migration from development to mass-production readiness.

title: Architecting Multi-Model Agentic AI Systems: A Taxonomy and Reference Architecture for Specialized LLMs in Production Agents
authors: Sandeep Shivam, Milan Parikh, Venkat Nutalapati, Vinay Soni, Vijaykumar Venganti
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Modern AI agents deployed in enterprise and cloud environments are rarely powered by a single monolithic large language model. Production systems require heterogeneous compositions of specialized models, each optimized for distinct computational roles: reasoning, perception, planning, memory, and action. This paper makes three primary contributions. First, we introduce the Multi-Model Agentic Systems Taxonomy (MMAS-T), formally characterizing six model classes General-Purpose LLMs, Mixture-of-Experts (MoE), Vision-Language Models (VLMs), Large Reasoning Models (LRMs), Small Language Models (SLMs), and Large Action Models (LAMs) along four dimensions: functional role, computational profile, modality, and deployment context. This taxonomy addresses a gap in prior work, which has treated individual model classes in isolation without a unified compositional framework for multi-agent orchestration. Second, we propose a four-layer Reference Architecture (MMAS-RA) decomposing agentic systems into Perception, Cognition, Memory, and Action layers, with explicit model-to-layer assignments and four reusable design patterns. Third, we present a comparative empirical evaluation of three agent configurations on a realistic enterprise workflow-automation scenario. Results show that heterogeneous multi-model architectures achieve 38.8–41.8% end-to-end latency reduction and 32.0–41.0% operational cost reduction versus single-model baselines, with task success rates improving from 91.2% to 96.8%. Statistical significance is confirmed via paired t-tests (p < 0.01) on 200 task executions.

title: Identifying Information Technology Research Trends through Text Mining of NSF Awards
authors: Said Varlioglu, Hazem Said, Murat Ozer, Nelly Elsayed
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Information Technology (IT) is recognized as an independent and unique research field. However, there has been ambiguity and difficulty in identifying and differentiating IT research from other close variations. Given this context, this paper aimed to explore the roots of the Information Technology (IT) research domain by performing a large-scale text mining analysis of 50,780 abstracts from NSF CISE grants awarded from 1985 to 2024. We categorized the awards based on their program content, labeling human-centric programs as IT research programs and infrastructure-centric programs as other research programs based on the IT definitions in the literature. This novel approach helped us identify the core concepts of IT research and compare the similarities and differences between IT research and other research areas. The results showed that IT differentiates itself from other close variations by focusing more on the needs of users, organizations, and societies.

title: Research on Control and Optimization Methods for Polymer Residue in Aluminum Etching Process
authors: Chao Ding, Litian Xu, Aishu Sang, Kejun Zhang, Sen Li
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: In the backend Al (Aluminum) interconnection process of semiconductor manufacturing, dry etching of aluminum inevitably produces polymer by-products. These non-volatile polymer residues are a critical issue leading to post-etch wafer surface contamination, critical dimension variation, and ultimately product yield loss. In advanced manufacturing processes, the requirements for removing by-products such as horn-type polymer are even more stringent. To address this challenge, this study explores the following strategies for controlling and optimizing the behavior of etching polymer. Firstly, optimize the process parameters during the etching process, including the BCl<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>/Cl<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> gas ratio, power, process pressure and ESC temperature, to suppress the formation of excessive polymers. Secondly, optimize the Photoresist (PR) thickness before Al etching, including increasing the Descum step to make the PR thin down and reduce the generation of C-based polymer, thereby further increasing the removal rate of Al-based polymer by-products. Finally, various Post Etch Treatment (PET) were also explored for the removal of Polymer, including Ar PET step, Flush Step, and WET condition. The experimental results show that through the above-mentioned process optimization, the residual density of the polymer after aluminum etching has been significantly reduced by more than 90%, while maintaining excellent etching profile morphology, high selectivity ratio and uniform etching rate. This has important practical significance for improving the manufacturing yield of advantaged chips.

title: Parallelism Strategies and Concurrency Effects for Mixture-of-Experts Inference on GPU Systems
authors: Ananya Hegde, Akshata Kumble, Ravi Gupta
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures reduce inference cost by activating only a sparse subset of parameters per token. However, when these models exceed single-GPU memory, practitioners must distribute them across devices using tensor parallelism (TP), expert parallelism (EP), or a hybrid of both, and there is little empirical guidance on which strategy to choose. We present a controlled comparison of these strategies for MoE inference serving on NVIDIA A100 GPUs using vLLM. Across multiple models and workload settings, we find that EP achieves 85–102% of TP throughput depending on expert count, matching or exceeding TP for models with 60–64 experts, while naive TP+EP hybrid placement underperforms both alternatives. We also show that a diffusion-based MoE language model is substantially slower than its dense counterpart, highlighting the need for multi-GPU expert distribution in this emerging class. Overall, our results suggest that request concurrency has a larger impact on throughput than parallelism choice in many cases, and that EP is a strong default for large-expert MoE models.

title: A Study of Model Poisoning Attacks on Federated Large Language Models
authors: Amir Faiyaz, Rukayat Olapojoye, Gaurav Karwa, Tara Salman
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: The adoption of large language models (LLMs) in privacy-sensitive applications has raised many privacy concerns resulting from data sharing. This has led to a growing interest in federated learning LLM (FedLLM), where private fine-tuning models are used without requiring the sharing of private data. However, the multi-agent nature of FedLLM introduces new attack surfaces that remain underexplored in LLM settings. This paper presents the first in-depth analysis of model poisoning attacks targeting FedLLM. We analyze three classic untargeted attacks —scaling, sign-flipping, and Gaussian attacks — under the FedLLM setting. We demonstrate their potentially catastrophic effects, including perplexity spikes exceeding 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</sup> times and complete degradation of BLEU scores. Our results, conducted with 40% malicious clients, reveal that FedLLM and its underlayering adaptation layer can amplify specific attack vectors while marginally mitigating others. These findings highlight critical vulnerabilities in FedLLM and underscore the need for a secure, communication-efficient FedLLM.

title: Machine Learning-Based Analysis of Global Fisheries Production, Consumption, and Sustainability Trends
authors: Jennie B. Fernandez
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: This study presents a machine learning framework for analyzing global fisheries production, consumption, and sustainability trends using key indicators such as aquaculture production, capture fisheries output, seafood consumption, and fish stock sustainability. A dataset consisting of approximately 12 variables from publicly available sources, including Kaggle and global fisheries records, was utilized. Multiple machine learning models, including Linear Regression, Random Forest, Support Vector Machines (SVM), XGBoost, and Neural Networks (DNN), were applied for regression and classification tasks. Results show that XGBoost and Random Forest achieved the highest classification performance, with accuracies of 0.90 and 0.87, respectively, while Linear Regression demonstrated strong predictive capability for continuous outcomes (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.85). Feature importance analysis identified aquaculture production, capture fisheries output, and seafood consumption as key drivers of fisheries performance. Countries were classified into High, Medium, and Low Risk categories based on sustainability thresholds. Although the findings highlight the effectiveness of machine learning in modeling fisheries systems, limitations related to dataset generalizability and lack of external validation were identified. The study emphasizes the potential of data-driven approaches in supporting sustainable fisheries management and policy decision-making.

title: A Comprehensive Survey on Explainable Graph-Based Models for Disease–Gene Association Prediction
authors: Chalumuru Suresh, Saketh Karumudi, Srikruthi Muthyam, Vishwanth Talluri, Sri Vaishnavi Yadavalli
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: The prediction of gene–disease associations (GDAs) is essential for elucidating disease mechanisms and advancing precision medicine. Owing to the high cost and limited scalability of experimental validation, computational approaches, particularly network-based learning models, have gained significant attention. This survey examines recent advancements in GDA prediction, focusing on graph neural networks (GNNs), heterogeneous graph modeling, hypergraph learning, and ranking-based optimization techniques developed over the past five years. We analyzed methods that integrate protein–protein interaction (PPI) networks, gene functional similarity, disease semantic similarity, and biomedical knowledge graphs to capture complex biological relationships. Special emphasis was placed on strategies addressing data sparsity and class imbalance, including contrastive learning, margin-based ranking losses, and hard negative mining. Building on these developments, we propose a weighted hypergraph neural network framework for GDA prediction. Our approach constructs a weighted PPI graph to preserve high-confidence biological interactions and employs hypergraph message passing to model higher-order gene–disease relationships. To enhance the robustness and ranking consistency, we incorporated progressive hard negative mining, margin-based ranking loss with score regularization, and dynamic threshold optimization. Experimental evaluations demonstrated improved ranking performance and generalization across multiple diseases. This work provides a comprehensive overview of contemporary GDA methodologies and contributes a scalable, ranking-oriented framework for disease gene discovery.

title: Education Methodologies(Pedagogy) and Child Development Programs for Individuals with Down Syndrome
authors: Sushil Kumar, Selvakumar K, Mamata Das
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Down syndrome is one of the most common genetic conditions affecting cognitive development and learning patterns. Effective education methodologies and early child development programs can significantly enhance adaptive skills, communication, and social integration for individuals with Down syndrome. This paper presents a pedagogical framework tailored to the learning styles and developmental needs of children with Down syndrome. The proposed approach emphasizes multi-sensory learning, structured teaching strategies, language enhancement, motor skill training, and inclusive education. The framework integrates clinical understanding, psychological support, and individualized educational planning to create holistic learning environments that foster independence and social participation.

title: Lightweight Vision-Based Docking for UAVs on Resource-Constrained Edge Devices
authors: Krishnapriya A, Keerthana Sri V, Sahaya Anselin Nisha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: Autonomous charging docking for UAV platforms requires centimeter-level positional precision under strict computational constraints. This paper presents a lightweight hybrid navigation architecture optimized for resource-constrained edge devices such as the Raspberry Pi Zero 2W. The system integrates regression-based ArUco localization, hardware-aware fiducial acceleration, pointer-based terminal alignment, wheel encoder odometry, and Federated Kalman Filter fusion. Experimental validation demonstrates docking errors below 6 cm and sub-degree angular deviation while sustaining 60 Hz visual processing under low-power operation. The proposed architecture enables practical autonomous docking without high-end LiDAR or GPU hardware.

title: Rapid Detection of SARS-CoV-2 Variants via SERS and Machine Learning
authors: Battamir Adilbish, Ankhbayar Nyamdavaa, Kiran Kaladharan, Gordon Cichon, Fan-Gang Tseng, Tseren-Onolt Ishdorj
publish: 2025 International Symposium on Computer Science and Educational Technology (ISCSET)
abstract: This study develops a machine learning approach to detect SARS-CoV-2 variants (Beta, Gamma, Omicron) using surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy with a barcode-based multiplex assay. A dataset of 54 SERS spectra, comprising 28 Positive and 26 Negative samples, was analyzed, targeting characteristic peak intensities at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$380 ~\text{cm}^{-1}$</tex> (Omicron), <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$540 ~\text{cm}^{-1}$</tex> (Beta), and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1336 ~\text{cm}^{-1}$</tex> (Gamma). Raw spectra were preprocessed with baseline correction, normalization, and smoothing to minimize noise. Features, including peak intensities, their ratios, and total spectral intensity, were selected to distinguish Positive and Negative samples. Three machine learning models SVM, RF, and LR were trained on 80 % of the data (43 samples) and tested on 20 % (11 samples), using five-fold cross-validation to prevent overfitting. SVM achieved the highest accuracy (90.00 %) and perfect precision (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 0 0 0 0}$</tex>), followed by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R F}(\mathbf{8 1. 8 2 \%})$</tex>, while LR recorded the lowest accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(72.73 \%)$</tex>, limited by the small dataset and complex, non-linear SERS spectra. The framework supports extension to other pathogens, such as Influenza A/B, using additional reporters. The limited dataset size necessitates further data collection and validation. Future work will explore deep learning models, like convolutional neural networks, and employ Gauss-Lorentz methods for data augmentation to enhance dataset size and model robustness for automated, point-of-care diagnostics.

title: Effect of Modular Vs Standard LFSR in Novel Homogenous Reversible Logic Based LFSR-APUF
authors: Nishant Bansal, Bhaskar Dutta, Gaganpreet Kaur
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Hardware security has assumed importance as a trusted security measure in Industry 4.0 Internet of Things (IoT). Hardware security relies on the use of one way Physically unclonable Function (PUFs) of which strong PUFs are preferred for authentication due to their scalability and complexity. Arbiter PUFs (APUF) are a class of strong PUFs popular owing to their low overheads and ease of integration with circuit design. LFSR-APUFs have in particular established themselves as light-weight, modelling resistant security solutions. In this work the analysis on choice of LFSR used in LFSR-APUF had been done extensively. The LFSR-APUF design under study is a novel homogenous, reversible logic based efficient design where LFSR is used for challenge generation. The focus of the study is to understand the effect of modular LFSR vs Standard LFSR on the performance metrics of the APUF. The analysis has been done for 16-bit response generation. The results for analysis have been evaluated in terms of Inter Hamming distance and Uniqueness of the proposed LFSR-APUF

title: Study of Film Discoloration and Moisture Influence in Plasam-Enhanced Sub-Atomsphericic Deposition for CSTIC 2026
authors: Hui Li, Miao He, Lei Wang, Yeliang Wang, Guangquan Lv
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: This study investigates the discoloration phenomenon in plasma-enhanced sub-atmospheric fill (PE-SAF) films, which manifests as visible defects accompanied by reduced film thickness (as measured by focused ion beam scanning electron microscopy, FIB-SEM), significant stoichiometric deviation (characterized via scanning electron microscopy with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, SEM-EDS), and elevated surface charge (detected by digital surface photovoltage, DSPV). A comprehensive approach combining multiphase simulation and controlled experimentations was employed to elucidate the underlying mechanism. The results indicate that increasing the equipment front-end module (EFEM) pressure differential relative to the external environment from 2.5 - 3 Pa to 7.5 - 8 Pa markedly reduces the incidence of film discoloration, with complete suppression achieved in small-batch tests. This optimization ultimately contributed to a 4% improvement in wafer yield during high-volume production, demonstrating significant practical implications for enhancing quality control in semiconductor manufacturing.

title: Enhancing Stormwater Sampling Methods with UAV Water Collection Module
authors: Amalie J. Keefe, William R. Borges, Wren Sobolewski, Colby T. Robbins, Tyberious E. Brooks, Blake Hament, William T Pluer
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: Traditional water collection methods can be laborious, time-consuming and sometimes dangerous. They require researchers to physically access and manually sample sites during or shortly after hazardous weather conditions due to the temporal nature of stormwater runoff. This also requires delay between sampling and analysis which can compromise time-sensitive metrics. Drone-based water collection provides a fast, efficient alternative capable of operating in adverse weather, offsetting potential cost increases. In the Piedmont Region of North Carolina, storm water runoff is a major source of pollutants, like nitrogen and phosphorus, and collecting samples during storms would expand available data. This research develops and tests a water-collection module for an unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) implemented on a SwellPro Fisherman FD3 drone.The design is divided into five subsystems: sampling mechanism, water collection, sensor integration, communications array, and propeller guard. Water is collected using a small diaphragm pump and a modified sampling bottle that enables system flushing in accordance with water collection standards. In the sampling and collection system, pump tubing is spooled when not in use to prevent tangling and sway, using a 15 kg-cm servo and custom 3D-printed mechanisms. The intake tube is housed in a sampling cage containing dissolved oxygen, temperature, and pH sensors, enabling simultaneous sample collection and in situ measurement. The system provides real-time readouts and records data for later laboratory analysis. In the communication system, controls and telemetry are managed by two ESP-32-S3 microcontrollers communicating via LoRa HF modules. This modular architecture supports expandability, transferability to other UAVs, and independent subsystem development. Isolation from the primary drone controller enables granular power management, improved error handling, and reduced integration constraints.Subsystems were evaluated through flushing efficacy, three-point load bearing stress tests, cable strength, sensor accuracy benchmarked against laboratory instruments, communications range and delay, and an efficiency comparison between UAV-based and traditional hand sampling measuring collections within a one-hour window. Full system testing was conducted at the Haw River, NC. This work aims to improve stormwater monitoring and support pollutant identification and prevention efforts in North Carolina’s Piedmont Region.

title: Module-Based Single-Frequency FBAW Filter Design for the mmWave Band (24.25–27.5 GHz)
authors: Xian Ji, Ruoxi Zhu, Yong Huang, Sui Xiao, Jian Mao
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: This work presents a module-based single-frequency Film Bulk Acoustic Wave (FBAW) filter for the 5G mmWave n258 band (24.25–27.5 GHz). AlN air-gap FBARs with five-sided apodization are adopted to enhance lateral acoustic confinement, while a double-motional Modified Butterworth Van Dyke (MBVD) model provides accurate circuit-level prediction. A hybrid LC–acoustic strategy effectively shifts the intrinsic resonant frequencies (~30–31 GHz) downward to the target band while preserving high Q and low loss. A 7-resonator module achieves 3.25 GHz 3-dB bandwidth, < 0.4 dB insertion loss, and > 23 dB rejection. Cascading two modules improves isolation to > 51 dB with < 1.1 dB total IL, demonstrating a scalable solution applicable to FR3/6G bands.

title: Robust Active Noise Control Using Nonlinear Error Compression: A Logarithmic-Hyperbolic Approach
authors: K. Anil Kumar, M.V.R. Vittal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: The traditional active noise control (ANC) systems are quite vulnerable to non-Gaussian noise, especially impulsive noise, which negatively impacts their ability to cancel unwanted noises. This study presents an innovative approach to cancel both Gaussian and non-Gaussian impulse noise, emphasizing robustness against impulsive noise. Many algorithms from the hyperbolic family have demonstrated success in mitigating impulsive noise recently. An innovative adaptive approach for impulsive noise in active noise control systems is suggested in this study, which makes use of the logarithmic hyperbolic active tan function (LHATF). In reality, the cost function of LHATF exhibits smoothness for small faults and resilience for large ones. Therefore, we can ensure the steady-state error and stability of LHATF as long as the filtering settings meet specific requirements. The simulations confirmed the theoretical conclusions that are based on the mean square error (MSE). Furthermore, a variable step-size LHATF (VSS-LHATF) is recommended to further enhance LHATF's filtering accuracy. The proposed LHCAF and VSS-LHCAF exhibit improved filtering accuracy and stability compared to conventional robust adaptive filters.

title: Analysis of Chip Failure Caused by Misoperation in HTOL Test
authors: Haitao Liu, Dan Yang, Shuanshe Chao, Niuyi Sun, Na Mei, Keqing Ouyang
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: The HTOL test is a critical reliability test that is prone to issues. Some failures are not directly caused by aging but rather by human error or an incomplete test plan. It's essential to minimize these factors, as they can significantly distort the test results and require substantial time and resources to resolve. This article provides a comprehensive analysis of failures caused by human misoperation during HTOL testing, offering valuable insights and lessons for those encountering similar problems.

title: Enhancing Public Administration Security through Blockchain and Intelligent Routing
authors: P. Anitha Rajakumari, Naveen B, Hariharan P K
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: High cases of mismanagement and fraud are normally caused by inefficiencies and absence of transparency in the traditional agricultural subsidy distribution systems. This paper aims to address the challenges by introducing a Blockchain-Integrated Farmer Subsidy Management Framework enhanced with the integration of Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) and Graph Deviation Network ( GDN)-based anomaly detection. The suggested algorithm utilizes the blockchain technology to provide immutability and transparency in subsidy dealings and the WSN to constantly check the environmental factors like moisture, temperature, and humidity of the soil to deliver real-time data of the farm. Statistical deviation and Z-score analysis are used in the GDN module to identify abnormal sensor behaviors that are possible signs of fraud or anomalies in the system. The implementation uses FastAPI as the backend service, SQLite, and the user interface is done using Gradio. Experimental analysis proves the effective API response time (less than 100 ms), quick blockchain validation (less than 200 ms to verify 100 blocks), and effective anomaly detecting skills. The proposed framework will enhance transparency and reliability in the management of agricultural subsidies by means of integrating blockchain technology with wireless sensor networks and the method of detecting anomalies. The system offers a systematic method of tracking the transactions of the subsidies and detecting the possible anomalies of sensor data.

title: Enhanced Thermal Face Recognition through Advanced Feature Extraction Methods: A Biometric Approach Using CNN
authors: M. Chengathir Selvi, Sarumathi R, Indhuja M, Bharathi R
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Thermal face recognition is a biometric system used for secure and reliable person identification. Existing, visible-light face recognition systems fail under low light environments, occlusions and temperature variations, whereas thermal imaging captures stable facial heat patterns that enable robust identification in nighttime and occluded conditions such as masks, glasses and facial alterations. This paper proposes an enhanced thermal face recognition framework that integrates a fine-tuned ResNet-50 for deep feature extraction, Neighborhood Multi-Granulation Particle Swarm Optimization (NM-PSO) for feature selection, and a Support Vector Machine (SVM) for classification. The goal of this research is to demonstrate how to enhance performance in the area of thermal face recognition by integrating a modified ResNet-50 network for deep feature extraction; NMPSO for feature selection; and support vector machine (SVM) for classification. The ResNet-50 has been modified to learn to recognize temperature-invariant and identity-discriminative features in the thermal domain. In addition, NMPSO optimizes the high dimensional embedding of deep features extracted from the ResNet-50 by removing redundant or irrelevant features. Results from experimental evaluation of the Charlotte dataset indicate that thermal face recognition using the previously described hybrid RMPSO-SVM architecture provides superior recognition accuracy, improved robustness to occlusion and thermal fluctuations, and better overall computational efficiency. Thus, this research shows that the hybrid ResNet – NMPSO-SVM has great potential for real-time applications in surveillance and secure biometric authentication.

title: From TF-IDF to Transformers: A Comparative and Ensemble Approach to Sentiment Classification
authors: Dip Biswas Shanto, Mitali Yadav, Prajwal Panth, Suresh Chandra Satapathy
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Sentiment analysis, also referred to as opinion mining, primarily tries to extract opinion from any text-based data. In the context of movie reviews and critics, sentimental analysis can be a helpful tool to predict whether a movie review is generally positive or negative. It can be difficult for the ML models to understand the context or metaphysical sentiment accurately, as ML models rely largely on statistical word representations. The objective of this paper is to examine and categorize movie reviews into positive and negative sentiments. Diverse machine learning models are considered in doing so, and Natural Language Processing (NLP) methodologies are employed for data preprocessing and model assessment. IMDb dataset is used. Specifically, Naïve Bayes, Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machines (SVM), LightGBM, LSTM, and transformer-based models such as RoBERTa and DistilBERT were evaluated. After a lot of testing with accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC, RoBERTa performed better than all the other models, with an accuracy of 93.02%. A soft voting ensemble that combined all the models also improved classification performance, showing that model ensembling works well for sentiment analysis.

title: An Explainable Federated Learning–Based Intrusion Detection Framework for Zero-Day Cybersecurity Threats in SDN
authors: Mohamed Sithik M, Nikesh S, Mohammed Ziyahid S.A, Ramamoorthi S, Priya S, Mohammed Uveise S A
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Software-Defined Networking (SDN) enables centralized control and programmability of modern networks; however, this architectural flexibility introduces significant security challenges, particularly in detecting zero-day cyberattacks. Traditional intrusion detection systems (IDSs) often suffer from class imbalance, limited generalization to unseen attacks, high false alarm rates, and lack of interpretability. This paper proposes a federated learning-based intrusion detection framework for SDN environments that addresses these limitations while preserving data privacy across distributed network domains. The proposed architecture enables multiple SDN controllers to collaboratively train a global deep learning-based IDS model without sharing raw traffic data. To enhance transparency and trust, explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) techniques are integrated to interpret detection decisions and identify critical traffic features contributing to attack classification. Zero-day detection capability is evaluated by excluding selected attack classes during training and assessing performance on unseen attacks. Experimental evaluation on the UNSW-NB15 and CICIDS2017 benchmark datasets demonstrates improved detection performance, enhanced minority-class recognition, and reduced false positive rates. The results indicate that the proposed federated and explainable IDS framework provides a scalable, privacy-preserving, and practical security solution for next-generation SDN deployments.

title: Vision-Based Integrated Detection of Traffic Signals and Lane Markings for Autonomous Vehicles
authors: Sangeetha K, Saravanan V, Sushil Kumar V, Tarun P V
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: In modern autonomous driving, reliable perception systems cannot be ignored because only through them, road environments can be properly interpreted and safe navigation is possible. This paper describes a vision-based and lightweight perception pipeline that can detect traffic lights and road signs in real time. The architecture is a combination of a YOLOv8n object-detection model to identify traffic lights and an Attention-U-NET segmentation network to identify lanes. Training select subsets of the LISA Traffic Light dataset, the TuSimple lane dataset and simulated driving scenarios in CARLA, thus guaranteeing ecological and computational efficiency. Experimental analysis shows strong accuracy of detection and high-quality real-time performance: the model based on the YOLOv8 can achieve a detection accuracy of more than 90 percent in detecting traffic-signals and a low inference latency of 4.9 ms (or 204 FPS). The relative comparisons with ResNet-18, and EfficientNet-B0, attest to the fact that the framework proposed here organization offers a reasonable trade-off between accuracy and computational power. The hybrid system is able to help in performing critical driving tasks, such as halting at red lights and maintaining lane positioning, in simulated conditions. These findings emphasize the appropriateness of the framework to the low-latency, edge-based autonomous-driving perception stacks, and identify future directions in pedestrian detection, resistance to adverse weather and multimodal perception fusion.

title: Optimized Gaussian Large Language Model (LLM) Reprogrammed for Temporal Predictions
authors: Stefano Frizzo Stefenon, João P. Matos-Carvalho, Laio Oriel Seman, Valderi R. Q. Leithardt, Kin-Choong Yow
publish: 2026 22nd International Conference on Intelligent Environments (IE)
abstract: This paper proposes a hybrid time series forecasting framework that combines Gaussian filtering for signal denoising with a Large Language Model (LLM) reprogrammed for temporal prediction (Time-LLM), optimized considering a structured random search across multiple agents. The Gaussian filter preprocesses raw data to suppress high-frequency noise while preserving structural trends, and the Time-LLM aligns continuous time series with discrete language tokens through cross-modal reprogramming. Evaluated on hourly turbine flow data from three Brazilian hydroelectric plants. For a horizon equal to 10 steps ahead, the proposed method achieves a root mean square error of 88.3, mean absolute error of 66.9, and mean absolute percentage error of 1.8 %, outperforming state-of-the-art benchmarks, including TiDE, NHITS, NBEATS, GRU, DeepAR, TFT, TCN, Informer, PatchTST, FEDformer, KAN, TimesNet, and standard Time-LLM.

title: A Cryptographic and Usability-Aware Framework for Securing Social Media Posts Using ECC Digital Signatures
authors: Heider A. M. Wahsheh, Mohammed S. Al-Zahrani
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: The unregulated proliferation of information on online platforms is deteriorating public trust, and the only recourse seems to be beyond mere reactive approaches using traditional AI systems such as detection methods that are biased and do not generalize well. A cryptographic and usability-friendly approach to embedding digital signatures directly in social media posts using Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) is presented. We created a lightweight proof-of-concept tool that can be used to allow users in Saudi Arabia to sign and verify content before sharing it on platforms such as X (formerly Twitter), WhatsApp, Telegram, etc., without requiring any infrastructure changes. The system is integrated with the Saudi National Root Certification Authority (Root-CA) to support verifiable authorship through trusted certificates. Two ECC schemes — ECDSA and EdDSA (Ed25519) are compared in terms of signing speed, verification time, signature size, and platform support. Fifty participants were engaged in a formal usability evaluation (following ISO 9241-11 guidelines), which evaluated efficiency, effectiveness, and satisfaction using the System Usability Scale (SUS). Results indicate that Ed25519 outperforms ECDSA in cryptographic efficiency and user experience, including faster execution, higher task success rates, and improved usability scores. This implementation provides a proof-of-concept for integrating legally binding, cryptographically sound, and user-friendly authentication with day-to-day social media operations, contributing to achieving Saudi Vision 2030 digital transformation objectives.

title: Integrating Artificial Intelligence into Healthcare: Predictive Analytics for Early and Equitable Diagnosis
authors: Raja Muzzamil Ali Chohan, Ekereuke Udoh, Muhammad Ibrahim, Edita Gashi, Nicholas Ioannides
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Chronic illnesses like cardiovascular disease and diabetes continue to cause worldwide morbidity and death, especially in low-resource and underrepresented groups where diagnosis are delayed. This study uses two well-established epidemiological datasets, the Framingham Heart Study for 10-year cardiovascular risk prediction and NHANES for diabetes risk classification based on HbA1c and glucose laboratory markers, to test the potential of machine learning (ML) models for early and equitable detection. Logistic regression, Random Forest, and XGBoost models were created and tested using ROC-AUC, confusion matrices, calibration curves, and threshold sensitivity analysis after preprocessing and class imbalance. Logistic regression consistently had the best interpretability-performance ratio, producing moderate discrimination (ROC-AUC 0.698 for CHD, 0.725 for diabetes). Class weighting and lower decision criteria increased early detection sensitivity. Calibration analysis showed partial alignment of probability, particularly in lower to mid-risk categories, suggesting practical clinical use after recalibration. Correlation and feature analysis confirmed that physiological risk markers (e.g., age, blood pressure, HbA1c) matched clinical knowledge, whereas demographic data highlighted structural drivers of health. Interpretable machine learning, responsible threshold optimisation, and fairness-aware evaluation can aid preventative health risk stratification, according to the study. The findings also highlight external validation, subgroup fairness evaluation, and calibration optimisation before clinical deployment. This study presents a practical, transparent, and reproducible approach for introducing machine learning-based risk prediction into public health screening processes to promote early detection and chronic illness equality.

title: VR-Based Virtual Laboratory for Dental Students: Concept and Evaluation
authors: Batshagai Baatar, Bayar-Erdene Lkhagvasuren, Uranchimeg Tudevdavga
publish: 2025 International Symposium on Computer Science and Educational Technology (ISCSET)
abstract: This paper describes the design, implementation, and empirical evaluation of a 3D virtual reality (VR) laboratory tailored for dental education and its evaluation based on students' perceptions. Practical lessons for dentist students are essential important to learn correct movement of fingers and hands with special dentist tools and equipment. In reality to have live practical lessons are highly limited. Therefore, immersive simulation of tooth extraction procedures using Meta Quest 3 headsets and a custom-designed interactive environment-based 3D laboratory comes in teaching and learning in the classroom. The developed laboratory as a pilot test used for teaching at Etugen University in winter semester 2024/2025. In total 51 students are enrolled in that course and applied the 3D laboratory in learning. With target to figure out acceptance and satisfaction of students relating to offered laboratory the corresponding survey was designed sent to students. The result of the survey shows that 78 % of respondents agreed that integrating 3D laboratories into education is appropriate, while 74 % rated it as efficient. Notably, 64 % confirmed that this improved understanding contributed to better learning outcomes in the course. This is the only beginning of complete VR laboratory for dentist students' practical lessons and authors improves the 3D environments based on the students' feedback.

title: Parkinson’s Disease Gait Events Detection Modeled as Flow Charts
authors: María Victoria Tabeira, Alejandra Rial, Rinaldo Almada, Franco Simini
publish: 2026 IEEE Medical Measurements &amp; Applications (MeMeA)
abstract: A narrative review of Parkinson’s Disease gait parameters as compared to healthy controls gives reference values to be used for real time gait analysis. The review yields a reduction in walking speed $0.17 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ for Parkinson’s Disease patients, a reduction in stride length of 16 cm, and an increase in cadence of 1.75 steps/s. In order to develop a methodology which ensures direct and errorless clinical specifications to a wearable gait assistive device, we adopt formal flow chart construction to specify gait analysis, from the clinician’s point of view, ready for software implementation. The events detection is formalized with a simple flow chart applicable to right and left, heel strike and toe off. The principal flow chart is an endless loop which includes all gait events. We have analyzed eight healthy volunteers at self-selected speed with a software derived from our flowchart model. Mean gait parameters found are: speed of 1.21 m/s, stride length 127.8 cm and cadence of 113 steps/s. Moreover, for Parkinson’s Disease individuals, there is a shorter swing time and an increased double support time. The real time availability of these parameters support the development of PARKIBIP, a wearable device for gait monitoring and rehabilitation in daily-life conditions.

title: Rolling Towards Resilience: the Impact of Scooter Riding on Pediatric Knee Health
authors: Armando Crottogini, Bianca Schenk, Yves Staudt
publish: 2026 IEEE Swiss Conference on Data Science and AI (SDS)
abstract: Kick scooter riding is a common activity among children, yet its potential implications for musculoskeletal health remain insufficiently understood. This study applies a Gradient Boosting Machine (GBM) framework combined with Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) methods to examine associations between scooter use, overall activity patterns, and knee pain in children aged 8-12 years. The model achieved high predictive performance (accuracy: <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 3. 3 \%}$</tex>, recall: <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 8. 5 \%}$</tex>). Explainability analyses indicated that age and overall physical activity patterns were the primary contributors to knee pain risk, whereas scooter use alone was not a dominant predictor. Thus, scooter riding does not appear to exert a protective effect; rather, under certain conditions, it may contribute to knee stress. These findings underscore the multifactorial nature of pediatric knee pain and highlight the value of data-driven, holistic prevention strategies.

title: Towards AI Agents Supported Research Problem Formulation
authors: Anrafel Fernandes Pereira, Maria Teresa Baldassarre, Daniel Mendez, Marcos Kalinowski
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 IEEE/ACM International Workshop on Methodological Issues with Empirical Studies in Software Engineering
abstract: None

title: Automatic Vehicle Accident Detection and Classification from Images Using YOLOv8
authors: Evita Bhowmik, Prakhar Bhardwaj, Asif Uddin Khan, Dayal Kumar Behera, Kumar Surjeet Chaudhury, Binayak Kar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Accurate and real-time detection of road accidents is critical for enabling rapid emergency response and enhancing urban traffic safety . In this work a computer vision framework is proposed for accident detection using state of the model YOLOv8. Unlike conventional systems reliant on sensor-based inputs this work uses DL to process video streams. It can detect vehicular objects in real time. This enables the identification of potential collision events with minimal latency. The system utilizes a modular pipeline built on OpenCV, PyTorch and YOLOv8 for efficient real-time inference. At this point, we are not including severity classification. But current design makes it easier to add alert system and anomaly detection. Annotated visual feedback is rendered using cvzone that facilitates both live monitoring and post-event analysis. Frame extraction utilities are also implemented to support dataset generation and model expansion. This framework builds the stage for creating modular safety systems using cameras and can be used in smart cities, and autonomous surveillance systems.

title: Securing APIs in Government Clouds and Runtime Fabric Using FIPS-Enabled MuleSoft
authors: Venkata Pavan Kumar Gummadi, Lakshmi Sujatha Chilamkurthi, Srikanth Kavuri
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: MuleSoft-based API-led connectivity architectures in government clouds and Runtime Fabric (RTF) environments handle highly sensitive data and mission-critical workflows, necessitating stringent FIPS 140-2/3 compliance and FedRAMP-aligned security. This paper presents a comprehensive security framework for FIPS-enabled MuleSoft APIs, integrating gateway-level controls with service level objectives (SLOs). The approach combines authentication, authorization, FIPS-compliant transport security, threat protection, data privacy, input validation, and centralized policy governance with SLO-centric observability, enabling government agencies to monitor security posture via formal service level indicators (SLIs) alongside reliability metrics in high-assurance environments. Unlike traditional approaches that treat compliance as an isolated concern, this work treats security as a first-class SLO dimension with explicit SLIs, error budgets, and operational feedback loops across FIPS-enabled MuleSoft control planes.

title: Renal Calculi Detection and Multiclass Classification Using MobileNetV2 Model
authors: Kottaimalai Ramaraj, Anitta D, R Indumathi, G Vinodha, S Sakkaravarthi, S J Kavitha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: Efficient medical evaluation and therapy decisions depend on the rapid and precise recognition of kidney disorders. This research suggests a deep learning (DL)-based autonomous categorization approach that uses MobileNetV2 and a transfer learning approach to overcome these issues. Around 12,446 CT scans collected from distinct clinical origins and verified by medical professionals, are divided into four classes: cyst, normal, stone, and tumor. The suggested approach gets away with the necessity for manually created feature engineering by using MobileNetV2's lightweight architecture to extract discriminative features straight from input images. The suggested algorithm outperforms conventional approaches, attaining an overall accuracy of 99.95%. The durability and dependability of the classification findings across all classes has been confirmed using the confusion matrix. The results demonstrate MobileNetV2's efficacy as a reliable and computationally viable automated renal illness categorization solution, presenting it as a potential decision-support solution for real-time healthcare applications.

title: DFT calculations of magnetocrystalline anisotropy energy with fixed spin moment
authors: Justyn Snarski-Adamski, Joanna Marciniak, Wojciech Marciniak, Justyna Rychły-Gruszecka, Mirosław Werwiński
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: The development of new-generation permanent magnets is based on experimental efforts and innovative theoretical tools for modeling magnetic properties. Magnetocrystalline anisotropy energy (MAE) – one of the main intrinsic properties of permanent magnets – can be calculated using density functional theory (DFT). However, MAEs determined with different exchange-correlation potentials can vary widely. We show how these seemingly contradictory results can be reconciled using the fully relativistic fixed spin moment (FR-FSM) method. This is because the equilibrium pairs [MAE, ms] calculated with different exchange-correlation potentials overlap with the MAE(ms) curve determined from the FR-FSM method (ms denotes the spin magnetic moment). The FR-FSM method also enables the hypothetical maximum MAE value for a given material to be estimated. In the case of magnetic alloys, MAE(FSM) analysis allows the optimal alloying additions to be determined in order to improve the MAE value. The high independence of the MAE(ms) works well for exchange-correlation functionals such as LDA and GGA. However, it is not a universal measure and varies with changes in the value of the U parameter when using LDA+U or GGA+U functionals. Concluding, the framework we describe for MAE versus FSM calculations can be a useful tool in the design of new permanent magnets.

title: Feature Map Compression for Split Learning in Mobile Wireless Environment: Theory and Practice_supp1-3696694.pdf
authors: Zhihao Qu
publish: N/A
abstract: Split Learning (SL) enables collaborative model training by distributing computation between resource constrained mobile edge devices and a central server. In this distributed framework, mobile edge devices compute the early layers of the model and transmit feature maps to the server. However, due to practical mobile wireless bandwidth limitations, these feature maps should be compressed before transmission. In this paper, we propose a lightweight Mask-Encoded Spar sification (MS) compressor that enhances Top-k sparsification with a narrow-bit mask, significantly reducing compression error while improving computational and communication efficiency. Our theoretical analyses reveal that feature map compression generally induces estimator bias in the gradients. Furthermore, we establish the relationships between compression error and convergence, as well as between the choice of cutlayer and output error. The first relationship demonstrates that reducing compression error facilitates convergence, confirming the advantage of MS, whereas the second provides theoretical guidance for determining the cutlayer, indicating that earlier layers are more sensitive to compression errors. Extensive experiments on vision and language models demonstrate that our method outperforms existing compression techniques in terms of compression error and model performance, achieving up to a 21.38× speedup in communication efficiency under wireless environments.

title: Experimental Demonstration of 225 GBaud WDM-PAM Transmission over Hollow Core Fiber for Next-generation AI Data Centers
authors: Haiyun Xin, Yao Zhang, Jin Tang, Qiang Guo, Haofan Yang, Maxim Kuschnerov, Huixiao Ma
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: Driven by AI applications, data rate of optical interconnect in data center networks (DCN) keeps increasing, and will soon evolve to 3.2 Tbps port bandwidth in the near future. System solutions for high baud rate intensity modulation and direct detection (IM-DD) signal transmission are urgently required. At the same time, hollow core fiber (HCF) has been actively studied in recent years due to its superiority in reduced latency, leading to a lot of breakthroughs. Thus, it is meaningful to investigate the feasibility and performance of applying HCF in DCN. In this article, we analyzed the performance improvements that HCF can provide. Thanks to reduced chromatic dispersion, and nonlinear effects, longer transmission distance and higher capacity can be achieved. In addition, bidirectional transmission can be supported due to suppressed Rayleigh backscattering. Then, we experimentally demonstrated an 8<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lambda$</tex-math></inline-formula> 225 GBaud IMDD-WDM transmission over a hollow core fiber. By using PAM4/6, a total net rate of 6.4/7.6 Tbps over 10 km transmission is achieved, respectively considering a BER threshold of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${4.85 \times 10^{-3}}$</tex-math></inline-formula>/<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${2.4 \times 10^{-2}}$</tex-math></inline-formula>.

title: A Parallelism-Driven, Area-Aware Technology Mapping Framework for Memristive Logic-in-Memory
authors: Ikkyum Kim, Minhong Kim, Heechun Park
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Data-centric applications continue to be limited by the memory wall. Logic-in-Memory (LiM) architectures based on non-volatile memories (NVMs), such as memristors, offer a promising solution by enabling in-memory computation and eliminating costly data movement. While several approaches leveraging memristor-aided logic (MAGIC) operations have been proposed, many fail to fully exploit the parallelism and spatial efficiency of memristor crossbars, especially under physical design constraints. In this paper, we propose a parallelism-driven, area-aware look-up table (LUT)-based mapping framework of arbitrary logic circuits into a memristor crossbar using MAGIC operations. We present a crossbar-aware floorplanning strategy that leverages topological LUT information to enable two-directional parallel computation (i.e., HNOR and VNOR), thereby maximizing computational LUTs with minimal execution cycles. We formulate an integer linear programming (ILP) approach to define the placement of each LUT and its fanin locations, improving routability while preserving parallelism. Moreover, we introduce our novel A∗-search-based fan-in routing method with Hanan grid that fully utilizes the aligned fanins and intermediate results. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves maximum parallelism and crossbar utilization, thereby reducing cycle counts by 19.6% and ensuring that all benchmarks map successfully under strict area constraints. We also confirm the scalability and practical applicability of the proposed framework for large-scale logic mapping in memristor-based LiM systems.

title: An LLM-Enhanced Anomaly Detection Method for Identifying RFI in Multi-temporal SAR Data
authors: Jiayi Zhao, Yaxing Yue, Xuepan Zhang, Zhihao Wen, Qihang Xu, Zhiguo Shi, Guisheng Liao
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: With the increasing complexity of the electromagnetic environment, synthetic aperture radar (SAR) sensors are frequently affected by radio frequency interference (RFI), which manifests as de-focused, block-like anomaly artifacts in SAR images. Traditional intelligent detection methods rely on extensive annotation, which is impractical due to the massive volume of data and the extreme scarcity of RFI-contaminated samples. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a large language model (LLM) enhanced unsupervised RFI detection framework, leveraging the unprecedented capabilities of LLM in multi-modal reasoning. First, to incorporate semantic guidance, we introduce a text encoder module based on bidirectional long short-term memory. This module encodes LLM-generated image quality assessment reports into high-dimensional feature vectors, providing semantic constraints for anomaly detection. Second, a fusion metric is introduced to synthesize the dual-polarization quick-look image into a single-channel image, efficiently reducing input data volume while preserving interference features. Third, a temporal filter is applied to the multi-temporal synthesized image sequence to construct clean background images as a training set. Finally, a multimodal anomaly detection network, named RFI-MADNet, is proposed. It integrates the textual embeddings into a nested autoencoder-generative adversarial network architecture with self-attention and refined loss for intelligent RFI detection and localization. The proposed method effectively achieves robust detection on class-imbalanced real-world SAR data without requiring manual labeling. Experimental results show that the proposed method achieves superior RFI detection and competitive localization performance across various regions compared to current methods, with improvements of at least 14% in the average area under the curve metric and over 1% in the mean intersection over union metric.

title: Model-Free Optimal Incremental Control for Attitude Tracking of Hypersonic Morphing Vehicles With Actuator Saturation
authors: YUTENG CHEN, JING CHANG, WEISHENG CHEN, XIAOPING LI, WEIMIN BAO
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: High-performance attitude control of hypersonic morphing vehicles (HMVs) is challenged by time-varying, morphing-induced dynamics and strict actuator saturation constraints. This paper proposes a model-free incremental adaptive dynamic programming (ADP) framework to address these challenges in a unified manner. The framework integrates three components: (1) a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) recurrent neural network used in a hybrid offline–online manner, where offline pretraining provides a high-quality initialization and online adaptation reconstructs the system dynamics in real time and provides gradient information without requiring an explicit physics-based model; (2) an incremental ADP controller combined with a Dynamic Boundary Saturation Algorithm (DBSA), which incorporates actuator limits into a nonquadratic cost function to compute optimal incremental control inputs while reducing the need for persistent excitation; and (3) a Prescribed-Time Disturbance Observer (PDO), which guarantees convergence of the disturbance estimation error within a user-specified time and enhances robustness against rapidly varying uncertainties. Closed-loop stability and boundedness are established via Lyapunov analysis. Numerical simulations on a nonlinear HMV model demonstrate improved convergence, reduced overshoot, smoother actuator behavior near saturation, and robust tracking under model uncertainty, while maintaining moderate online computational cost.

title: Camouflaged Target Detection Based on Cross Modal Frequency-Domain Feature Enhancement and Text-Driven Fusion Network
authors: Jie Lai, Jie Geng, Ruihui Peng, Wen Jiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Camouflaged target detection (CTD) is a challenging task that involves identifying targets with high visual similarity to their surroundings. Existing methods primarily focus on spatial-domain features, while cross-modal fusion often lacks guidance from high-level semantics, leading to features that are not sufficiently discriminative and are prone to noise interference. To address these issues, we proposes an innovative cross modal frequency-domain feature enhancement and text-driven fusion network (CMFT). First, high-level semantic information is extracted from descriptive text by a frozen CLIP text encoder to provide precise semantic guidance for subsequent fusion. Next, a frequency-domain collaborative enhancement (FDC) module is constructed to enhance high-frequency details in shallow layers and compensate for low-frequency global information in deep layers, thereby improving both the target's detailed texture and global structure in the feature space. Then, a text-driven coupled affine fusion (TCF) module is designed to dynamically generate scaling and translation parameters based on text embeddings, achieving adaptive alignment and fusion of visible and infrared features. Finally, a quality-aware intersection over union loss is introduced to provide sample quality-aware supervision for bounding-box regression, thereby strengthening the learning of reliable predictions and reducing interference from low-quality samples. Experimental results show that CMFT outperforms existing methods on both custom and public benchmark datasets.

title: Communication-Augmented Wide Neural Network Control for Accelerated SoC Equalization in Battery Energy Storage Systems
authors: S T Saranya, Pritam Bhowmik
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Fast and effective state of charge (SoC) equalization in battery energy storage systems (BESS) is important for the robustness, longevity, and stability of DC microgrids. Conventional decentralized and heuristic-based control methods often exhibit slow convergence and limited adaptability to dynamic load conditions and battery aging. To address these drawbacks, this paper proposes a wide neural network (WNN)-based communication control architecture that integrates local and global system information to accelerate SoC equalization. Unlike deep learning methods, the proposed approach maps voltage, current, SoC, and multiple battery states to the optimal reference power and converter duty cycle using a two-stage regression process. Input features are selected using MRMR and ReliefF techniques to obtain a computationally efficient and highly reliable control algorithm suitable for real-time applications. Under dynamic conditions, the WNN-based system with global information reduces SoC equalization time by 56.8%, with duty cycle prediction RMSE of 4.63 × 10⁻⁴, thus offering energy management for microgrids.

title: Unified First-Order Power Synchronization Control of Grid-Forming Converters
authors: Shan Jiang, Ye Zhu, Georgios Konstantinou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: Power synchronization control (PSC) plays a critical role in shaping the frequency response of grid-forming (GFM) converters. However, existing PSC implementations are developed with diverse control structures, which hinders a unified interpretation of their dynamic characteristics and associated design trade-offs. This paper demonstrates that common PSC methods, i.e., constant power, droop, and virtual synchronous generator (VSG) control, can be unified by a first-order transfer function (termed the complex droop factor) with four coefficients and up to three degrees of freedom. This unified representation reveals how PSC inherently governs both primary frequency response (PFR) and virtual inertial response (VIR). In particular, constant power control provides only VIR, droop control provides only PFR, and VSG control provides both. However, VSG control is prone to synchronization instability as it is essentially a proportional–derivative controller. To overcome these limitations, a fractional VSG control is proposed to bridge the gap between droop and VSG control. By independently regulating three degrees of freedom, the proposed method enables flexible shaping of droop, inertia and damping characteristics, allowing simultaneous consideration of power overshoot, frequency deviation, and response speed during disturbances. Furthermore, a three-step tuning guideline is developed to facilitate practical parameter design while maintaining consistency with classical VSG control principles, followed by an adaptive control law that yields adaptive virtual inertia. The proposed method is validated through experimental studies under various network transients and operating conditions with different system strengths.

title: Unleash Your Potential
