authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Annals of the History of Computing

title: Design and Evaluation of a Remote Laboratory for Electrical Engineering Education: Vector Network Analyzer and High-Frequency Circuit Experimentation
authors: DavId P. Reid, Maksim V. Kuznetcov, Alexander T. Don, Iram Shahzadi, Symon K. Podilchak, Timothy D. Drysdale
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: We present a vector network analyser (VNA) remote laboratory for electrical engineering education, designed to provide first-time users of VNAs with self-regulated access to scattering parameter measurements of high-frequency circuits. The browser-based interface provides student users with appropriate levels of control over measurement parameters and data, whilst mitigating the frustration and boredom that may be associated with more conventional, passive-type demonstrations by academics of complicated, research grade VNAs (which are typically of high-cost). We evaluated the learning experience of third-year engineering students at the University of Edinburgh, using two User Experience Questionnaire (UEQ) attributes (dependability and stimulation), as well as student self-reported affective state during learning. Students reported positive perceptions of control over the laboratory (mean UEQ dependability = +1.04) and motivation to interact with it (mean UEQ stimulation score = +1.10). The most frequently reported affective state was engagement (78% of respondents) and the least reported was boredom (14%). We also show that students who felt positive affective states during the learning activity had significantly higher levels of perceived control over the remote laboratory. This supports the literature on control-value theory and demonstrates the need for remote laboratory interactions to be carefully designed to meet student needs and learning objectives. Finally, using a content analysis of student feedback on their affective state during the laboratory activity, we show that the remote laboratory interface provided an appropriate level of control for first-time VNA users without reducing the desirable levels of productive challenge necessary for deep learning.

title: Career Center
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Annals of the History of Computing

title: SPECTRA-AVQA-Net: Sparse Perceptual Enhancement With Cross-Modal Transformation for Audio–Visual Question Answering
authors: Debashis Das, Suman Kumar Maji
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Audio–visual question answering (AVQA) is an emerging topic in AI that requires joint reasoning over audio, visual, and textual modalities. However, existing AVQA approaches often suffer from two key limitations: inadequate spatiotemporal alignment between audio and visual signals and imbalanced semantic contributions across modalities, which hinder effective multimodal reasoning and contextual understanding. To address these challenges, we propose SPECTRA-AVQA-Net, a unified architecture designed to enhance multimodal feature extraction, alignment, and fusion for robust AVQA. Our framework introduces sparse range-aware multi-head attention (SRA-MHA) to selectively attend to informative modality cues while efficiently modeling multirange dependencies. In addition, a multi-scale feed-forward network (MS-FFN) captures both local and global modality-specific patterns through depthwise separable convolutions and pointwise projections. We further develop a multi-stage cross-modality attention transformer (MS-CMAT) that progressively aligns semantic representations between audio and visual streams through hierarchical, question-guided cross-modal interactions. Extensive experiments on the MUSIC-AVQA benchmark demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework. SPECTRA-AVQA-Net achieves 78.73% and 77.09% accuracy on the real and synthetic subsets, respectively, establishing a new state-of-the-art and highlighting its strong capability for complex audio–visual reasoning.

title: HQT-TI: an Efficient Hilbert Curve Based Index for Spatial Keyword Queries
authors: Lianyin Jia, Yongwang Miao, Suprio Ray, Jiaman Ding, Xiaodong Fu, Xiuxing Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: This paper introduces HQT-TI, a novel indexing method designed to improve the efficiency of spatial keyword queries. HQT-TI consists of two main components: a Hilbert QuadTree (HQT) based spatial index and a Trie-Inverted index (TI) combined textual index. HQT integrates the Hilbert curve with a Quadtree, establishing a direct relationship between the two. TI combines a trie and inverted index to minimize the intersection cost associated with long lists, thus improving the speed of keyword queries. The HQT based Spatial Query algorithm (HQT-SQ) reduces overlap checks and limits irrelevant object retrieval by employing query drill-down and depth first search with limited breadth expansion in spatial queries. Meanwhile, the Segment List Intersection based Keyword Query algorithm (SLI-KQ), built on TI, efficiently handles segment list intersections for keyword queries. The combination of HQT-SQ and SLI-KQ results in HS-SK, a highly efficient spatial keyword query algorithm. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that HS-SKQ outperforms SFC-Quad by up to two orders of magnitude, achieving up to a 5.46× speedup over the best existing competitors, making it a promising solution for large-scale spatial keyword query processing.

title: PhysFusion: A Transformer-based Dual-Stream Radar and Vision Fusion Framework for Open Water Surface Object Detection
authors: Yuting Wan, Liguo Sun, Jiuwu Hao, Zao Zhang, Pin Lv
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Detecting water-surface targets for Unmanned Surface Vehicles (USVs) is challenging due to non-stationary wave clutter, specular reflections, and frequent long-range observations where small or low-profile objects exhibit weak appearance cues. Although 4D millimeter-wave radar can complement cameras under degraded illumination, maritime radar point clouds are typically sparse and intermittent, and reflectivity-related attributes often present heavy-tailed variations under scattering and multipath, making it difficult for conventional fusion designs to exploit radar cues effectively and maintain stable predictions across frames. We propose PhysFusion, a physics-informed radar–image detection framework tailored for water-surface perception. Instead of relying on direct fusion of sparse radar returns and dense visual features, PhysFusion improves multimodal detection from three complementary aspects: radar representation learning under cluttered and heavy-tailed radar observations, query-level radarguided cross-modal interaction for more effective fusion with multi-scale visual features, and lightweight temporal aggregation for stable frame-to-frame prediction. This design enables more reliable radar cue utilization and more consistent multimodal detection in challenging water-surface environments. Experiments on WaterScenes and FLOW demonstrate that PhysFusion achieves 61.5% mAP<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">50:95</sub> and 90.3% mAP<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">50</sub> on WaterScenes (with T=5 radar history) using 5.6M parameters and 12.5G FLOPs, and reaches 94.8% mAP<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">50</sub> and 46.2% mAP<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">50:95</sub> on FLOW under the radar–camera setting. Ablation studies further verify the effectiveness of the proposed design under a consistent evaluation protocol.

title: VizDefender: Unmasking Visualization Tampering through Proactive Localization and Intent Inference
authors: Sicheng Song, Yanjie Zhang, Zixin Chen, Huamin Qu, Changbo Wang, Chenhui Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: The integrity of data visualizations is increasingly threatened by image editing techniques that enable subtle yet deceptive tampering. Through a formative study, we define this challenge and categorize tampering techniques into two primary types: data manipulation and visual encoding manipulation. To address this, we present VizDefender, a framework for tampering detection and analysis. The framework integrates two core components: 1) a semi-fragile watermark module that protects the visualization by embedding a location map to images, which allows for the precise localization of tampered regions while preserving visual quality, and 2) an intent analysis module that leverages Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to interpret manipulation, inferring the attacker's intent and misleading effects. Extensive evaluations and user studies demonstrate the effectiveness of our methods.

title: P3RS-MT: Privacy-Preserving Personalized Recommendation Services in Medical Tourism
authors: Yan Xu, Yao Wang, Jie Cui, Hong Zhong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: To obtain the most suitable recommendation service for medical tourism, patients typically submit medical records to service providers, who perform similarity computations to recommend customized medical tourism options. To preserve data privacy, patients should upload encrypted medical records, enabling service providers to perform similarity computations on ciphertext. However, medical records are typically high-dimensional and sparse, making encrypted similarity computation less efficient and accurate. In this paper, we propose a privacy-preserving personalized medical tourism recommendation service named P3RS-MT. The proposed scheme employs weighted Tanimoto coefficient (WTC) to enable efficient and accurate similarity computations, while adopts somewhat homomorphic encryption (SHE) to safeguard medical records from exposure to service providers. Since SHE does not natively support division operations-required for WTC-we address this limitation using Newton's method for iterative approximation. Theoretical analysis and experimental evaluations show that P3RS-MT is secure, accurate and efficient.

title: Global–Local Interaction and Recalibration Network for Salient Object Detection in Optical Remote Sensing Images
authors: Xiaofei Zhou, Chenwei Zhu, Liuxin Bao, Deyang Liu, Bolun Zheng, Runmin Cong, Jiyong Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics
abstract: Optical remote sensing images (RSIs) exhibit extensive spatial coverage and complex geographic backgrounds, where salient objects in the optical RSIs present a variety of scales, shapes, and orientations. Over the past few years, many deep learning-based approaches, including convolutional neural network (CNN)-based and Transformer-based models, have been devoted to salient object detection (SOD) in optical RSIs. However, those models do not fully explore the complementarity and differences between CNN and Transformer features, resulting in a degradation in performance. Therefore, in this article, we propose a global–local interaction and recalibration network (i.e., GLIR-Net), which leverages the complementarity of global context from Transformer and local details from CNN while harmonizing their inherent differences. Specifically, in the encoder, we use the two-branch architecture comprising a Transformer branch and a CNN branch to extract the global contextual and local detailed features, respectively. Second, we employ the multiscale feature interaction (MFI) module to promote the interaction of the CNN and Transformer features, where the two features are mutually enhanced by their complementarity. Third, the two kinds of features are aggregated via the feature recalibration module (FRM), where the CNN and Transformer features are fused and recalibrated to harmonize their inherent differences. Finally, by deploying the decoder, we can progressively acquire the high-quality saliency map for each optical RSI. We conduct extensive comparative experiments with state-of-the-art models on two public datasets, and the experimental results firmly demonstrate that the proposed GLIR-Net outperforms all the other competitors both qualitatively and quantitatively.

title: A Compact and Modular Fiber Optic Sensor with Low Cross‑Axis Coupling for Three‑Dimensional Dynamic Acceleration Measurement
authors: Xueyin Tan, Chuanyi Tao, Rui He, Yungui Xia, Zhuqiang Zhong
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Metrologically accurate three‑dimensional acceleration measurement in complex environments remains a critical challenge in aerospace and civil engineering. In this work, a novel complete modular three‑dimensional acceleration measurement system based on fiber Bragg grating (FBG) sensors with cross‑shaped diaphragms is proposed, fabricated, and experimentally validated. The system integrates three core units: a miniaturized FBG sensing unit (40 × 40 × 40 mm³) built with three cross‑shaped diaphragms on a stainless‑steel base for independent tri‑axial measurement, a high‑speed optical signal demodulation unit, and a real‑time data acquisition and analysis unit. These cross‑shaped diaphragms combine the characteristics of diaphragm–beam structures, balancing high sensitivity with excellent resistance to external interference. The proposed system not only overcomes the inherent limitations of conventional electrical acceleration measurement systems such as electromagnetic interference and poor durability in harsh environments, but also effectively mitigates the key problem of high inter‑axis coupling in integrated multi‑axis measurement systems while realizing the miniaturization of modular configurations. Comprehensive experimental tests are performed to assess the overall operational performance of the proposed system, and the results show that the compact measurement system has resonant frequencies of 402.68 Hz, 412.45 Hz, and 403.75 Hz along the X‑, Y‑, and Z‑axes, respectively, and achieves high sensitivities of 58.25 pm/g, 58.57 pm/g, and 59.91 pm/g at 300 Hz for each axis. Moreover, the inter‑axis coupling of the system remains below 5% with lateral interference below 4%, which makes it particularly suitable for high‑fidelity three‑dimensional vibration and acceleration monitoring in aerospace structures and precision mechanical systems.

title: Cryo-CMOS Integrator-Based DAC for Scalable Biasing of Semiconductor Qubits
authors: Luc Enthoven, Job van Staveren, Jiang Gong, Masoud Babaie, Fabio Sebastiano
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: Semiconductor-based cryogenic quantum processors require the accurate biasing of a large number of gate electrodes, which are typically individually wired to room-temperature DACs. To prevent the wiring bottleneck when scaling to future very large processors, this work proposes a scalable cryo-CMOS DAC that can operate with a S&H demultiplexer close to the quantum processor. By adopting an integrator-based switched-capacitor DAC with a dynamically-biased high-voltage output stage, both the required large output range and high resolution can be achieved while sharing the DAC over a large number of electrodes, thus improving the power efficiency. Fabricated in a 22-nm FinFET technology, the DAC occupies 0.076 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. At 4.2 K (RT), it achieves an LSB of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$57.1~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>V (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$68.3~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>V) over a 3 V range with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$188~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">rms</sub> (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$192~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">rms</sub>) noise and 36.5-LSB (12.6-LSB) INL while dissipating <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$157~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>W (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$138~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>W). The low power dissipation and the potential to drive more than 30,000 electrodes paves the way for scalable biasing of quantum processors operating down to mK temperatures.

title: Advancing Crop Health: An Integrated Deep Learning Approach For Plant Disease Detection
authors: Murthuja P, Radha Krishna Ghodke, Mohan Reddy Pasurla, Prasanthi Kaluri
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Plant diseases present a significant threat to agricultural activities and food security particularly where experts cannot be easily put in place to detect the diseased plants. The proposed paper introduces an entire plant disease diagnosis system based on the use of deep learning to perform classification, lesion segmentation, severity Estimaton, and automated treatment prescription, which is useful on large commercial farms. The pipeline starts with a strong preprocessing step such as image normalization, image augmentation and noise removal to increase the performance in real world conditions. The main classifier is an EfficientNet-B4 model that has been fine-tuned on the PlantVillage dataset with 54,304 images of 38 plant classes and it has an 94 percent accuracy. Image processing methods detect infected regions and create lesion masks after the process of classification. The severity of the disease is computed as a percent of the lesion pixels of the total leaf pixels. The rule-based recommendation engine correlates each disease and level of severity with suitable biological, chemical and preventive interventions. The last interface shows the disease name, percentage severity and advice on the cure in a concise and easy to understand form that will allow agriculture decision support to be scaled and achieved in a practical manner.

title: A Dynamo-Based Framework for Streamlining MEP Design Workflows in Autodesk Revit
authors: Sadia Akter, Mst Mithila Afroz Hridy, Preonty Paul, Erin Purvis, Bryan Kuhr
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: Autodesk Revit is heavily relied upon by modern mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) design firms for system modeling and coordination; however, many essential workflows remain repetitive and manually intensive. Significant engineering time is consumed, and opportunities for human error are introduced by these tasks, particularly within smaller firms that lack access to proprietary or paid automation tools. As project complexity and sustainability requirements continue to grow, a strong need is created for efficient, consistent, and accessible automation solutions within standard Revit workflows. This work presents the development of automated MEP design workflows using Dynamo within Autodesk Revit, with a focus on space placement from linked architectural models and the structured investigation and documentation of sanitary plumbing riser diagram automation for future development. The methodology is centered on the identification of repetitive manual processes and their translation into structured Dynamo scripts that extract, transform, and apply data across linked Revit models. For space placement, architectural rooms are identified, relevant parameters are extracted, the data is reformatted to match MEP requirements, and spaces are automatically assigned with consistent parameter mapping. For plumbing riser diagrams, applicable standards, calculation logic, and workflow structure are documented using IAPMO-based data to outline how pipe sizing and flow calculations could be automated in future work. Both workflows are evaluated using sample and client-provided Revit models and are validated through comparison of automated results against manually completed designs where applicable. Testing is focused on accuracy, consistency, Revit version compatibility, and overall time savings. The results demonstrate that targeted Dynamo automation significantly reduces repetitive modeling effort, improves design consistency, and supports reliable, real-world MEP engineering workflows. User documentation and training materials are also included to ensure long-term usability and maintainability of automation tools.

title: Detection and Mitigation of DDoS Attacks in SDN Using DNN Approach
authors: Samantha S, Akshaya Shree C, Ishwarya R, Gowthami N, Tamilselvi S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack is one of the most significant security threats to SDN-based networks. In a DDoS attack, a large number of compromised devices, often part of a botnet, forward an overwhelming amount of traffic simultaneously to a targeted network or a service. The purpose is to exhaust resources and make the target unavailable to legitimate users, disrupting normal operations and causing service downtime. In context of Software Defined Networking, the network is prone to serious vulnerabilities because of its highly centralized architecture. Attackers can exploit this vulnerability by generating high volumes of traffic by flooding the SDN controller with an excessive number of TCP/UDP/ICMP requests and eventually slowing down the network’s response. An effective DNN-based DDoS detection and mitigation framework is needed to overcome this problem. Therefore, by utilizing Autoencoder-based Deep Learning approach and Harris Hawk Optimization (HHO), the proposed model is proven to perform well in dynamic network traffic scenarios and achieved an accuracy of 96.86% demonstrating its effectiveness in maintaining network reliability and availability.

title: A Secure Image Sharing System Using Hybrid Chaos-Based Encryption
authors: Molika Y, Puli Venkata Sai Yashwanth, P Vishnu Vardhan, Ravi Bhargav, Priyanka Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Due to the nature of digital communications, there is a necessity for transmitting images through public networks in a secured manner. The proposed technique involves the use of a chaotic image encryption algorithm by dividing an image into smaller fragments then performing encryption on each fragment by means of Tent-Sine chaotic system. Encryption is achieved through permutation, substitution and XOR diffusion algorithms whereas SHA-256 algorithm will be employed in generating seeds for encryption.The proposed algorithm also involves the application of microservices in a sender-receiver authorizing process using the technologies Node.js, MongoDB and Python Flask. From the experimental analysis carried out, the following results were found regarding security performance. NPCR of more than 99.5%, acceptable UACI values, entropy of more than 7.999 and encryption time below 0.5 seconds for 256×256 images. The above indicates high security with regard to encryption and decryption processes.

title: Recommender Systems for Reducing Software Fault Rates in Automotive Software Development
authors: Dimitrios Datsogiannis, Wolfram Hardt
publish: 2025 International Symposium on Computer Science and Educational Technology (ISCSET)
abstract: This paper presents the research results of using a recommendation system (RS) to evaluate automotive development processes from the initial design phases to the final software release. The model uses a dynamic questionnaire managed by a recommender system, which aims to detect deficiencies and faults during the development while increasing transparency among stakeholders. The question pool with 137 questions, consists of weighted questions, derived from key development metrics and serves as the basis for this evaluation. Questions are selected during each development cycle with Contextual Multi-Armed Bandit (CMAB) algorithms, allowing for minimal stakeholder effort while maximizing feedback relevance. The current study explores the system's performance across four behavioral change scenarios that simulate different rates of software fault occurrence. Three reinforcement learning algorithms, eGreedy, LinUCB, and Thompson Sampling, were applied and compared. The tuning of the algorithms is also part of the performance assessment. The results are assessed based on the defined performance indicators. The findings indicate that the system is not only scalable but also achieves improved performance over time as it continues to operate and adapt to changing development conditions.

title: Refusing Fairness: A Temporal Audit of Dialect Bias in Modern Language Models
authors: Cynthia C. Nosiri, Hashmath Fathima, Kofi Nyarko
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: This study extends Groenwold et al.’s evaluation of dialectal bias in language models by comparing outputs from African American Vernacular English (AAVE) and Standard American English (SAE) prompts across five modern LLMs: Claude 3.5 Sonnet, GPT-4, Gemini 1.5 Pro, Qwen2, and LLaMA 3. We benchmark these models on semantically equivalent dialect pairs to assess sentiment consistency, lexical similarity, and output quality. Using BLEU, ROUGE, and BERTScore metrics, we evaluate textual fidelity, while a two-phase sentiment analysis pipeline reveals polarity drift across dialects.Our results confirm persistent dialectal bias: AAVE prompts consistently generate more negative sentiment across all five models. We introduce refusal behavior analysis to evaluate how safety-aligned models handle dialectal inputs. Claude 3.5 exhibited disproportionately high refusal rates on AAVE prompts (16.87% vs. 11.91% for SAE), with 52.3% of AAVE refusals categorized as safety/toxicity concerns. These findings reveal that alignment and safety mechanisms may inadvertently amplify linguistic bias, underscoring the importance of dialect-aware benchmarking for building fair and inclusive language technologies.

title: AI-Based Front-End Optical Identification for 2D Emerging Chips
authors: Shengxian Ke, Ho Hon Lawrence So, Xiaonan Deng, Yunfei Liu, Tongge Zhang, Yihan Tang, Jie Feng, Chen Wang
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: We present an AI-based front-end optical identification pipeline for 2D emerging chips using superpixel patches from routine experiments and characterization. A dual-head ResNet-18 predicts one primary label and multiple attributes. We compare direct supervised training with self-supervised pretraining followed by finetuning. Primary classification exceeds 0.99 accuracy across models, while attribute recognition benefits more from pretraining. DINO achieves the best overall attribute performance, improving micro-F1 from 0.830 (control) to 0.856. The results show that self-supervised pretraining improves fine-grained recognition while reducing dependence on large-scale manual labeling.

title: AI–ML–Based IP Framework For Accurate Brain Tumor Detection in IoT-Enabled Medical Imaging
authors: Shwetha G.K., Sarika Ghanshyam Jadhav, Suruthi S., A. Shaji George, Athira Manoj, T.C. Manjunath
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: Malignant brain cancer is ultimate outcome of uncontrolled proliferation of brain cells, which in turn causes a host of other neurological disorders. A background survey is given w.r.t. significant obstacles to early diagnosis that can be overcome with use of Computer-Aided-Diagnosis system. One of these obstacles is the proper identification of various diseases in MRI images. To improve accuracy of (MRI) scans we use the method of malignancy concepts. Finally, image containing brain tumor is located in the quadrant with highest MSF. Present research compares the suggested architecture to existing models that are discussed in order to demonstrate its merit. MRI brain tumor detection accuracy results of an impressive 97% with a precision of 95%, sensitivity of 98%, a specificity of 96%, a F1-score with 97%. Their experiment & simulation results conclude that developed architecture is robust, leading to an improvement in detection accuracy of brain tumors in various locations.

title: Best Implementation Strategy of PS5000 in Smart TV Chip Testing
authors: Chelsea Zhou
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Smart TV chip testing requires robust solutions for complex functions such as eARC, MIPI, and analog performance verification. PS5000 plays a critical role in enabling digital high-speed tests, AC parameter checks, and high-resolution analog measurements. Traditional methods face limitations in stability and efficiency. To address these challenges, this work introduces an optimized implementation strategy leveraging PS5000’s advanced features, including CDR & FA for data integrity, enhanced TMU for precise timing analysis, and C-DAC for linearity testing. These improvements significantly enhance test accuracy, reduce time cost, and achieve up to 15% yield improvement in real-world applications.

title: Noise and Sequence Length in Time-Series Forecasting: a Unified Evaluation of Transformers, DLinear, LSTM, and GRU
authors: Salma Albelali, Moataz Ahmed
publish: 2026 22nd International Conference on Intelligent Environments (IE)
abstract: Intelligent environments in critical domains require forecasts that remain reliable under noisy sensing and changing operating regimes. This work presents a two-factor evaluation that varies the input window length and training noise level across multiple forecast horizons, four model families (iTransformer, LSTM, GRU, and DLinear), and 13 datasets spanning lowand high-frequency signals. A unified preprocessing pipeline with multi-seed reporting quantifies accuracy (relative MSE and relative MAE) and stability. Three main findings emerge: (i) Forecast horizon dominates, as shorter horizons consistently reduce relative error; (ii) Long noisy windows degrade most, with error and uncertainty increasing more than additively when long input windows are combined with non-zero noise; and (iii) Architecture matters under noise, as attention-based and gated models mitigate degradation, whereas a single linear head is most vulnerable. The study provides a reproducible benchmark across timescales and practical guidance: favor short horizons, use long input windows only under clean sensing conditions, and stresstest forecasting pipelines with realistic noise.

title: Federated Cloud Intelligence: A Privacy-Preserving, Trustworthy and Sustainable Framework for Multi-Cloud AI
authors: Abuzar Khan, Ahmad Junaid, Abid Iqbal, Ghassan Husnain
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: This study proposes a Federated Cloud Intelligence for Privacy-Preserving AI, with new layered framework that can support secure and eco friendly learning across different cloud providers. Instead of centralizing data, our method trains models locally on varied client datasets and combines their updates using federated learning (FL) to stay compliant with data protection rules. The experiment have shown that the federated setup reached an average accuracy of 0.844 over five communication rounds, just slightly lower than the centralized baseline of 0.850. Meanwhile, the loss decreased from 0.367 to 0.285, coming close to the centralized value of 0.318. To build trust, a blockchain-based layer that permanently stored updates with little extra cost, adding blocks each round with an average consensus delay of 0.189 seconds. Tests showed that this consensus process reduced the impact of malicious client attacks, keeping accuracy stable around 0.827. Further it is then incorporated with zero-knowledge proofs (ZKM), where adds only 0.196 seconds of latency and 260–360 MB GPU memory overhead and showcases an accuracy up to 0.844. A reinforcement learning agent optimized workload scheduling by shifting the computation from AWS to GCP, reducing carbon scores by 20% with minimal accuracy trade-off. Finally, explainability analysis revealed balanced provider contributions from 0.021 to 0.023 and highlighted key features such as logPurchases and storePurchases.

title: Advanced 2nm Node Nanosheet FET Compact Model Based On Artificial Neural Network
authors: Biaoxing Gan, S.Cristoloveanu, Yong Xu, Jing Wan
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: In this paper, we have developed a novel, simplified SPICE model for Nanosheet FET that requires no physical formula to drive it, necessitating only the input of corresponding data. Results demonstrate that our ANN model, which employs specialized pre-processing and post-processing, accurately fits both DC and AC curves from TCAD simulation of 2nm node Nanosheet FET. This model can replace the traditional BSIM-CMG model while achieving high precision. The fitting error of off-state and on-state current is reduced by 40.3% and 7.8% respectively against TCAD results, compared to the BSIM-CMG model. The developed model is further implemented in Verilog-A and used for inverter and ring oscillator (RO) simulations in SPICE. Moreover, in an 11-stage RO simulation with V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">dd</inf> at 0.65V, static leakage current is 40.79% higher from simulation with BSIM-CMG model compared to that with ANN model, while frequency is 7.55% lower. This aligns closely with fitting error results in device models.

title: Enhancing Healthcare Systems Security Using Microservices and Zero Trust
authors: Makaire Njie, Viktoria M. Korzhuk
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: The goal of this study is to create a scalable and secure system for the healthcare system through the use of a combination of Microservice Architecture (MSA) and Zero Trust Security (ZTS). To do so, we have developed two innovative models: MediTrustNet - A Context-Aware Semantic/Syntactic Trust Assessment Model for preventing medical errors, and MicroShieldEHR - A ZTS-enabled micro-service-based Electronic Health Record (EHR) System. In addition, we simulate a health care environment in order to evaluate the performance of our integrated MediTrustNet and MicroShieldEHR system according to three evaluation criteria (Accuracy, Latency, Decision Quality) that each were constrained to meet the ZTS requirements. Our main contributions include: 1) A dual-layered trust assessment methodology that utilizes Word2Vec and BLEU to assess both the semantic and syntactic aspects of a user's access request in order to reduce medical errors and enhance the accuracy of the user's access; 2) A micro-service based EHR system utilizing Service Mesh, OAuth2, and dynamic policy engines to enforce ZTS at service-level boundaries; 3) A decision engine capable of generating real-time decisions that adhere to relevant legal frameworks (HIPAA, GDPR, AIDA). Our results show a maximum F1 score of 94.2% in determining high-risk requests with an average of only 190 ms of increased latency. The potential to greatly improve the protection of patient health data, the resilience of the system, and compliance with relevant regulations (such as HIPAA and GDPR) is present if our framework is applied to a production environment like that of a typical hospital network.

title: Comparison of frame-rate enhancement strategies for brachial pulse wave velocity estimation by ultrafast ultrasound
authors: Agata Barbagini, Simona Turco, Jens Muehlsteff, Massimo Mischi
publish: 2026 IEEE Medical Measurements &amp; Applications (MeMeA)
abstract: The assessment of pulse wave velocity (PWV) in the brachial artery could provide valuable insight into the mechanical behaviour underlying oscillometry, ultimately improving the accuracy of non-invasive blood pressure measurement. The wall displacement-based (WD) method is a widely adopted solution for PWV estimation in large arteries, but its application to smaller vessels such as the brachial artery remains challenging due to stringent temporal sampling requirements. In previous work, we showed that conventional ultra-fast ultrasound coherent compounding processing fails to provide reliable WD-based PWV estimates in the brachial artery, under typical acquisition settings. In this study, we investigate alternative processing strategies aimed at increasing the effective temporal sampling rate without increasing the transmission pulse repetition frequency. Three approaches are compared: conventional compound-first processing, per-angle motion-first estimation, and a time-aligned per-angle motionfirst strategy. Numerical simulations of the brachial artery are used to evaluate PWV estimation performance under different noise conditions and reference PWV values. The results show that time-aligned processing partially mitigates temporal undersampling effects for physiologically relevant PWV values, at the cost of increased sensitivity to noise. While all approaches remain affected by noise and temporal resolution limitations, the time-aligned strategy yields PWV distributions that extend toward higher values compared to conventional processing. These findings highlight the role of temporal sampling in WD-based PWV estimation and motivate further investigation toward noise-robust implementations.

title: Chemical Bonding at the Graphene Films/Cu Substrate Interface for High-Efficiency Heat Dissipation
authors: Chenfei Zhou, Xiuzhen Lu
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Escalating thermal management demands from increasing heat accumulation in high power density electronics under integration and miniaturization trends drive this chemically bonded graphene films/Cu substrate structure investigation. Through controlled functionalization, oxygen-containing functional groups were introduced on graphene films while polydopamine were engineered on Cu substrates, establishing chemical bonds at the interface. Thermal testing revealed superior performance of the engineered interface, while exhibiting amplified heat dissipation efficiency at elevated power densities. These results validate the effectiveness of chemical bonds in enhancing interfacial heat transport while highlighting the critical role of interface engineering in next-generation thermal management systems for high power density electronics.

title: Further performance testing of p-type transistor with dual-curvature cavity
authors: Ying Zhou, Guanqun Yu, Jianlei Zhao, Hongjian Mu, Yingna Han, Baohua Shi
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: This work demonstrates the performance benefits of a dual-curvature cavity design in p-type transistor, building on prior reported studies while incorporating comprehensive electrical characterization. Fabricated in a mature semiconductor wafer production line, the dual-curvature cavity enables larger SiGe Source/Drain volume. Compared to the baseline ball-shaped cavity, the new design shows no obvious change in Vt roll-off and slightly improved DC performance. Additionally, ring oscillator tests reveal AC performance gains, validating the cavity’s advantage for both static and dynamic operation. Furthermore, this kind of cavity architecture exhibits excellent reliability with no observable degradation in HCI and TDDB characteristics. These results significantly extend prior literature by systematically analyzing and providing quantitative comparisons with baseline data based on multiple electrical and reliability parameters, offering a promising solution for advanced MOSFET scaling.

title: Quantum-Inspired Hybrid Tamper Detection System for Secure Data Vaults in Offline Environments
authors: Prerna J, Puchha Bhavya, Ranjni U. A
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Conventional secure monitoring systems often rely on network connectivity and centralized servers. But this has been a very critical challenge in maintaining confidentiality and integrity in remote or offline regions where we can deploy secure data walls. This paper proposes the quantum-inspired hybrid tamper detection system that will integrate our software-simulated by PUF for cryptography, which generates a key and then a hardware-assisted vault monitor mechanism which will use a low-power sensor and third a secure encrypted tamper logging framework which is capable of detecting physical intrusion or any attempts without depending on the cloud. The system employs a quantum-inspired PUF model to derive unique and device-specific keys, improving resistance to cloning compared to traditional stored-key systems. Furthermore, a hybrid hardware architecture that integrates an infrared break-beam module, a photo-diode sensor, magnetic reed switches, and an ambient-light-resistant enclosure is designed to provide uninterrupted vault monitoring during low-illumination or nighttime conditions. All events are stored locally in an AES-GCM encrypted, hash-chained, append-only log that operates entirely offline. Experimental evaluation carried out through the implemented Python simulation demonstrates reliable key reconstruction and low-overhead event signing, while enabling reliably detection of log-tampering attempts under the tested conditions. The proposed system is low-cost, scalable, and well-suited for deployment in military, banking, and institutional environments where network connectivity is limited or restricted.

title: Reform of the Health Informatics Curriculum in Mongolia: Needs and Development Perspectives
authors: Purevdolgor Luvsantseren, Ajnai Luvsan-Ish, Javzmaa Tsend, Oyuntsetseg Sandag, Baasandorj Chilkhaasuren, Odgerel Boldbaatar, Akhit Tilubai, Nyamdavaa Uugandavaa, Darambazar Gantulga
publish: 2025 International Symposium on Computer Science and Educational Technology (ISCSET)
abstract: This To reform the Bachelor of Health Informatics curriculum in Mongolia, a comparative analysis was conducted with similar international programs, and the revised curriculum was developed based on satisfaction surveys from both graduates and employers. A satisfaction survey was conducted in which 49 graduates evaluated the curriculum using a 19-question questionnaire, and 14 employers assessed the graduates' competencies through an 8-question questionnaire, forming the basis for two distinct datasets. Factor analysis (PCA and EFA with Varimax rotation) was performed on the resulting dataset. The analysis identified five latent factors from the graduates' evaluations—Quality of Instruction, Practical Training and Skills, Learning Environment and Resources, Curriculum Structure, and Professional Development Opportunities–and one factor from the employers' assessments: Job Requirements and Skills Evaluation. Based on these findings, the revised curriculum was developed through a comparative analysis with programs from the University of Sydney, Oregon Tech, and Montana Tech. The updated curriculum consists of 120 credit hours and complies with Order A/147 of the Minister of Education and Science, as well as the IT Curriculum Guide 2017. A notable feature of the revised program is its emphasis on specialization in health data processing, information systems architecture, and computer networking. This research contributes to enhancing the quality of Health Informatics education and aligning the academic training with the needs of graduates and labor market demands.

title: Performance Evaluation of a ResNet50 CNN for Corn Leaf Disease Classification
authors: Rhowel M. Dellosa
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: This study evaluates the performance of a ResNet50-based Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for classifying corn leaf diseases using image data. The dataset consists of 4,188 corn leaf images grouped into four classes: Healthy, Blight, Common Rust, and Gray Leaf Spot. Images were preprocessed through resizing, normalization, and augmentation before model training. The model achieved an overall accuracy of 62% on the classification task. Class-level results showed the strongest performance for Common Rust, with precision of 0.64, recall of 0.98, and F1-score of 0.77. In contrast, Gray Leaf Spot had the weakest performance, with precision of 0.55, recall of 0.06, and F1-score of 0.11, indicating that the model struggled to identify this disease reliably. The confusion matrix further showed frequent misclassification of Gray Leaf Spot and moderate confusion between Healthy and Blight classes. These findings suggest that while ResNet50 shows potential for automated corn disease classification, additional improvements in dataset balance, feature learning, and model optimization are necessary before practical deployment.

title: A Study on Spatial Correlation Between Gas Pressure Difference and Film Thickness Based on an Unsupervised Learning Model
authors: Qiaoling Tian, Ziyang Liu, Guangyao Zhang, Kai Zhang
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: The uniformity of film thickness (THK) has a key impact on device performance in the plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition (PECVD) process. Traditional single-point analysis and other existing methods cannot effectively reveal whether there is a spatial correlation between pressure difference and film THK. They also fail to explain the complex spatial coupling mechanism. To address these problems, this paper proposes a deep convolutional autoencoder (CAE) method that integrates unsupervised learning ideas. The model takes the SH pressure distribution matrix inside the chamber as input. By using the advantage of convolutional neural networks in spatial feature extraction, it can automatically capture and quantify the spatial correlation between the gas pressure and film THK. Furthermore, our model can predict the film THK distribution from the pressure distribution. This provides a new theoretical basis and a data-driven approach for improving film THK uniformity.

title: Explainable 3D-CNN for Schizophrenia Detection using Multi-Site Structural MRI
authors: Sameen Raza, Amogh Gupta, Subhashree Mishra, Bhabani Shankar Prasad Mishra
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Schizophrenia is a chronic neuropsychiatric disorder characterized by structural brain abnormalities detectable using structural MRI (sMRI) [3], [5], [6]. This study proposes an explainable three-dimensional Convolutional Neural Network (3D-CNN) for automated schizophrenia classification using the multi-site COINSTAC MCIC VBM dataset [16], [31], comprising 3,729 subjects. The model learns volumetric representations directly from preprocessed 64 × 64 × 64 voxel inputs and the efficiency of the model is verified by calculating different parameters like accuracy, F1-score, Cohen’s Kappa and AUC. Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) [28] is integrated to localize discriminative brain regions and enhance clinical interpretability. Quantitative overlap analysis with anatomically defined regions of interest (ROIs) demonstrates substantial correspondence of activation within hippocampal and temporal regions, which are consistently implicated in schizophrenia [6], [7]. Training stability is validated through epoch-wise loss and accuracy curves, and computational feasibility is analyzed to support real-world clinical deployment. The proposed framework achieves competitive classification performance compared to recent 3D-CNN approaches [14], [27], [32], [33] while incorporating quantitative explainability on multi-site data. These findings highlight that volumetric deep learning combined with quantitative interpretability analysis provides a robust and clinically meaningful approach for schizophrenia classification.

title: HistDiff: Diffusion Models Empowered by Histogram Matching for Reducing Content Color Interference in Style Transfer
authors: Siying Liu, Zhenye Liu, Qiananna KongLang, Yingnan Ma, Anup Basu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Smart Multimedia (ICSM)
abstract: Previous methods for arbitrary style transfer often suffer from content-specified color interference, leading to undesirable color preservation or artifacts. In this paper, we propose a novel diffusion-based method, HistDiff, designed to enhance style expressiveness without compromising the integrity of the content structure. Specifically, we introduce a controllable histogram matching module that allows fine-grained management of color distribution. We utilize a pretrained diffusion model and incorporate an attention-based mechanism for style injection, which enables precise and localized style adaptation. Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance, significantly improving both content preservation and style fidelity across various benchmarks.

title: Towards Responsible Conversational AI for Mental Health Support: Balancing Empathy, Fairness, and Safety with Deep Learning
authors: Fatima Khurshid, Akhtar Jamil
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Empathetic conversational AI systems have a lot of promise for supporting mental health therapy, even if their practical use can be constrained by high processing needs and persistent problems with safety, fairness, and interpretability. This work presents a lightweight, computationally efficient architecture for empathic dialogue analysis and response generation that combines emotion classification, zero-shot personality approximation, semantic answer retrieval, explicit safety filtering, and fairness auditing into a single pipeline. Sentence-BERT embeddings are used as a common semantic representation, which eliminates the need for large-scale generative models and allows for accurate empathy categorization and interpretable personality inference. Similarity-based retrieval produces empathetic replies with minimal computing overhead and emotional alignment. The framework includes a dedicated safety layer to exclude risky, catastrophic, and clinically inadequate outputs as well as a template-driven fairness audit to evaluate demographic robustness in order to facilitate responsible deployment. Strong empathy classification performance, high emotion consistency in retrieved replies, regulated safety behavior, and consistent empathetic outputs despite demographic fluctuations under computational limitations are all demonstrated by experimental evaluations carried out on a large-scale empathetic discussion dataset. These findings show that conversational AI with empathy and responsibility may be developed without requiring enormous amounts of processing power, providing a useful and morally sound basis for scalable mental health support systems.

title: Bayesian Learning for Structure and Recipe Optimization with Limited Data and Mixed Variables
authors: Kai Zhang, Jiaxin Zhu, Guangyao Zhang
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Structural and recipe optimization is critical for achieving target process performance in semiconductor manufacturing, yet it is often constrained by limited experimental data and the presence of heterogeneous input variables in both continuous and discrete domains. This paper proposes a Bayesian learning framework based on Gaussian Process Regression for data-efficient modeling and optimization in mixed-variable spaces. The framework jointly accounts for continuous factors such as structural geometry, power, and gas flow, together with discrete settings such as recipe type and working conditions, enabling accurate mapping from equipment design and process parameters to key metrics including deposition rate and film uniformity. This probabilistic framework provides not only predictions but also uncertainty quantification under small-sample conditions. Case studies on semiconductor equipment design demonstrate that the approach enables efficient and effective co-optimization of structure and recipe, offering a practical path toward accelerating process development with limited data.

title: Enhancing Trust in Maternal Health Predictions Through Explainable AI with Quantitative Assessment
authors: Maria Muzaffar, Ihtesham Ul Islam, Muhammad Sohail, Maemoona Farooq, Tauseef Rana
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Traditional black-box machine learning systems, are powerful, often fail to gain trust of clinical staff due to their opacity. For the solution of this gap, this research proposes an Explainable AI (XAI) framework designed to enhance trust and usability of ML models in maternal healthcare. Specifically, SHAP (SHapley Additive Explanations) and LIME (Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations) are employed to provide transparent, interpretable insights into model behavior. The study follows an empirical, model-based, and quantitative research design using two public and anonymized datasets: a multi-class Maternal Health Risk Prediction dataset and a binary Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) dataset. Preprocessing includes missing value imputation, IQR-based outlier removal, class balancing using SMOTE, and feature selection using Mutual Information and RFECV. Multiple models of machine learning i.e., Support Vector Machine (SVM), XGBoost, Neural Network, Random Forest, Decision Tree, and Logistic Regression, are evaluated using standard performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, F1-score, and AUC. Experimental results show that SVM achieves the highest accuracy 98.42% on the GDM dataset, while XGBoost leads on the maternal health dataset with 86.33% accuracy. The explainability of the models is evaluated using fidelity, consistency, robustness, and simplicity. The scores of these evaluation metrics for both datasets are fidelity (1.0 and 1.0), consistency (1.0 and 1.0), robustness (1.0 and 0.99) whereas, simplicity shows separate values for each features of both datasets. All these scores are optimal or near- optimal levels, confirmed the reliability of the generated explanations.

title: Utilizing Blockchain and IPFS for Government Correspondences in Saudi Arabia
authors: Mohammed O. Alannsary
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: This paper proposes a permissioned blockchain and private IPFS framework aimed at enhancing inter-agency correspondence in Saudi Arabia. The proposed design supersedes the outdated centralized system, ensuring immutability, verifiability, and secure document exchange through the Government Secure Network. A pilot roadmap outlines governance, integration, and key performance indicators for nationwide implementation. The work presents an implementation-ready architecture bridging technical and policy dimensions of e-government modernization.

title: Predicting Financial Success Through ESG Metrics: Leveraging Machine Learning and Explainable AI
authors: Abuzar Khan, Ahmad Junaid, Abid Iqbal, Ghassan Husnain
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: This study examined the relationship between Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) performance and financial outcomes via unified combination of machine learning and deep learning frameworks. A global and publicly available dataset of firms was analyzed by combining ESG indicators with financial metrics such as revenue, profit margin and market capitalization. Machine learning models such as XGBoost, LightGBM and TabNet, a state of the art are applied for both regression and classification analysis throughout the dataset. The results have shown strong predictive performance with classification accuracy reaching 0.88 and AUC values above 0.90 while the regression models has achieve R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> value above than 0.90 and RMSE as low as 0.34. Explainable AI methods such as SHAP and LIME have identified the market capitalization, water usage and carbon emissions as the most influential key drivers in the domain while governance remaining the leading ESG factor across most settings, while cross domain evaluation have further confirmed robustness with domain level accuracies of 0.69 and 0.78. Scenario simulations also indicated that targeted ESG improvements such as a 10% increase in governance scores produced measurable although modest gains in profitability. These findings were then incorporated into a prototype decision support system which allowed managers to test ESG strategies in an interactive way. Overall the framework delivers robust interpretable and practical insights that align sustainability practices with financial performance.

title: Machine-Learning-Based Surrogate Model for Predicting Plasma Etching Profiles
authors: ZiyaoLuo, Xiuzhen Lu
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Precise prediction of plasma etching profiles is required in advanced semiconductor manufacturing, where process development has traditionally been constrained by costly trial-and-error procedures and expensive physics-based simulations. In this study, a data-driven surrogate model based on deep neural networks (DNNs) is constructed to predict etched-profile geometries under varying process conditions. Multiple network architectures, regularization strategies, and preprocessing schemes are systematically evaluated through k-fold cross-validation. High prediction accuracy (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> > 0.98) is achieved, demonstrating that reliable surrogate modeling of plasma etching can be obtained at significantly reduced computational cost. The proposed framework provides a rapid alternative to high-fidelity simulations and establishes a foundation upon which future optimization or inverse-design methods may be developed.

title: Self-Supervised Cross-Modal Representation Learning for Fine-Grained Image–Text Knowledge Fusion
authors: Sai Reddy Busi Reddy, Pradeep Kumar Chilukury, Naresh Kumar Methuku, Krishna Kishor Tirupati
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: This paper introduces a new self-adaptive loss learning scheme to solve the challenges of the extreme class imbalance problem in long-tailed image classification. Unlike the conventional methods based on static loss functions, the proposed method introduces a dynamically learned weight mechanism through bilevel optimization, which allows the model to adaptively emphasize hard and underrepresented samples during training. The approach is evaluated on a long-tailed variant of the CIFAR-100 dataset on which the standard performance of models is significantly decreased by severe distribution skew. Experimental results show a significant improvement with a substantial improvement over traditional cross entropy training, Top-1 accuracy improved from 15.64% to 51.66%, Macro-F1 score improved from 15.30% to 51.73%, and balanced accuracy improved from 15.64% to 51.66%. Furthermore, the proposed approach brings faster and more stable convergence in which training loss is reduced from 3.21 to 2.39 while training accuracy is boosted to 52.76%. These results demonstrate the power of adaptive loss learning to reduce long-tailed bias, and provide a scalable and generalizable approach to robust deep learning under imbalanced data regimes.

title: TASH-Net: A Novel Deep CNN for Brain Tumor Classification from MRI Images
authors: Hussein Alkatout, Jinan Charafeddine, Mohammad Attique Khan, Jihad Al Jaam, Taha Houda
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: The process of identifying brain tumors from MRI scans proves difficult because of the extensive data quantity and the intricate nature of tumors. The research introduces TASH as a hybrid deep convolutional neural network which combines Vision Transformer elements with MBConv blocks and fractal design structure in a 357-layer system that uses 6.7M parameters. TASH is compared with state-of-the-art pre-trained models including InceptionV3, ConvNeXt-Base, MNASNet, Vision Transformer, and Swin Transformer. All models were fine-tuned and evaluated on two public MRI datasets. The TASH model achieved 97% accuracy on Dataset 1 but InceptionV3 produced the best results (99%) when working with Dataset 2. The proposed hybrid architecture shows better classification results because it uses its parameters effectively which makes it appropriate for using MRI images to identify brain tumors.

title: Experimental Characterization of Epoxy-Bonded Ring-Type Ni/PZT/FeGaCu Composites for Displacement Sensing Applications
authors: Mukesh Soni, Sumit, A. Arockiarajan
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Magnetoelectric (ME) composites have attracted significant attention as advanced functional materials for sensing applications. However, their use in displacement sensing remains limited due to low sensitivity, the requirement of an external bias magnetic field, and inefficient interfacial coupling. Therefore, enhancing sensor performance through optimized geometric design and effective material distribution is crucial for achieving high sensitivity, low-bias operation, and reliable practical implementation. In this study, epoxy-bonded ring-type Ni/PZT/FeGaCu ME composites were systematically investigated. First, various geometrical configurations, including single-ring and distributed-ring structures, were fabricated and experimentally characterized. ME measurements revealed that the square Distributed Ring Structure-4 configuration exhibited the highest ME coefficient of 1742 millivolts per centimetre per oersted, demonstrating significant improvement compared to previously reported ME composites. Furthermore, displacement sensing measurements demonstrated that the single-ring configuration achieved a maximum sensitivity of 5.673 μV/μm with excellent linearity (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> > 0.95), representing up to a 94.77% improvement over previously reported ME sensors. Moreover, fatigue characterization revealed that exposure to 105 cycles of a DC magnetic field caused no measurable degradation in performance, confirming the robustness and long-term stability of the proposed ME composites for displacement sensing applications.

title: Design and Evaluation of a Compact, MR-Compatible Origami Continuum Robot with Variable Length
authors: Qiqiang Hu, Jian Tao, Hu Jin, Hangjie Mo, Erbao Dong, Xiaojian Li, Gang Feng, Shiwu Zhang, Dong Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Medical Robotics and Bionics
abstract: Continuum robots have recently gained attention for their great value in medical applications. This paper presents a novel design approach for MR-compatible medical continuum robots with the ability to variable length and controllable motion at compact size and light weight. The robot employs a small-scale origami continuum structure with an outside diameter of 15 mm, is driven by three tendon-shape memory alloy (SMA) actuators, and senses the motion of actuators by a thin linear potentiometer (Thinpot). The robot system is lightweight at 76.7 g and compact, with the proximal sensing and actuation module measuring only 34 mm in diameter and 90 mm in length. It is first demonstrated that the Thinpot is insensitive to magnetic disturbance and MR-compatible and can realize accurate feedback control of the large stroke of SMA actuators, even in a real 1.5T MR environment. Further, the bending angle and tip position of the robot can be controlled based on an inverse kinematic model. The robots equipped with different tools will show versatility in biomedical applications, such as endoscopy and needle insertion.

title: Self-Navigation in Non-Adversarial Crowds: An Invariant Set-Based Approach
authors: J. Veejay Karthik, Leena Vachhani
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control Systems Technology
abstract: Self-navigation in noncoordinating crowded environments is a challenging task within multiagent systems consisting of nonholonomic robots operating through local sensing. Here, self-navigation refers to complete autonomy without relying on any external systems for navigation. In this setting, our primary objective is the development of a fast, novel, sensor-driven controller that directly computes control commands to enable safe maneuvering while coexisting with other agents. We propose an input-constrained feedback controller for nonholonomic mobile robots and characterize the associated invariant sets. These invariant sets are the key to maintaining stability and safety amidst noncooperating agents. We then introduce a planning strategy that steers the generation of invariant sets toward the agent’s intended target, enabling robots to compute provably safe control inputs without preplanned paths or trajectories when navigating crowded multiagent environments. The practicality of our technique is demonstrated through hardware experiments, and we provide a comparison with existing navigation strategies where each robot makes independent sensing and decisions. The proposed approach is well-suited to scenarios that demand rapid, on-the-fly control computations based solely on real-time perceived safety bounds.
