title: Pre-trained Under Noise: A Framework for Robust Bone Fracture Detection in Medical Imaging
authors: Robby Hoover, Nelly Elsayed, Zag ElSayed, Chengcheng Li
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Medical Imagings are considered one of the crucial diagnostic tools for different bones-related diseases, especially bones fractures. This paper investigates the robustness of pre-trained deep learning models for classifying bone fractures in X-ray images and seeks to address global healthcare disparity through the lens of technology. Three deep learning models have been tested under varying simulated equipment quality conditions. ResNet50, VGG16 and EfficientNetv2 are the three pre-trained architectures which are compared. These models were used to perform bone fracture classification as images were progressively degraded using noise. This paper specifically empirically studies how the noise can affect the bone fractures detection and how the pre-trained models performance can be changes due to the noise that affect the quality of the X-ray images. This paper aims to help replicate real world challenges experienced by medical imaging technicians across the world. Thus, this paper establishes a methodological framework for assessing AI model degradation using transfer learning and controlled noise augmentation. The findings provide practical insight into how robust and generalizable different pre-trained deep learning powered computer vision models can be when used in different contexts.

title: Edge–Cloud Predictive Maintenance Models for Industry 4.0
authors: Ahmad Junaid, Abuzar Khan, Abid Iqbal, Ghassan Husnain
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: This paper presents a predictive maintenance framework for Industry 4.0 that combines preprocessing, feature engineering, machine learning, deep learning, explainability and prescriptive optimization within a single unified system. The dataset was first cleaned, balanced and engineered to retain 10 key features, with torque identified as the most important predictor. Baseline models such as CatBoost, LightGBM, and TabNet showed strong performance, with CatBoost achieving an accuracy of 0.994 and a ROC-AUC of 0.994. Deep learning simulations were also compared with lightweight edge models and cloud based architectures, and the shallow MLP achieved an accuracy of 0.962 with a latency of 1.2 milliseconds. SHAP and LIME further showed that torque related ratios and failure type labels played an important role in accurate prediction. This helps make the system more transparent and easier to trust in real-world industrial use. Final benchmarking showed that edge models delivered inference nearly 10 times faster, whereas the highest accuracy of 0.993 and F1 score of 0.992 were obtained by the cloud models. Overall, the results indicate a scalable, balanced and sustainable predictive maintenance system that improves efficiency, accuracy and operational cost.

title: Prediction of Chip Aging-Induced VMIN Shift Based on Spice Simulation
authors: Haiyong Wang, Qi Wei, Chenfei Wu, Hailiang Zhang, Guohua Zhou, Keqing Ouyang
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Aging effects significantly degrade transistor performance, leading to increased minimum operating voltage (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">min</inf>) in deep sub-micron technologies. This paper presents a SPICE-based simulation methodology to predict aging-induced V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">min</inf> shift by integrating compact aging models. The V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">min</inf> shift of simulation prediction align good with high-temperature operating life (HTOL) measured result. Results demonstrate the feasibility of simulation-driven V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">min</inf> shift prediction for robust circuit design over product lifetime.

title: Safeguarding Scientific Algorithms: A Framework for Detecting Algorithmic Plagiarism in Metaheuristic Research
authors: Colin L. Heffern, Halbert N. Nguyen, Walter H. Cook, Gunni U. Kamran, Giovanna Camacho, Matthew L. Bolton
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: Academic misconduct increasingly occurs through algorithmic duplication, in which existing, lightly modified methods are presented as novel. This problem is especially relevant in metaheuristic optimization, a field that has experienced rapid growth along with concerns about derivative algorithms. This paper presents an AI-driven framework for detecting algorithmic plagiarism in metaheuristic research by comparing the computational logic of algorithms rather than their surface-level similarity. The proposed pipeline extracts pseudocode from academic PDFs, canonicalizes algorithms, and compares them against a reference database using four levels of similarity analysis: surface, structural, primitive, and semantic. Scores from each level are combined into an overall similarity score. The system was tested through controlled verification cases and real non-novel examples identified by experts. Results showed clear separation between disguised derivations of the same algorithm and genuinely distinct algorithms, supporting the framework’s ability to measure algorithmic novelty. These findings suggest that automated detection of algorithmic plagiarism is feasible. It could support research integrity efforts by helping journals and reviewers identify submissions in need of close expert review.

title: A Lightweight Approach to Spam Link Identification Using Automated Domain Reputation Scoring
authors: Petar Alilović, Davor Cafuta, Danijela Pongrac, Brigitta Cafuta, Ivica Dodig
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: This paper examines the extraction and reputation analysis of domains identified within an email dataset containing both spam and ham messages. Using the Enron corpus, data processing was conducted in a virtualised Ubuntu environment (VMware) to ensure security. A custom Python application extracted domains from the message bodies, with the structured output stored in .parquet format for subsequent analysis on the host system. The reputation of each identified domain was assessed using the Open PageRank API. The methodology includes comprehensive statistical analysis, such as calculating average scores and examining the distribution of domains across a categorised ranking scale (1–10). Experimental results reveal a significant disparity between the two categories, with domains associated with spam exhibiting a notably lower average reputation. The study outlines the technical implementation, the technology stack, and provides an evaluation of the benefits and limitations of this approach, highlighting its potential application in improving automated spam filtering systems

title: Is It Too Early to Talk “Quantum Software”?
authors: Hassan Soubra
publish: IEEE Annals of the History of Computing
abstract: Quantum computing (QC) is often framed as a technological race defined by qubit counts, hardware breakthroughs, and error-correction milestones. Between roughly 2014 and 2025, however, a distinct quantum software layer began to emerge alongside advances in physical devices. Drawing on indicators such as investment patterns, patent activity, the institutionalization of university programs, and emerging measurement standardization efforts, this essay suggests that the mid-2020s may represent a formative period in the consolidation of quantum software as a recognizable field. The question is not simply whether it is premature to speak of “quantum software” but what historians can observe when a discipline begins to take shape ahead of its anticipated underlying hardware.

title: System Verification of Compute Express Link over Optics Towards Large Scale Memory Pooling
authors: Xuhui Liu, Chun-Zhang Chen, Yue Geng, Liang Wang, Xiaoli Fang, Quan Pan, Hanming Wu
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: With growing interest in enhanced bandwidth and reduced latency between the central processing unit (CPU) and memory devices, numerous system-level interconnecting technologies have been focused on system solutions in recent years. Among them, compute express link (CXL), developed based on peripheral component interconnect express (PCIe) physical layer, has emerged as one of the most promising solutions, owing to its capability to ensure efficient and flexible utilization of memory resources. Nevertheless, existing platforms still fail to meet all the comprehensive requirements for practical optical and electrical applications, especially in modern data centers between computing and memory cabinets. In this work, we have proposed and experimentally demonstrated a system interconnecting verification platform including an Intel Xeon CPU, an Agilex field programmable gate array (FPGA) and two linear-drive pluggable optics (LPO) optical modules. Benefiting from robust compatility and adaptability, this verification platform has obtained minimal end-to-end latency (629.7 ns) and high bandwidth (14.4 GB/s). Moreover, the eye diagrams of the whole loopback link have been monitored to confirm the feasibility of our verification platform system. Current work also demonstrates high performance in signal transmission and provides potential perspectives for the realizing scale-up memory pooling and further enabling next-generation optoelectronic interconnecting system.

title: Governed Agentic Cloud Data Pipelines with MCP Gateways
authors: Aswathnarayan Muthukrishnan Kirubakaran, Vinoth Punniyamoorthy, Akshay Deshpande, Adithya Parthasarathy, Nachiappan Chockalingam, Mythili Annamalai Sekar, Naga Surya Pasupuleti, Shiva Carimireddy
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Autonomous agents are increasingly used to orchestrate cloud data and machine learning pipelines to improve adaptability and operational efficiency. However, most agent-tool integrations rely on loosely specified interfaces and prompt-driven invocation, providing limited guarantees on correctness, security, and governance. This restricts adoption in production and regulated environments where auditability, policy compliance, and operational reliability are mandatory. We present a governed orchestration architecture in which all agent actions are mediated by a Model Context Protocol (MCP) gateway that enforces bidirectional JSON Schema validation, semantic compatibility, and policy constraints while recording append-only telemetry for auditing and root cause analysis. The gateway establishes a protocol-level trust boundary between autonomous reasoning and production tools. We implement a prototype and evaluate it on open datasets and workloads across batch and micro-batch pipelines with controlled failure and violation injection. Results show that MCP mediation improves pipeline success rate by up to 7.6%, reduces mean time to recovery by 58.3%, blocks over 96% of injected schema, policy, and security violations, and introduces less than 6.2% p95 end-to-end latency overhead. These findings indicate that MCP-based gateways enable reliable and auditable deployment of agentic orchestration in production cloud pipelines.

title: LAPO: A Load-Aware and Priority-Based Offloading Strategy for Latency-Critical Applications in Vehicular Fog Computing
authors: Md Altab Uddin Molla, Asif Uddin Khan, Binayak Kar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: For critical real-time service delivery, task offloading acts as a key function, primarily for latency-sensitive and high-priority applications such as emergency response or collision avoidance. The paper presents a new strategy for task offloading called Load-Aware Priority-Based Offloading (LAPO), where the offloading of computational tasks from vehicles to fog nodes is performed dynamically. The LAPO functions are based on the prevailing system load conditions on two fronts: task load and priority. Contrarily to conventional offloading methods that route or treat all tasks in an equal manner, the LAPO follows a dual-metric decision framework, considering some load parameter of the fog node and some urgency factor of the task so as to improve resource utilization and minimize response time. In the simulation evaluation, LAPO performed better than the two baselines in terms of latency, task failure rates, and, most importantly, the prioritization of critical tasks in congested network scenarios. This method is particularly effective in dense urban environments characterized by high vehicular traffic. By incorporating intelligent load balancing and priority scheduling mechanisms, the system can significantly enhance safety-critical response times while improving overall Quality of Service (QoS).

title: The Impact of Data Analytics On Decision Making Evidence Based Practices
authors: Mahmoud Allahham, Bashar Khaled Almagharbwh, Jawad Hasan Alkhateeb, Taha Houda, Jihad Jaam
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: The paper examines how educational data analytics influence student-level decision-making in schools and will focus on how evidence-based practice mediates increases in the effectiveness of data-driven strategies. The research design was a quantitative one and a structured questionnaire was given to 350 teachers, principals and school administrators in Jordanian schools. The research paper focuses on the impact of the systematic use of the education data on the strategic, teaching and learning, and administrative decision-making. The findings indicate that educational data analytics plays a significant role in enhancing the quality of decisions made by the decision-makers as it gives them the correct information with regard to student performance, resource allocation and curriculum effectiveness. Also, this relationship is partially mediated by evidence-based practice, which means that the more sustainable educational outcomes are achieved based on the analyzed data and empirical evidence. Nevertheless, the factors that limit the comprehensive adoption of analytics in the school practice are lack of data literacy, intermittent data quality, and inadequate analytical infrastructure. According to the findings, the practice of forming data-informed culture at the school level is crucial and relies on the fact that evidence-based practice can turn crude data into actionable plans. The paper hypothesizes that to enable educational data analytics to contribute to effective, transparent, and informed decision-making, it is crucial to amuse educators with their independent and evaluative abilities.

title: Holding the Line: A Digital Twin Framework Supporting Policy Design for U.S. Power Grid Resilience
authors: Daniel Freedman, Alison Pike, Avery Suriano, Walker Watson, Henning Mortveit
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: The U.S. power grid faces growing reliability challenges as rising peak demand, renewable intermittency, and baseload capacity retirements strain existing infrastructure. While long-term solutions require grid expansion, near-term resilience depends on optimizing current resources. Price-based demand response (PBDR) incentivizes households to shift electricity use away from peak periods; however, existing evaluations aggregate load reductions across entire service territories, obscuring impacts on specific grid assets and communities. To address this gap, this paper develops:• A high-resolution digital twin framework that couples a synthetic population, hourly electricity demand profiles, and a distribution network to derive system-level demand reductions from individual households• A stochastic adoption model and demand reallocation algorithm to capture how household demographics drive load shifts across the distribution network• A case study of Virginia’s Eastern Shore under two candidate PBDR policies, producing 4–5% peak load reductions consistent with empirical literature while resolving shifts at the household and substation levelsThe results demonstrate that established demand response effects can be reproduced at household and substation resolution without loss of fidelity. By linking demographic characteristics and load-shifting behavior to substation-level outcomes, the framework can inform more resilient demand-side energy policy. This framework is extendable nationwide, providing a scalable approach to evaluate the grid impacts of utility policies.

title: A Vision-Based Deep Learning Framework for Egyptian Sign Language Recognition Using CNN and VGG-16
authors: Fatma G. El-Megharbel, Mennatalla T. Elgamal, Mazen Ebrahim, Tamer M. Nassef
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Sign language is considered the main linguistic bridge for the Deaf and Hard-of-Hearing community; however, a continuous communication gap still exists because only a limited number of hearing individuals have achieved fluency in sign languages. This study presents an efficient vision-based deep learning system for recognizing Egyptian Sign Language (ESL) using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and transfer learning techniques.The proposed models were developed using a dataset that contains 20 signs that are frequently used in everyday life in Egypt, expressed in two different forms according to the Egyptian dialect and Standard Arabic.The Model’s accuracy was evaluated through the integration of spatial feature extraction using CNNs with temporal modeling to capture sign motion, specifically employing the VGG16 architecture. To measure real-world hand and body landmarks, Media Pipe Holistic was utilized to enhance recognition performance during testing. The experimental results indicate that the VGG16-based model achieved an overall accuracy of 96.12%, while the CNN-based model achieved an overall accuracy of 96%. To further develop and validate the proposed algorithm, a real-time graphical user interface application was created for sign prediction. These results establish the proposed framework as a scalable and effective solution for automating sign language recognition and improving the overall quality of life for individuals in the Deaf and Hard-of-Hearing communities.In this paper, Arabic Sign Language is referred to as ArSL to avoid confusion with American Sign Language (ASL).

title: Reconstruction of Arterial Blood Pressure Waveforms from Photoplethysmogram Using a Modified Transformer-CNN Model
authors: Mathew Cigi, V Raj Kiran, P. M. Nabeel, Jayaraj Joseph
publish: 2026 IEEE Medical Measurements &amp; Applications (MeMeA)
abstract: Accurate and continuous monitoring of arterial blood pressure (ABP) is essential for the diagnosis and management of cardiovascular diseases. While photoplethysmography (PPG) signals offer a convenient and non-invasive alternative to conventional cuff-based measurements, predicting continuous ABP waveforms from PPG remains challenging due to the complex and non-linear relationship between these signals. This study presents a novel deep learning framework that integrates a transformer encoder with a convolutional neural network (CNN) decoder to reconstruct ABP waveforms directly from PPG signals. The transformer encoder is designed to capture long-range temporal dependencies and contextual relationships within the PPG signal, enabling modeling of dynamics over extended time windows. In contrast, the CNN decoder focuses on reconstructing morphological details of the ABP waveform, including characteristic features such as the systolic upstroke and diastolic decay. The proposed model was evaluated on the PulseDB dataset and achieved a mean absolute error (MAE) of $\mathbf{4. 5 6} \boldsymbol{\pm} \mathbf{5. 7 2 ~ m m H g}$ for continuous ABP waveform reconstruction, demonstrating strong correlation between predicted and reference signals. Additionally, the model achieved MAEs of 4.79 ± 6.82 mmHg for systolic blood pressure (SBP) and $3.93 \pm 5.34$ mmHg for diastolic blood pressure (DBP). The obtained error magnitudes and variability are within the limits recommended by the Association for the Advancement of Medical Instrumentation (AAMI), indicating estimation accuracy. Overall, this work highlights the potential of transformer-CNN hybrid architectures for accurate, continuous, and non-invasive blood pressure monitoring in clinical and wearable healthcare applications.

title: Teryaq: An IoT-Enabled System for the Smart Delivery of Temperature-Sensitive Medications
authors: Durrah Aloulah, Futun Alqudayri, Mozn Alzunidi, Farah Alsubaheen, Shadan Aldhawi, Amal Aljohani
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Temperature-sensitive medications transportation faces significant challenges, especially in maintaining the needed environmental conditions to guarantee safety and efficacy. Preserving the quality of the medication is critical, and many factors play a major role in the medication safe to be used. Temperature stability is a critical factor that needs to be monitored and controlled, especially during hospital-to-patient deliveries of temperature-sensitive medications. However, traditional transportation methods show limitations in this regard, increasing the risk of temperature excursions that can compromise patient safety. This paper presents a smart system called Teryaq which is designed to enhance the transportation of temperature-sensitive medications by ensuring optimal delivery conditions. The system integrates real-time environmental monitoring with intelligent routing algorithms to minimize risk and improve efficiency. Leveraged with wireless communication and embedded sensors, the system continuously monitors temperature and automatically transmits reports and alerts. The system identifies the safest and most efficient routes by analyzing data such as delivery time and traffic patterns. It combines hardware and intelligent software to ensure reliable performance, even under rapidly changing conditions. This work aims to ensure safe, reliable, efficient, patient-centered delivery of temperature-sensitive medications while minimizing medication waste.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Intelligent Workload Scheduling for Cloud-Native Systems Under Bursty Demand
authors: Venkata Raja Satya Teja Nagavalli, Sapan Bharadwaj Bonala, Sharath Chandra Samineni
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Cloud-native systems often have bursty and highly imbalanced workload dynamics that undermine the performance of traditional resource schedulers especially when peak demand is challenging to predict on a short-term basis. This paper introduces an uncertainty-aware, burst-risk-directed workload scheduling scheme in cloud systems that integrates window-based workload modeling, burst-aware temporal feature engineering, long short-term memory learning, and risk-sensitive CPU allocation. In contrast to normal heuristic or forecast-only schedulers, the one presented actually marks high-risk burst windows and becomes more conservative in allocation when the burst likelihood is high in order to align the scheduling decisions with the operational risk. Large-scale cloud workload trace experimental evaluation indicated good burst-risk detection performance with accuracy of 0.9707, precision of 0.9594, recall of 0.9829, F1-score of 0.9710, and ROC-AUC of 0.9868. The proposed method minimized the SLA violation rate to 0.5426, reduced mean under-provision to 0.0112, and boosted burst-window protection to 0.1441 in terms of minimizing the violation rate, minimizing mean under-provision, and maximizing burst-window protection, respectively, compared to heuristic and forecast-based baselines. These findings point to the originality and practical significance of incorporating burst-risk intelligence into dependable cloud-native resource management.

title: Analysis of the impact of SACQ solder balls and high-reliability solder paste on board-level temperature cycle life
authors: Yunong Zhao, Zhuqiu Wang, Niuyi Sun, Dan Yang, Na Mei, Keqing Ouyang
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: As advanced packaging and chip applications continue to evolve, board-level thermal cycle life remains a challenging requirement. This study, through specialized board-level thermal cycle testing, systematically analyzed the effects of SACQ solder balls and high-reliability solder paste on thermal cycle life, drawing three key conclusions that can guide chip life improvement efforts.

title: Privacy-Preserving AI using Federated Multimodal Attention Networks for Healthcare and Robotics
authors: Sathish Kumar Natarajan, Azween Abdullah, Ilangovan Perumal, Prabhu Natarajan
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: The integration of multimodal data in healthcare and autonomous navigation presents privacy challenges while demanding high accuracy. This paper introduces a novel framework combining multimodal attention networks with federated learning for privacy-preserving AI. Our approach leverages modality-aware attention mechanisms to fuse heterogeneous data, including medical images, clinical records, RGB-D streams, and LiDAR data, while ensuring differential privacy (ε = 1.0, δ = 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−5</sup>). The system demonstrates 96.3% accuracy in healthcare diagnosis and 89.4% success rate in navigation, with an 85% communication reduction and <200ms latency. Our key contributions include a novel multimodal attention architecture for federated learning, a privacy-utility trade-off analysis, and comprehensive experimental validation across various domains.

title: Predicting Startup Success: A Machine Learning Framework Integrating Technology, Founder Psychology, Mentorship, and Sustainability
authors: Debani Prasad Mishra, Abhijeet Raj, Subham Mohanty, Rohnit Guru, Rohan Jha, Dibya Jyoti Mishra
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Imagine a world where new companies are driving technology forward, and the movement of the economy even lags behind. Unbalanced outcomes become apparent very soon and indicate severe limitations to the traditional methods of predicting success that neglect morality and fulfilling the human needs. The given study seals the given gap and establishes the four pillars of technological change, connections in mentorship, the psychology of a founder, and sustainability habits, combined into one predictive model. A select list of 100+ new companies was constructed with other criteria such as flexibility, people skill, and leadership involvement compiled with numerical scores. Generation of the scores was done by a hand coded approach. To illustrate, Resilience Scores (1-10) were based on a Likert-scale assessment of problems and changes perceived and logged in a database, whereas Decision-Making Speed (hours) was based on time patterns in actions of founders. This mode takes the k-means clustering on the new businesses to classify them into behavioural categories and applies logistic regression to predict their probable feasibility in associating deep knowledge with formal computations. This paper presents a consistent framework of investigating the regulations of various aspects of business prosperity. The framework challenges the old money-focused concept and changes the focus to power, moral response, and leadership in threatening circumstances. Altogether, the research has a certain positive impact on the field of startup analytics and provides real-world instruments to assist investors and founders to proceed with managing the current business issues.

title: Cross-Scene HSI Classification Combined with Channel-Spatial Attention Fusion Network
authors: Yalin Li, Aili Wang, Haibin Wu
publish: 2025 International Symposium on Computer Science and Educational Technology (ISCSET)
abstract: Hyperspectral imagery (HSI) classification faces challenges stemming from intricate spectral signatures and distributional discrepancies. In this paper, we introduce a new classification method that fuses spatial-spectral attention and domain generalization technology, and propose a hybrid deep attention architecture that integrates CNN and transformer for dual-branch (local-global) hyperspectral classification, enabling efficient extraction of discriminative spectral features. In addition, we use a coordinated optimization framework with a Lion optimizer and cosine annealing strategy to reduce local optimality. Results from experiments on various HSI datasets indicate the proposed method surpasses state-of-the-art HSI classification models, which validates its efficacy in cross scene HSI classification.

title: A Novel Medical Images Random Cryptosystem
authors: Eman Alnagi, Amal Saif, Ashraf Ahmad
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Medical images are considered highly sensitive data that must be protected for privacy purposes. Encryption algorithms are one way to secure medical images before transmitting them to concerned parties. In this paper, a novel cryptosystem is proposed for medical images. The encryption and decryption algorithms utilize linear feedback shift registers (LFSRs) to generate random numbers to represent the positions of image blocks to be processed. Accordingly, the blocks are encrypted using Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) with a new mode of operation, random vector cipher (RVC), in a random order. This random encryption results in shuffled cipher blocks, and the number of these blocks would exceed the actual number of original image blocks, which may confuse the attacker in distinguishing the type of data encrypted. In addition to the traditional AES key, several secrets are used to leverage the robustness of the cryptosystem. The algorithm is implemented and tested on samples of the Medical MNIST dataset, resulting in uniform histograms for the encrypted images while preserving the correctness and integrity of the original images.

title: AI-Enabled Remote Proctoring: Architecture and Implementation for Digital Examinations
authors: Vishal Shukla, Aatam Prakash Sharma, Surbhi Ghai, Ashwath Suresh Hegde, Lalit Rupani, Pradnya Desai
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: With the increased use of online examinations since the pandemic, academic integrity has come under assault like never before, without bodies in the room to guarantee the proper integrity and reliability of assessment. Therefore, educators turn to new solutions and mechanisms that can ensure reliability and integrity across virtual assessment situations. This paper presents an AI-based online examination assessment system that monitors student activity in front of a camera during an exam, and at the same time, renders warnings to the student and/or assesses integrity breaches after the exam. This system relies on computer vision-based techniques, the YOLO object detection model—to detect complicated behaviors, unfocused eyes, multiple bodies, and other devices in view, as well as browser actions like minimizing screens and switching windows—during an online examination and assesses browser activities like minimizing windows and toggling tabs. Warnings issued are rendered during the exam in real time, while video footage and pixelated warning frames will be electronically stored for further integrity assessments. By merging multiple approaches to vigilance under one umbrella through an AI-integrated system, a scalable, efficient, and privacy-preserving solution is enabled to accommodate the demand for an appropriate online assessment solution in the modern world since student motivations are not always discernable.

title: A Hadoop-Enabled Framework for Detecting Crop Yield Anomalies and Optimizing Irrigation in Smart Agriculture
authors: Rishuvan Selvakumaran, Kanishka Anantha Narayanan, Rakshan Dass, P.Malin Bruntha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: In precision agriculture, effective irrigation management and early identification of anomalous crop yield variations continue to be major obstacles, especially when dealing with extensive heterogeneous agricultural datasets. Current methods frequently concentrate on isolated statistical techniques or small-scale machine learning models without scalable distributed processing. A Hadoop-enabled framework for scalable agricultural anomaly detection and irrigation optimization is put forth in order to overcome this constraint. For distributed feature extraction, the framework combines MapReduce and Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS). Z-Score analysis, Isolation Forest, One-Class Support Vector Machine, and Random Forest classification are then used for hybrid anomaly detection. Experiments conducted on multi-region historical agricultural datasets (100K–2M records) demonstrate linear scalability in processing and superior anomaly detection performance using Random Forest (F1-score: 0.94). When compared to single-node execution, the suggested architecture cuts the feature extraction time by about 83%. The framework supports the deployment of sustainable smart agriculture, improves water-use efficiency, and makes data-driven irrigation advisories possible.

title: Exposing the Illusion: A Comprehensive Survey on Deepfake Detection Techniques, Challenges, and Future Horizons
authors: Zainab Rafiq, Muhammad Wasim, Rwaida Obaid Alssadi, Widian Alssadi
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: The rapid advancement in deep learning methods has made it possible to produce extremely realistic synthetic media, which are referred to as deepfakes. Deepfakes have creative value in film and media production but are also pose significant threats to privacy, security, and public trust. This paper conducts an extensive overview of deepfake detection techniques, classifying them according to their inherent techniques, such as machine learning, deep neural networks, frequency-based, and multimodal analysis. We analyze the efficacy and limitations of these techniques under different datasets and real-world cases. We further discuss the essential challenges in deepfake detection, including generalizability to out-of-distribution data, adversarial robustness, and lack of high-quality annotated datasets. Lastly, we emphasize new trends and provide directions for promising future work, such as explainable AI integration, lifelong learning, and cross-modal forensics. The survey endeavors to inform researchers and practitioners with a comprehensive view of the existing state and direct future advancements toward protecting the authenticity of digital media.

title: Predictors of Licensure Examination Performance Among BEEd Graduates Toward a Random Forest Predictive Framework
authors: Jocelyn Sagun-De Vera
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: This study examined the statistical predictors of licensure examination performance among Bachelor of Elementary Education graduates and used the findings as a basis for proposing a Random Forest predictive framework. The study utilized a quantitative predictive research design and analyzed the records of 51 graduates from academic years 2018 to 2022 who had taken the Licensure Examination for Teachers (LET). Predictor variables included age, sex, attendance to LET review, academic awards, entrance examination scores, and college grade point average (GPA), while overall LET rating served as the target variable. Descriptive results showed that most respondents were female, had attended formal review sessions, and obtained a GPA of 88. Statistical findings from the available dataset revealed that sex, attendance to LET review, and college GPA were significantly associated with LET performance, with review attendance showing the strongest influence. Correlation and multiple regression analyses indicated that sex, attendance to LET review, and college GPA were significant predictors of LET performance. Based on these findings, a Random Forest predictive framework is proposed for future model implementation and validation.

title: The influence of ISSG hydrogen-to-oxygen ratio on the growth of tunnel oxide for Nor flash memory cycling disturb loss
authors: Xing Wang, Ye-Tao Lu, Cheng Yu, Min Zuo, Yuan-Feng Xu, Lan Sun, Yi-Hua Shen, Zhen-Sheng Xue, Huang Liu
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: The tunnel oxide in NOT OR(Nor) flash memory transistors is one of the most critical and fundamental layers that enable data storage functionality. The quality of the tunnel oxide directly determines data retention capability. During read, write, and erase operations, the thickness and quality of the tunnel oxide are crucial. If this oxide layer has defects, uneven thickness, or contamination, leakage paths can form, leading to charge loss and resulting in data errors. The characteristics of the In-Situ Steam Generation (ISSG) perfectly align with these requirements. The oxide layers grown by ISSG exhibit extremely low initial defect density and a near-perfect Si-SiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> interface. This paper provides an in-depth exploration of improving Nor flash memory cycling disturb loss by adjusting the hydrogen-to-oxygen ratio in the ISSG process. The results indicate that a lower hydrogen-to-oxygen ratio enhances the endurance of Nor flash memory performance, increases the number of program/erase cycles, and improves the Chip Probing(CP) yield of Nor flash memory .

title: Enterprise Governance for Generative AI: Prompt Lifecycle and Secure Middleware
authors: Subhash Tatavarthi, Raghunath Reddy Koilakonda, VenkatasatyaRavikiran Bikkavolu, Sai Krishna Tarakampet, Manoj Gudala
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Organizations are rapidly adopting Generative AI in enterprise decision-making, but governance has not kept pace. Traditional software controls are not designed to manage systems where behavior is shaped by prompts, context, and user intent. These elements change frequently and are rarely tracked, tested, or audited. As GenAI integrates with enterprise platforms, this lack of control introduces real risks. These include policy drift, unintended data exposure, inconsistent outputs, and declining trust in AI-driven decisions.This paper introduces a governance approach built on two foundations: Prompt Lifecycle Management (PLM) and Secure Middleware Design. Prompts are treated as governed assets with defined lifecycle controls, while middleware acts as a control layer to enforce access, policy, and consistency.Applied in enterprise scenarios, this approach leads to more consistent outputs, reduced prompt drift, stronger access control enforcement, and improved auditability. It enables organizations to move from experimental AI use to reliable and compliant enterprise deployment.

title: Optimizer-Driven Techniques for Retuning Prompts on LLM Backend with Migration across Models for Healthcare and Cyber Security Operations
authors: Hassan Rehan, Zane Zimmerman, Jamshaid Iqbal Janjua
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: The relocation of the workflows that are provided by one model to another is becoming more and more frequent, yet it remains particularly difficult in the healthcare and cyber security operations where the accuracy, safety, privacy, and cost factors are off the table. We present an optimizer-based design where migration is a black-box, constraint-sensitive, optimization problem, automatically re-optimizing existing instructions to a new LLM backend, and not based on internal model information. The method combines a meta-optimizer, transformation operators, and warm-starting using history evaluation traces to accelerate adaptation and minimize the cost of trials. We test the framework on popular LLM backends, such as OpenAI GPT-4, Anthropic Claude, and Google Gemini, on operationally motivated healthcare tasks and cyber security tasks, such as clinical note summarization and triage, compliance-oriented question answering, incident report analysis, alert triage, and threat intelligence extraction. The proposed approach is effective, better at adaptation and the cost of operations in various settings, such as compared to manual retuning, zero-shot transfer, and random search, and constrained to domains and safety. The findings indicate that optimizer-based migration is a viable and scalable approach to ensuring stable LLM performance when quickly changing the backend in production environments.

title: Language-Driven Portrait Editing and Animation via Diffusion and Motion Control
authors: Shoaib Mustafa Khan
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: We present a feasibility study for a novel pipeline that unifies natural language–driven portrait editing with temporally consistent talking-head animation by integrating GPT-4o instruction parsing, YOLOv8-Seg region masking, Stable Diffusion XL (SDXL) inpainting, and HunyuanPortrait motion transfer. Unlike existing frameworks that treat image editing and motion reenactment as isolated tasks, our system bridges them into an end-to-end workflow where user queries (e.g., "make the man wear a hat") are automatically decomposed, spatially localized, semantically edited, and animated. Experiments were conducted on dual RTX 3090 GPUs with separate CUDA allocation for SDXL and HunyuanPortrait to enable joint execution. Quantitative evaluation demonstrates that identity preservation and perceptual quality are largely maintained across edits, with PSNR of 16.78 and SSIM of 0.596 for original image-to-video consistency, compared to PSNR of 15.95 and SSIM of 0.500 for inpainted image-to-video. Cross-video comparison further confirms stability, yielding SSIM of 0.519 and LPIPS of 0.134 between original and inpainted outputs. These results highlight the viability of combining diffusion-based semantic editing with portrait animation, while also revealing current limitations in fine-grained segmentation, inpainting completeness, and inference efficiency.

title: Agri Hub: The Farmer's Digital Partner (Central platform for all farming needs)
authors: Supriya Telsang, Nishant Ninawe, Piyush Pagar, Surabhi Nikam, Om Agarwal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Indian farming, which accounts for 45% of the total workforce and 18% GDP, has faced challenges such as disease outbreaks, wrong choice of crops, loss of profit due to intermediaries, and fragmented technology. This paper proposes a multilingual AI-driven digital farming platform named "AgriConnect," which combines disease diagnosis of crops, choice of crops, prediction of the crop calendar, and lastly a farmer-seller market into one. This platform has a scalable three-tier architecture that combines a React and Vite frontend, a Node.js and Express backend with MongoDB, and finally a FastAPI machine learning inference service. Plant disease diagnosis is processed by a Vision Transformer Model "google/vit-base-patch16-224-in21k," which was fine-tuned on an increased PlantVillage dataset with 38 classes. The top-1 validation accuracy reached 95.12%. With the combination of disease diagnosis, choice of crops, prediction of the crop calendar, and finally creating a farmer-seller market, the tool has brought an end to tool fragmentation, which is part of the aims of Agriculture 4.0 enabled by technology.

title: CARRIE UGV: A Human-Centered Terminal UGV for Carry-on Transport and Wayfinding Support
authors: Arjun Nambiar, Chin Yong Chung, Chuyang Yang, Gunwoo Lee
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: Airports are facing increasing demand for accessible passenger support, particularly for older adults and passengers with disabilities navigating post-security terminal environments. This paper presents CARRIE UGV, an Uncrewed Ground Vehicle (UGV) concept designed to augment existing assistance services by transporting carry-on luggage and supporting indoor wayfinding after Transportation Security Administration (TSA) screening. Guided by a design thinking approach, the concept development was informed by accessibility needs, prior airport assistance studies, and practitioner input from wheelchair-assistance personnel at Daytona Beach International Airport, Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport, and Incheon International Airport. These inputs highlighted persistent service gaps in carry-on handling and terminal navigation, particularly in complex and high-demand operating environments. In response, CARRIE UGV was designed as a human-centered system that integrates real-time environmental sensing with visual and auditory guidance to enable safer, less burdensome passenger movement. The paper further discusses preliminary feasibility through system-level risk, cost-benefit, and sustainability considerations. As a conceptual design, CARRIE UGV demonstrates the potential for UGV-enabled airport services to enhance accessibility and improve the post-security passenger experience without replacing existing human assistance.

title: A Deep Learning-Based Surrogate Model for LOCOS Etch Process TCAD Simulation Using a Conditional Variational Autoencoder
authors: Yan Pan, Yiting Ye, Boying Meng, Kai Xu
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Drain extended MOS (DEMOS) devices are critical components in power management and amplifier circuits, whose performance hinges on the breakdown voltage (BV). Traditional BV characterization via TCAD simulation or wafer fabrication is accurate but costly and slow. We overcome this limitation with a hybrid Deep Neural Network (DNN) and Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) framework, achieving 96% accuracy on simulation data and high performance on measured data, drastically cutting development time and cost.

title: Neural Representation Learning for Unsupervised Control-Flow Concept Drift Detection
authors: Atyasha Apurva, Ananya Kulkarni, B Swathi, Manoj Kumar MV, Prashanth BS, Sneha HR
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: Process mining techniques traditionally presuppose that business processes do not change, nevertheless, in reality, business processes may and often do change with changes of policy, system upgrades or reduced business needs. The concept drift of control flow is brought about by such changes and this reduces the accuracy and reliability of discovered process models. The paper provides an unsupervised neural network-based system that identifies and locates instances of concept drift in control flow when using streaming logs of events. The strategy is in a continuous state of analyzing event sequences to learn latent representations of both past and new traces with no labeled data in the form of information. The comparisons of the temporal distributions of these representations allow observing the sudden and gradual shifts in the process behavior. The noise or weighty occasions that result in false detections are minimized by statistical distance metrics and reconstructive validation. When drift is identified, the framework isolates the fragments of the processes, which are altered, and the activities and transitions are distinguished. The testing on realistic and synthetic process mining benchmarks proves good detection accuracy, known false alarm, and localization.

title: RAST 4.0: Restorable Arbitrary Style Transfer via Content Leakage Correction
authors: Yingnan Ma, Xudong Li, Anup Basu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Smart Multimedia (ICSM)
abstract: Style transfer aims to synthesize images by combining the content structure of one image with the target style. The RAST framework has been developed to enhance content fidelity through a multi-restoration mechanism. However, prior versions, such as RAST 1.0 and RAST 2.0, encountered issues related to color deviation and insufficient style embedding. In this work, we introduce the RAST 4.0 framework to refine the style transfer process. RAST 4.0 incorporates content leakage correction to maintain content integrity. Additionally, we modify the restoration process to optimize the style embedding performance, ensuring a more effective and balanced integration of style information. Extensive qualitative and quantitative experiments demonstrate that RAST 4.0 surpasses previous RAST frameworks and state-of-the-art methods in both content preservation and style consistency, while maintaining computational efficiency.

title: Non-Invasive Driver Activity Recognition using mmWave Radar
authors: Basim Alhumaily, Fahad Ayaz, Sajjad Hussain, Lina Mohjazi, Ahmed Zoha
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Driver monitoring systems are crucial for automotive safety, yet conventional camera-based solutions suffer from lighting variability and significant privacy concerns. This paper presents a privacy-preserving, illumination-invariant alternative using a single Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) mmWave radar sensor to classify subtle driver head and upper-body movements indicative of distraction or fatigue. We collected a dataset from 15 subjects performing six distinct and kinematically similar activities. A specialized signal processing pipeline transforms raw radar data into micro-Doppler spectrograms using the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT). To address the critical challenge of data scarcity, we introduce a tailored data augmentation strategy that adapts the SpecAugment technique to generate a robust training set while preserving the physical characteristics of the radar signatures. Without augmentation, a lightweight, fine-tuned MobileNet-V2 model achieves a baseline accuracy of only 60% on an unseen test set of subjects. By applying our proposed augmentation method with an augmentation factor of 10, the classification accuracy increases significantly to 91.32%. This result underscores the effectiveness of our approach and demonstrates the feasibility of using a single radar sensor for robust, non-invasive driver monitoring, paving the way for the next generation of reliable and privacy-compliant automotive safety systems.

title: Enhanced Parkinson’s Disease Detection using Ensemble Machine Learning and Spiral-Derived Handcrafted Features
authors: Vaibhav Dutt Trivedi, Sanjana Malhotra, Rajkumar S, Arumuga Arun R
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: The early diagnosis of Parkinson’s Disease (PD) is vital for early clinical treatment and better patient outcomes. Hand-drawn spiral images have recently been identified as a non-invasive and economically viable method for neurological analysis. In this paper, a holistic machine learning approach is proposed for distinguishing between healthy and Parkinson’s-affected individuals using hand-designed features derived from spiral images. The proposed feature set comprises statistical features, Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) texture features, Hu moment-based shape features, and edge density features. Various classifiers such as Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest, Logistic Regression, k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN), XGBoost and Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) were used. A weighted soft-voting ensemble of the top three best-performing classifiers (Random Forest, kNN and Logistic Regression) further improved the classification accuracy. Hyperparameter tuning was performed using stratified 5–fold cross-validation. Experimental analysis shows that the proposed ensemble method provides stable and competitive results with lower computational complexity compared to deep learning-based approaches.

title: A Rigid Harness with Haptic Feedback for Robotic and Traditional Service Dogs
authors: Amalie J. Keefe, Justin Kauff, Blake Hament
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: This project aims to develop a harness for the Unitree Go1 and Go2 quadrupeds that implements haptic feedback to the user when obstacles are encountered in their path. This research builds upon previous robotic Seeing Eye dog designs, incorporating feedback from a focus group session, which has led to further understanding what some members of the visually impaired community value in assistive devices. Though future implementations of this work may include more advanced object detection, with computer vision tools such as LiDAR, this project uses an Arduino Uno and ultrasonic sensors for proof of concept. The final deliverable of this project is a functional prototype implemented on the robotic quadruped that includes vibration motors, adaptable mechanical integration, and an ergonomic handle for the harness. Technical design requirements have been outlined, including battery life, budget, and handle dimensions and weight. The prototype will be reviewed at an additional upcoming focus group session for feedback and iterative design. Incorporating user responses to make a useful and desired product, this research looks to advance equity, inclusion, and accessibility for members of the visually impaired community, aiding their independence through ease of navigation in unfamiliar spaces.

title: Energy-Efficient Container Orchestration for HPC Workloads using ML in Kubernetes on Cloud Platforms
authors: Abhimanyu Bajaj, N.S.Pradeep, V.Aarthi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: Cloud computing has revolutionized application development by enabling scalable, flexible, and automated deployment. Kubernetes is at the heart of container orchestration and continuous integration. Although containerization and orchestration technologies are widely deployed, they have not yet been integrated into the High-Performance Computing (HPC) environment, especially for energy efficiency and smart workload distribution. The standard Kubernetes scheduling mechanisms tend to result in underutilized resources, and creating new custom schedulers introduces additional complexity, testing difficulty, and maintenance issues. This research presents an Energy-Efficient Container Orchestration (EE-CO) architecture for HPC workloads that combines Machine Learning (ML) with Kubernetes on the cloud infrastructure. EE-CO optimizes adaptive workload allocation, performance surveillance, and resource management in heterogeneous CPU architectures. It enhanced workload allocation efficiency and cost-effectiveness, and reduced energy consumption and computational costs. The potential of ML-powered Kubernetes orchestration for developing sustainable, scalable, and energy-efficient HPC deployments in the cloud.

title: Evaluating the Englishscore Test as a Digital Alternative for Academic English Assessment in Mongolian Higher Education
authors: Bolormaa Ayurzana, Zolzaya Choijin
publish: 2025 International Symposium on Computer Science and Educational Technology (ISCSET)
abstract: This study investigates the reliability, validity, and user perceptions of the EnglishScore test as a digital alternative for academic English assessment among students at Mongolian public universities. Aligned with the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (CEFR) and motivated by national education reforms, the EnglishScore test offers a mobile-based platform designed to assess core English skills efficiently. Data collected through a structured questionnaire reveal that while many students appreciate the test's accessibility, convenience, and quick results, concerns persist regarding its comprehensiveness and comparability to established exams such as IELTS and TOEFL. Technical challenges, including unstable internet connections and time constraints, also affect the testing experience. The findings suggest that although EnglishScore holds promise as a flexible assessment tool, further refinement is necessary to enhance its reliability and acceptance as a formal academic evaluation. This research contributes to the growing discourse on digital language assessments and provides insights for educators and policymakers seeking to implement effective, technology-driven evaluation methods.

title: Synergistic Fusion of Multimodal Data for Automated and Accurate Diagnosis of Parkinson's Disease
authors: Daria Hemmerling, Martyna Fröhlich, Marta Kaczmarska, Miłosz Dudek, Justyna Krzywdziak, Kamil Burza, Marek Wodziński
publish: 2025 International Conference on Smart Multimedia (ICSM)
abstract: We present a mixed-reality (MR) protocol on HoloLens 2 that standardizes multimodal acquisition across 17 brief, self-guided tasks probing speech/voice, ocular motor control (IR eye tracking), head/upper-limb motor function (IMU, RGB), and gait/posture. After quality control, datasets with ≥ 70% completeness from 137 participants (66 Parkinson’s disease (PD), 71 heathy-control (HC)) were analyzed. Signals underwent domain-specific processing and feature extraction, enabling a comparison of data-fusion strategies. Early fusion with L1-regularized logistic regression achieved AUC 0.995, Accuracy 0.964, F1 0.963 (stratified 5-fold cross-validation). Late-fusion stacking reached AUC 0.985, while decision-level soft voting yielded AUC 0.982. Ablation and meta-model analyses identified head-IMU dynamics and speech/voice as the dominant contributors, with hand-motor and gait/posture providing complementary signal. The results indicate that MR-standardized acquisition combined with principled fusion delivers robust, interpretable PD/HC discrimination and offers a practical foundation for longitudinal and multi-site validation.

title: Azure Windows Defender for Enterprise Cybersecurity: Architecture, Automation, and Measured Operational Impact
authors: Jagadish Almaipeta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Modern enterprises operate in increasingly complex hybrid and cloud-native environments, where traditional perimeter-based security controls are no longer sufficient. The rapid adoption of cloud platforms, remote work models, and distributed applications has expanded the attack surface and introduced new cybersecurity challenges. This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of leveraging Azure-based security services, with a particular focus on Windows Defender technologies, to build a scalable, automated, and resilient enterprise cybersecurity posture. The study explores system architecture, operational deployment strategies, automation capabilities, and measurable security benefits observed in real-world enterprise environments. The findings demonstrate that integrating Windows Defender with Azure security services enables proactive threat detection, reduced response times, and improved operational efficiency, while supporting compliance and governance requirements across large organizations. The evaluation is based on enterprise operational metrics collected over a three-month post-deployment observation period. This study contributes empirical evidence and an operational framework for enterprise-scale security automation using Azure-native XDR.

title: Alumni Employment Outcomes as a Basis for Curriculum Evaluation in a State University’s Bachelor of Arts in Communication Program
authors: Ramon Gian A. Bron
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: Graduate employability is often regarded as an important indicator of efficacy of the academic program, particularly in respect to areas of study that involve communication and that lie at the interface of media production, digital technologies, and workforce preparation. Where state universities are concerned, employment outcomes are applied both as measures of instructional success, but also as indicators of curriculum development, resource allocation and policy development. The application of the use of employability assessments, however, is more frequently assumed to be a compulsory-based tracer study, rather than a systematic curriculum redesign mechanism. In evaluating the curriculum and improving academic system, the paper uses the employment performance of graduates of a Bachelor of Arts in Communication program of a state university in the Philippines as a point of reference. The evaluation model was of a mixed-methods approach with a combination of quantitative tracer survey findings and qualitative interpretation of findings. Quantitative findings were used to establish the employment trends and competency scores, but qualitative findings, founded on interviews and input by employers or industry collaborators, were used to elucidate job transition experiences, use of skills, adequacy of training and curriculum shortcomings. The number of responses received was 80 out of 252 graduates which was approximately 32 percent of the whole population. Out of the respondents, 57 were working within half a year after graduation. The congruence between degree and job was largely positive with 32 congruence showing high, 30 moderate and 8 not congruent. Competency assessment results were desirable and a grand weighted mean of 3.97, which is verbally rated as Agree. Irrespective of such outcomes, graduates were able to mention the issues connected with the lack of experience, limited work, inapplicability of training, knowledge deficiency, and ineffective preparation in some technical knowledge. The study uses employment outcomes as system-level feedback to evaluate and redesign the curriculum unlike compliance-oriented tracer approaches. The findings would help in curriculum development, increased access to industry level resources, improved job placement and higher industry relationships.

title: LLM-Assisted Software Development in Aerospace Engineering Education: A Structured Workflow
authors: Raed Kafafy, Mohamed Okasha, Ossama Abdelkhalik
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly being applied to software development tasks; however, their structured integration into aerospace engineering workflows remains insufficiently documented. This paper presents a structured workflow for LLM-assisted software development in aerospace engineering education, emphasizing problem decomposition, modular prompt design, iterative validation, and human-in-the-loop verification.The proposed methodology is demonstrated through a case study involving the development of a web-based visualization tool for orbital mechanics instruction. Rather than focusing on the software architecture itself, the study analyzes the interaction between domain expertise and AI-generated code, identifies common error categories, and evaluates the role of structured prompting in improving reliability and development efficiency. The results highlight both the potential and limitations of LLM-assisted engineering, underscoring the necessity of expert validation in physics-based applications. The presented workflow provides a practical framework for integrating large language models into aerospace software development and educational tool prototyping.

title: AI Assisted Toll Revenue Forecasting and Reconciliation using Flower Pollination Optimized Dl Model
authors: Venkateswarlu Mullangi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Accurate toll revenue forecasting and reconciliation is essential for effective financial planning and operational control in Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS). This paper presents an Artificial Intelligence (AI)-based predictive analytics framework for toll revenue forecasting and reconciliation. The raw data is fed to data pre-processing technique, containing data cleaning and null value removal, followed by univariate and bivariate using data visualization to identify key patterns and trends. Feature engineering with normalization enhances data consistency and model stability. The proposed Spatial-Temporal based Long Short-Term Memory (ST-LSTM) model is employed to capture temporal dependencies and traffic variations influencing toll revenue. Further the Flower Pollination Algorithm (FPA) is incorporated for hyper parameter optimization. The model’s performance is evaluated using standard prediction metrics to ensure robustness and reliability. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed FPA optimized ST-LSTM have the MAE of 0.034, MSE of 0.00190 and RMSE of 0.04, r2 of 99% effectively improves toll revenue prediction accuracy and supports efficient reconciliation by identifying potential anomalies, constructing it a valuable decision-support tool for transportation agencies.

title: A Comparative Analysis of Predictive and DRL-Derived Target-Based Energy Management Smart Charging Scheduling for Small EV Fleets
authors: Maya J, Suyampulingam A
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: The fast-paced increase in the number of electric vehicles deployed each year makes it essential to develop efficient and cost-effective charging stations that consider the benefits of all stakeholders involved. The rise in high-power extraction tends to stress the grid beyond the acceptable threshold, which can put the electric network at higher risk of instability. Alongside, uncontrolled battery charging can significantly reduce battery health condition. This work proposes a cost-effective grid-tied solar-powered public charging station with a hybrid optimization framework that can allocate varied charging ports (AC/DC) dynamically to the vehicles based on their battery state of health. The battery management system is integrated with this optimization structure to acquire the temperature profile and battery health status for a safer charging schedule. The system is designed to evaluate the energy optimization with three strategies. A basic heuristic approach is compared with the model predictive control method and an advanced deep reinforcement learning-derived target-based control policy (DRLDTBC). The system is simulated for a period of 24 hours, and the result shows that the DRL-derived TBC policy achieves the lowest total grid cost, Rs.2703.43, and a targeted peak grid demand of 25 kW, demonstrating the superior potential of learned control methods in smart charging environments.

title: An Investigation into the Determinants of Aesthetic Prosthesis Development: A Case Study at Citi University
authors: Enkhbold Chuluunbaatar, Chantsaldulam Narandelger, Khos-Erdene Galbaatar
publish: 2025 International Symposium on Computer Science and Educational Technology (ISCSET)
abstract: Aesthetic prostheses play a vital role in enhancing psychological well-being and social acceptance by replicating the natural appearance of lost body parts. This study applies “Full-Profile Conjoint Analysis” to investigate consumer preferences for aesthetic prostheses in Mongolia, a region with limited research on prosthetic adoption. Four key attributes were analyzed: realistic appearance, usability, material selection and cost. Using SPSS Orthogonal Design, 16 product variants were generated and evaluated. The results revealed that usability (27.372%) and material selection (27.558%) had the highest impact on consumer choices, followed by appearance (23.942%) and price (19.855%). These findings provide valuable insights for improving prosthetic design, manufacturing, and affordability in Mongolia, laying the groundwork for future research and development in the field.

title: Maximize Equipment Capacity by EI Products and Digital Twins
authors: JunjieJeremy Wu, Jacob Kim, ChengFrank Wu, Pengfei Lyu
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Within high-volume manufacturing (HVM) foundries, tool efficiency constitutes a critical operational parameter, directly governing wafer throughput capacity. Performance deficiencies in specialized tool types can precipitate significant production bottlenecks. Lam Research addresses this challenge through a comprehensive throughput optimization methodology utilizing its Equipment Intelligence (EI) product suite. Systematic analysis of tool running logs enables the identification of prevalent alarms and potential failure precursors, thereby reducing unscheduled downtime (UDT). Concurrently, the application of video guidance and specialized tooling enhances the precision of preventive maintenance (PM) activities. This continuous improvement in PM quality and reduction in PM duration minimizes both scheduled downtime (SDT) and monitor duration. Collectively, these actions increase overall tool uptime (UT). Furthermore, digital twin simulations facilitate the virtual execution of process flows, enabling the identification of transfer and process bottlenecks. This integrated approach typically achieves a minimum sustained throughput enhancement of 3%, maximizing wafer output within equivalent timeframes.

title: High-Speed ADC CLK Optimization Using Advantest V93000 Wave Scale Precision Source
authors: Ya Gao, Jingyuan Zhou, Wenjun Zhou
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Clock optimization is both a critical challenge and a key determinant of performance in high-speed ADC systems. Clock jitter, which can be categorized into two core types: the random jitter and the deterministic jitter, which introduces sampling errors that are exacerbated by higher input frequencies and increased converter resolution. Primary sources of jitter include crosstalk, electromagnetic interference (EMI), power supply noise, ground bounce, and suboptimal circuit layout. This paper explores two clock-related phenomena observed during high-speed ADC testing on the Advantest V93000 platform. First, when evaluating ADC performance, the quality of the input clock is just as critical as the input signal itself in determining the final signal-to-noise ratio (SNR); the detrimental effects of both random and deterministic jitter on SNR can be effectively mitigated by leveraging low-phase-noise clock sources. Second, a flawed clock system, such as one affected by crosstalk-induced deterministic jitter, which generates clock spurs that undergo convolution with the input signal, distorting the ADC’s output spectrum and even triggering undersampling aliasing in severe cases. To address these issues, the solution lies in adopting the Advantest V93000 Wave Scale Precision Source (WSPS) with narrow band-pass filter (BPF) to optimize ADC’s sampling clock jitter. By suppressing the spurs, eliminated the aliasing and improved the overall ADC test accuracy.

title: A Deep Learning–Enabled Computer Vision System for Real-Time Tomato Spider Mite Detection
authors: Gagandeep Kaur, Manjeet Kour, Rohini Sharma, Meenakshi Rana, Mohit Singh Bisht, Dinesh Singh Dhakar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Tomato spider mite disorder is a problem which affects the heath of the tomatoes and is due to the mite Tetranychus urticae. The mite at first affects the leaf and causes its surface to have yellowish/whitish patches and the effect of the mite feeding at last cause pale/bronze patches. The disorder on becoming more damaging results in leaf falling and curling. The areas under the leaf are also damaged and webs are formed. The growth of the leaves is also inhibited. The photosynthesis also reduces and the fruits come out as bad. The mite flourishes in hot/humid weather and disorder causes market loss. To inhibit the growth of mite which is difficult in open areas the farmers use the chemicals, removal of infected leaves and natural predators. Due to these reasons the early detection of the disorder inhibits the issue. This work uses a GoogLeNet based model on an online dataset. The performance-metric attained by this work is very efficient and results in the accuracy of 100 percent which is perfect. This model in short is efficient, simple and can help farmers get the work done.

title: SAM-Guided Local-Global Feature Fusion for Pan-Cancer Nuclei Segmentation
authors: Aditya Vijay Shinde, C. K. Mohan, Ishu Priya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Accurate nuclear segmentation is crucial in cancer tissue images for diagnosis and treatment. Deep learning approaches have been effective before, but their performance degrades significantly under different image settings. While new foundations like SAM generate stable general features, they still need to be fine-tuned to suit the medical field. This work proposes a hybrid method leveraging local boundary information obtained by the modified U-Net3+ and the global context provided by the pre-trained SAM encoder. The combination is done using an innovative X-Gated Fusion Block that implements squeeze-excitation and cross-attention operations. Adaptive feature selection is enabled through the usage of gated linear units. The proposed solution was evaluated on three publicly available datasets of cancerous images: CryoNuSeg (30 images), NuInsSeg (665 images), and CoNIC (4,981 images). For CryoNuSeg, the SAM-guided model produced the Dice score of 0.8414 and mIoU 0.7284. It demonstrates 26.4% improvement over the U-Net3+ baseline, while enhanced U-Net3+ alone improved the metrics by 7.8%. Model training brought many interesting findings concerning overfitting and necessity of early stopping.

title: Classification of Slope Grade Using Knee Joint Angle Data Based on GAF-CNN Algorithm
authors: Zhuo Wang, Fangliang Yang, Hui Chen, Jiale Zhang, Xiangyang Wang, Chunjie Chen, Xinyu Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Medical Robotics and Bionics
abstract: This study proposes a Gramian Angular Field-Convolutional Neural Network (GAF-CNN) framework to classify slope-grade groups from knee joint angle data. First, non-negative matrix factorization was employed to analyze muscle synergies during slope walking using an open-source dataset, indicating that the biceps femoris (BF), semitendinosus (ST), and rectus femoris (RF) were major contributors associated with knee-angle variations. Based on Pearson correlation analysis, a weighted combination of the root-mean-square (RMS) values of BF and RF was selected as a representative indicator. Using this indicator, the 27 treadmill slope levels (-6% to 20%) used in the experiments were clustered into 13 groups as classification labels. The three input representations (Data<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">stance</sub>, Data<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">swing</sub>, and Data<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">gait</sub>) were each evaluated using three CNN architectures-a 1D-CNN, a Gramian Summation Angular Field CNN (GASF-CNN), and a Gramian Difference Angular Field CNN (GADF-CNN)-resulting in nine input–model combinations. The GADF-CNN with Data<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">gait</sub> achieved the highest classification accuracy (95.27%), followed by Datastance (95.18%), whereas Data<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">swing</sub> yielded the lowest accuracy (95.05%), using five convolutional blocks. The proposed framework exhibited stable performance under leave-one-subject-out cross-validation and group-wise five-fold cross-validation, and demonstrated strong potential for real-time deployment. Overall, transforming 1D knee-angle sequences into 2D images via the GAF representation markedly improved slope-grade classification accuracy.
