title: Multi-class Classification of Parkinsonian Gait using inertial sensors: A preliminary study
authors: Emanuele Manoni, Salvatore Monteleone, Martina Patera, Valentina Bellia, Juri Taborri, Eduardo Palermo, Antonio Suppa, Fabrizio Patanè, Ilaria Mileti
publish: 2026 IEEE Medical Measurements &amp; Applications (MeMeA)
abstract: Freezing of Gait (FoG) is a disabling symptom of Parkinson’s Disease that significantly increases fall risk. This study proposes a machine learning-based approach to classify FoG episodes and functional motor tasks using wearable inertial sensors. Inertial sensors were positioned on anatomical landmarks, including the head, trunk, pelvis, and lower limbs, to capture kinematics during structured walking tests and an obstacle course. Data from accelerometers, gyroscopes, and magnetometers were processed to extract time and frequencydomain features for a multi-class classification involving FoG, 180° turns, 90° turns, and sitting transitions. Three configurations were tested for feature extraction, varying by sensor placement: all segments, the pelvis, and the feet. The results demonstrate the highest efficacy of the “all segments” configuration for robust detection. Using all segments, the Support Vector Machine (SVM) with a Cubic kernel and the Wide Neural Network both achieved a peak validation accuracy of 97.3%. The Cubic SVM model showed similar performance in discriminating complex motor triggers when using the pelvis and feet configurations, achieving accuracies of 94.5% and 93.8%, respectively. These findings suggest that integrating multiple body segments enhances classification accuracy, providing a reliable foundation for real-time FoG monitoring and intervention systems.

title: Characterization of CR-Compensated GAAS Detector for X-ray Detection Applications
authors: Zeyu Sun, Boren Wang, Zhijuan Su, Xiaochun Lai, Yaping Dan
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Photon-counting computed tomography (PCCT) has emerged as a transformative imaging technology, offering superior spatial resolution and energy discrimination. However, conventional tellurium-based detectors face significant challenges under high-flux conditions due to polarization effects and charge losses. This study presents an investigation of chromium-compensated gallium arsenide (GaAs:Cr) photoconductive devices as a viable alternative. Through detailed analysis of spectra responses, temperature-dependent dark current behavior, and detection performance across a flux range of 20-900 Mcps/mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> under 40 keV X-ray irradiation, we demonstrate the major defect levels, high resistivity and carrier transport mechanisms of the detector.

title: Design of a Recurrent Neural Network-Based Classifier for ECG Rhythm Classification Using Multi-Domain Hand-Crafted Features
authors: Hernando González Acevedo, Beatriz F. Giraldo
publish: 2026 IEEE Medical Measurements &amp; Applications (MeMeA)
abstract: The automatic classification of cardiac conditions—specifically Arrhythmia (ARR), Congestive Heart Failure (CHF), and Normal Sinus Rhythm (NSR)—from electrocardiogram (ECG) signals remains a critical challenge in clinical diagnostics. This paper presents the design of a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) classifier using ECG recordings from PhysioNet-derived datasets. The proposed methodology relies on a multi-domain hand-crafted feature extraction strategy to capture the complex dynamics of cardiac signals. To ensure temporal consistency, ECG signals were segmented into 10 -second windows, from which a comprehensive feature set was computed across four key domains: time-domain statistics, Hjorth parameters, frequency-domain spectral descriptors, and nonlinear measures derived from Poincaré plots. A statistical feature selection pipeline—combining the Kruskal–Wallis test, Benjamini–Hochberg False Discovery Rate (FDR) correction, and effect size analysis—was applied to retain the most discriminative and non-redundant predictors. To identify the most effective temporal modeling strategy, the classifier incorporates a Bayesian hyperparameter optimization framework that automatically selects the optimal recurrent architecture among Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Bidirectional LSTM (BiLSTM), and Gated Recurrent Units (GRU) topologies. This process simultaneously tunes model complexity and regularization parameters to mitigate overfitting. In addition, class imbalance is addressed through a cost-sensitive learning approach using weighted loss functions. The resulting classifier achieves strong discriminatory performance across the three rhythm classes, demonstrating that a Bayesian-optimized RNN architecture combined with domain-specific features provides a solution for cardiac rhythm analysis.

title: Rewiring the Mind for AI: A Pilot Study on Neuro-Cognitive Changes from Prompt Engineering Training
authors: Hend Al-Khalifa, Layan Abdulrahman Alhuasini, Raneem Almansour, Heyam Al-Baity, Alanood Alsaleh, Ashwag Rafea S Alruwaili
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: The emergence of large language models (LLMs) necessitates effective prompt engineering, a skill that requires complex cognitive engagement for optimal human-AI interaction. This pilot study, involving 13 adult participants, investigates whether structured prompt engineering training leads to measurable neuro-cognitive changes. Our preliminary findings reveal that such training is associated with increased functional connectivity in the left precentral and postcentral gyri, indicating neural plasticity. Using resting-state fMRI and a seed-based connectivity analysis, post-training results showed increased connectivity in left sensorimotor regions. This suggests the development of embodied cognitive strategies and more efficient neural resource allocation. These early results suggest that interacting with AI can reshape brain networks involved in executive planning and action selection, offering initial empirical evidence of prompt engineering as a driver of neuroplasticity in human-AI collaboration. Due to the pilot nature of this study, a control group was not included, and these findings should be interpreted as preliminary.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Optimizing Penalty Kick Strategies in Football: A Comparative Study of PPO and IPPO
authors: Muhammad Fadhlul Wafi Ahmad Naim, Azhar Mohd Ibrahim, Mohd Zaid, Ali Ahmed Allam
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Penalty kicks are among the most decisive and pressure-filled moments in football, often shaping the outcome of matches. Their complexity lies in the split-second decisions made by both kicker and goalkeeper, which traditional statistical or biomechanical approaches struggle to fully capture. In this paper, we explore how Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) can optimize penalty kick strategies using the Google Research Football (GRF) environment. We trained agents with two algorithms, Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and Independent PPO (IPPO), to model realistic kicker-goalkeeper interactions. Performance was analyzed through goal success rates, goalkeeper saves, and decision heatmaps. The trained IPPO agents exhibit a goal success rate exceeding 85%, a notable increase compared to baseline strategies which uses PPO agents (~65%). Spatial heatmaps further revealed a strong preference for low-corner shots, aligning with real-world tendencies. These findings show that DRL-trained agents can learn adaptive and effective penalty strategies, offering practical insights for football analytics, training, and tactical preparation.

title: C-MINT: A Causality-Aware Tokenized Multimodal Transformer for Infant Pain Estimation and Time-to-Relief Prediction
authors: Oussama El Othmani, Sami Naouali, Riadh Ouersighni
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Neonatal pain assessment is challenging due to infants’ inability to verbalize discomfort, often leading to subjective and variable evaluations using scales like NIPS and PIPP-R. We propose C-MINT, a Causality-Aware Multimodal Transformer, for precise pain estimation and time-to-relief prediction in neonatal intensive care units (NICUs). C-MINT integrates facial video, cry audio, and physiological signals (heart rate variability, electrodermal activity) using cross-modal tokenization, a structural causal layer, and diffusion-based counterfactual augmentation to ensure invariance to confounders like lighting or medical devices. It employs multi-instance ordinal regression for pain intensity estimation and a survival model for time-to-relief prediction. Evaluated on the Neonatal Pain Assessment Dataset (NPAD), C-MINT achieves 94.2% accuracy and 0.91 F1-score for pain estimation, with a 3.8-minute mean absolute error for time-to-relief, outperforming baselines by 10–15%. With uncertainty calibration and token-level attribution, C-MINT offers interpretable, real-time monitoring, enhancing neonatal care.

title: Optimized Hybrid Deep Learning Framework for Early Detection of Breast Cancer Using Image Processing Techniques
authors: Pralohith Reddy Chinthalapelly, Prabhu Krishnaswamy, Prabhu Muthusamy, Kalyan Kondisetty, Sandeep C S, MM. Poornima
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Despite the progress that has been made on breast cancer diagnosis and therapy, it continues to be one of the most common causes of death in women due to late-stage detection associated with low specificities. While it is crucial to identify metastases at the initial stage, contemporary data-driven analytical models cannot appropriately classify tumors of small diameters due to their inability to detect minute structural differences and background tissue appearances. In this study, we introduced an improved hybrid deep learning model for the analysis of lung cancer CT images based on CNN-identified spatial features and attention-based transformer network that were used to provide contextual attention (CT) feature combined with radiomic-handcrafted features in order to perform comprehensive multi-level analyses. The architecture improves texture comprehending, refine charactering of lesion border and making better discrimination between benign or malignant regions. With its multi-branch fusion method, this system outperforms even state-of-the-art single task models by large margins. The experimental result also demonstrates that the proposed method outperforms classical models such as ResNet-50, VGG16 and EfficientNet-B0 with 98.7% accuracy, 98.3% precision, 99.1% recall and AUC-ROC of (Area Under ROC) is calculated to be at (AUC = ~(0.993). Those opposing results reinforce the orientation of stability and clinical utility. This helps with transparency for the radiologists and makes this model usable in practice. This hybrid approach thus augments the performing traditional imaging modalities with context learning, integration of radiomic features and serves as an objective diagnostic adjunct for aiding in early detection.

title: A Deep Learning Approach to Oral Cancer Detection Using Swin Transformers
authors: R. RajaSekar, Shaik Mastan Vali, T. Siva Sai Rama SubbaReddy, Leela Krishna, Manoj Sai
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: Oral cancer is a deadly disease that can be treated more effectively if caught early. Although most of the diagnosis is conventional methods involve expert clinical examination and histopathological analysis, there is always a risk of delay in diagnosis. In this paper, a proposed deep learning approach based on the Swin Transformer structure to classify oral images from clinical RGB cameras for the detection of oral cancer is discussed. For this study, we have the dataset of 950 clinical images containing cancer and non-cancer images. The proposed model successfully achieves the task of local and global feature encoding in the classification task by using the hierarchical self-attention mechanism, which proves to be effective in Medical image analysis. Standard parameters such as accuracy, precision, recall and F1-score were employed to train and test the system. The experimental results showed that the accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score obtained from the proposed approach were 95%, 94%, 96%, and 95% respectively, which is reliable and effective. The results suggest that transformer-based deep learning models could serve as valuable tools for enabling accurate and timely oral cancer diagnoses in clinical practice.

title: An Agentic Approach to Faithful and Transparent Islamic Question Answering
authors: Hind Al-Owais, Ibrahim Hashem, Ashraf Elnagar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: In Islam, religious guidance is sensitive, as a slight deviation from Islamic law is unacceptable to Muslims. This paper proposes an Islamic multi-agent question answering system specifically desgined for Zakat, one of the five main pillars of islam that aims to increase transparency and trust in AI-generated religious QA systems. This system architecture is made up of five specialized agents: an Intent Classification Agent, a Retrieval Agent that retrieves fatwas using dense semantic search, a GPT-4 based Reasoning Agent, a Verification Agent that uses Natural Language Inference (NLI) that checks faithfulness, and an Answer Composition Agent responsible for the final formatted response. We tested the system on a test set of 100 Arabic Zakat questions. The results showed that the system achieved a mean Faithfulness score of 0.79, with 85% of responses accepted. Out of the accepted results, 53% rated Excellent, 32% rated Good, and overall 15% were rated Poor. The NLI Verification Agent evaluated 662 sentences in total, 89.4% were classified as Supported, 9.2% Unsupported and only 1.4% were classified as Contradicted. This demonstrates that the system rarely generates content that conflicts with its retrieved sources. The proposed architecture of our system provides a structural framework for faith-based question answering systems, where automated verification and scholar-in-the-loop oversight work together to make sure that no answer reaches the user without traceable grounding in authoritative fatwa sources.

title: SOP-Driven Knowledge Base Construction for Intelligent Insurance Workflow Orchestration
authors: Shyalendar Reddy Allala, Vinil Pasupuleti, Siva Rama Krishna Varma Bayyavarapu, Shrey Tyagi, Srinivasateja Songa
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Insurance new business processing relies heavily on Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) that encode underwriting rules, routing logic, and compliance constraints in semi-structured or unstructured textual formats. Manual interpretation of these documents introduces latency, inconsistency, and operational risk in workflow orchestration. This paper proposes an automated framework for knowledge base construction from business SOPs to enable intelligent workflow orchestration in insurance new business processing. The proposed architecture integrates document ingestion, natural language processing (NLP)-based knowledge extraction, rule formalization, and workflow execution layers. The system transforms unstructured procedural text into executable rule representations using entity recognition, dependency parsing, and ontology mapping. Extracted rules are formalized into machine-interpretable production rules and integrated into a rule-driven orchestration engine. A prototype implementation demonstrates improved rule extraction consistency and reduction in manual configuration effort. Experimental evaluation on underwriting SOP datasets shows promising precision and recall in rule extraction tasks, supporting the feasibility of automated SOP-driven workflow automation in regulated insurance environments.

title: A Machine Learning Pipeline for Early Detection of Autism Spectrum in Children
authors: Mariam Mahmoud Dahish, Reem Kadry Montasser, Tamer H. Younis, Tamer M. Nassef
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: autism spectrum disorder is a neurological disorder characterized by a set of symptoms, including difficulty in communication or social interaction. The significance of early diagnosis for children with autism is that it allows them to progress and develop outcomes in their lives. Also, traditional diagnosis is slow and requires long sessions and interviews with the family, and occasionally the diagnosis can be delayed for up to four years, particularly for people in resource-limited countries. This study aims to provide an ML pipeline to optimization accuracy through a collected dataset of toddlers to detect autism. Through data preprocessing, including handling missing values, encoding, and scaling. Also, 10 cross-validation, hyperparameter tuning, SMOTE have been applied to check a balanced dataset. We evaluated through multiple ML models, such as XGBoost, SVM, random forest, and logistic regression, to find the effective performance in ML. assessed their performance using accuracy, precision, recall, f1-score. Based on our results, XGBoost achieved an overall accuracy of 99%.

title: Combined Vramp and Modeling Analysis of IMD Reliability Assessment
authors: Qingyuan Qin, Bo Yang, Ling Tang, Zhuo-Jie Wu
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: This paper investigates the impact of metal CD reduction, CMP optimization, and photo optimization process on the IMD (Inter-Metal Dielectric) voltage ramp (Vramp) tailing effect by Monte Carlo simulation based on FA and experimental data. Simulation results indicate that photo optimization demonstrates superior efficacy in mitigating Vramp tailing compared to the CMP optimization and metal CD reduction process. Experimental data further validate these simulation findings. The study reveals that photo optimization achieves the most significant improvement in the Weibull shape parameter of breakdown voltage (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">BD</inf>) at 55LP interconnects, CMP optimization demonstrates moderate effectiveness, whereas metal CD reduction shows the weakest improvement trend. Our methodology can be implemented as an effective reliability assessment tool for technology qualification.

title: Development of a Cloud-Coordinated Mobile Virtual Power Plant Using Public-Service EVs for Community-Level Techno-Economic Valorization
authors: Selva Vengatesh M, Charles Raja S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: The rapid growth of rooftop photovoltaic (PV) prosumers has caused excessive midday surplus export at low feed-in tariffs, although evening peak demand still relies on expensive grid imports as there is no localized storage and coordinated operation. Meanwhile, electric vehicles (EV) which are in public service lie idle for long periods of time and can be used as a mobile energy storage asset. To mitigate these loopholes this paper proposes Regional Assisted Virtual Electro-mobilized Net-plant (RAVEN), which is a cloud coordinated dual-stage mobile virtual power plant (mVPP) framework anchored at a public distribution facility (ration shop). RAVEN combines (i) a cloud-aware automatism for the scheduling of EV charging/discharge by an AI-based scheduler (for tariff consistency) and (ii) a Power-to-Value-added Asset (P2VA) mechanism for time-shifted surplus valorization with deterministic revenue-sharing. A year-long simulation for the year 2035 of 520 households (24% PV penetration) with a capacity factor of 300 kWh for public service EV has been performed to demonstrate that compared with conventional and DRL-based strategies, RAVEN results in more renewable utilization (from 0.7686 to 0.8953), reduction of annual and peak grid imports by 68.78 MWh, reduction of grid energy cost by ₹0.619 million and avoidance of 56.06 tons of carbon dioxide (CO2) annually.

title: Multi-Scale Attention-Enhanced Convolutional Neural Network for Real-Time Object Detection and Scene Understanding in Dynamic Environments
authors: Fathimathul Rajeena P P, Rahoof P P, Sunder R, Rasmi A
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: The rapid increase in autonomous systems and intelligent surveillance systems has increased the desire for accurate real-time object detection and full scene understanding in dynamic environments. Traditional deep learning methods often fail to maintain detection performance under event conditions that include illumination variations, partial occlusions, or rapid camera movement/follow-up. Thus, in safety-critical applications, it will be common for traditional deep learning methods to underperform. This study introduces a new Multi-Scale Attention-Enhanced Convolutional Neural Network (MSA-CNN) that attempts to perform both object detection and scene understanding in real-time while optimizing runtime performance. The framework utilizes a hybrid architecture which includes multi-scale feature extraction, attention-guided feature fusion, and real-time processing to improve the robustness of MSA-CNN against environmental variations. We provide extensive experiments based on COCO, Cityscapes, and KITTI benchmark datasets. The evaluation is based on mean Average Precision (0.5:0.95), mean Intersection over Union (mIoU), and frames per second (FPS). The framework achieved 52.8 mAP score, 82.3 mIoU score, and utilized 45 FPS, outperforming other state-of-the-art baselines including YOLOv8, Deformable DETR, Mask R-CNN, and Sparse R-CNN. The results of MSA-CNN provide substantial improvements to performance with both detection and full segmentation performance in real-time environments and will allow for real-world colonization in autonomous vehicles and surveillance systems.

title: Digital Sovereignty Beyond the Cloud: An Edge AI Framework for Small Island Developing States
authors: Mohamed Shareef
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Small Island Developing States face an architectural choice in AI deployment that is rarely named as such. Most systems route computation through distant data centers, creating dependencies that hold until they do not. Submarine cable disruptions make visible what cloud-dependent AI cannot solve from within: these systems stop working precisely when disaster response and public decision-making are most critical. This is not a temporary coverage gap. It is architectural. The SIDS Sovereign Edge Framework (SSEF) addresses this through a three-layer architecture pairing distributed edge devices, Small Language Models, and indigenous knowledge systems, drawing on an integrative review and comparative analysis of deployments in Indonesia, India, and Pacific island nations, where edge AI systems operate on hardware costing under US$10 whilst functioning without internet connectivity. Across these deployments, distinct operational patterns emerge. Autonomous geographic systems maintained disaster response functions through extended cable failures; solar-powered diagnostic models sustained healthcare delivery without connectivity; hybrid forecasting that incorporated traditional ecological indicators improved precipitation accuracy by 15 to 30 percent over satellite-only baselines. The evidence suggests digital sovereignty through edge AI is technically feasible for SIDS. That feasibility, though, is conditional on architectural choices that prioritize local operation and task specificity over remote cloud platforms whose availability, in these contexts, cannot be assumed.

title: AI-Driven Personal Finance Optimization with Ensemble Learning, Time-Series Forecasting, and Reinforcement Learning
authors: Chaitanya Tumma, Supraja Ayyamgari, Abhignan Srivatsava Sribhashyam, Saicharan Kadari
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Effective management of personal finances is crucial for achieving long-term stability and enabling informed decision-making. This paper proposes an AI-powered framework for managing and predicting monthly expenses by leveraging advanced machine learning and deep learning techniques. The system processes diverse user-specific details, including demographics, dependents, recurring expenses (e.g., transport, education, subscriptions, savings, and healthcare), and lifestyle indicators, through an ensemble of gradient boosting algorithms (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost), deep neural networks (DNNs), and explainable AI tools such as SHAP and LIME to ensure model transparency.In addition to providing accurate predictions of expenditure and savings behavior, the framework incorporates time-series forecasting models, including Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks and Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCNs), to anticipate future financial conditions. Personalized financial strategies are further optimized using reinforcement learning agents, thereby offering tailored recommendations aligned with individual constraints and objectives. Beyond financial forecasting, the model also integrates lifestyle-oriented health profiling by combining dietary pattern analysis with multi-task learning, thus offering a holistic perspective on both financial and personal well-being.By integrating predictive analytics, interpretability, and adaptive decision-making, the proposed framework delivers actionable insights, uncovers spending patterns, and empowers users to exercise proactive control over their financial planning.

title: MuRAL: A Multi-Resident Ambient Sensor Dataset Annotated with Natural Language for Activities of Daily Living
authors: Xi Chen, Julien Cumin, Fano Ramparany, Dominique Vaufreydaz
publish: 2026 22nd International Conference on Intelligent Environments (IE)
abstract: Recent progress in Large Language Models (LLMs) has enabled advanced reasoning and zero-shot recognition for human activity understanding with ambient sensor data. However, widely used multi-resident datasets such as CASAS, ARAS, and MARBLE lack natural language context and fine-grained annotation, limiting the full exploitation of LLM capabilities in realistic smart environments. To address this gap, we present MuRAL (Multi-Resident Ambient sensor dataset with natural Language), comprising over 21 hours of multi-user sensor data from 21 sessions in a smart home. MuRAL uniquely features detailed natural language descriptions, explicit resident identities, and rich activity labels, all situated in complex, dynamic, multi-resident scenarios. We benchmark state-of-the-art LLMs on MuRAL for three core tasks: subject assignment, action description, and activity classification. Results show that current LLMs still face major challenges on MuRAL, especially in maintaining accurate resident assignment over long sequences, generating precise action descriptions, and effectively integrating context for activity prediction. The dataset is publicly available at: https://mural.imag.fr/.

title: Partnership in Purpose: Biologically Inspired Behavioral Modeling for Trust and Performance in Human–Quadruped Robot Team
authors: Heather C. Lum, Jana Q. Algharaibeh, Are’an Burr, Jack E. Culp, Kaarin G. Holter, Ewart J. de Visser, Daniel Holman, Richard E. Niemeyer
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: This study investigates how biologically inspired behaviors influence trust and performance in human–robot teams. Drawing from established human–animal teaming models, particularly the partnership between humans and working dogs, we examine whether analogous behavioral frameworks can enhance human–robot interaction. Using a Boston Dynamics Spot quadruped robot in a simulated disaster recovery environment, we compare two behavioral configurations, robotic and biologically inspired, while holding functional capabilities constant. Data collection is on-going. We expect that biologically inspired behaviors will improve user trust, enhance shared mental models, and lead to higher team performance. Results from our study may provide actionable design guidance for future semi-autonomous systems operating in high-risk environments.

title: A Comprehensive Tool for Modeling Non-Ideal Effects in Column-Driver Signal Chain of Micro-Led Displays
authors: Aoran Xu, Sihao Wu, Yi Zhuo, Haotong Zhu, Junchen Dong, Zheng Zhou, Lifeng Liu, Dedong Han
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: We present a simulation tool for modeling non-ideal effects in the column-driver signal chain of micro-light-emitting diode (Micro-LED) displays. The framework accounts for both driver-level distortions, such as clock jitter, current mirror mismatch, and RC delay along column lines, as well as pixel-level imperfections including crosstalk, external quantum efficiency degradation, and stuck defects. By integrating these effects into a unified flow, the tool produces distorted images and quantitative metrics, enabling intuitive visualization of the impact of chain non-ideal factors on the entire display system.

title: Detection of AQI Anomalies in Indian Cities Utilizing Self-Supervised Learning (SimCLR) and Isolation Forest
authors: Ashwini Raju Agashe, Advait Gadekar, Sharvari Deshmukh, Ayesha Khan, Samiha Khan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)

title: Advanced Deep Learning Frameworks for DDoS Attack Forecasting with Neurosymbolic Reasoning
authors: Prakasam Venkatachalam
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Modern Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attacks exploit dynamic traffic patterns and adversarial drift, rendering static detection systems increasingly ineffective. This paper presents the Adaptive Threat Cognition Framework (ATCF), a modular deep learning architecture designed to forecast, interpret, and adapt to evolving DDoS threats. ATCF integrates four core components: a Temporal Encoding System (TES) for real-time traffic profiling, a Neurosymbolic Reasoning Engine (NRE) for interpretable threat inference, a Feature-Domain Multimodal Transformer (FDMT) for cross-domain fusion, and a Co-evolutionary Meta-Cognition Layer (CMCL) for adaptive learning under adversarial conditions. Evaluated across benchmark datasets and adversarial simulations, ATCF consistently outperforms existing models in precision, recall, and explainability. The framework demonstrates resilience against concept drift and offers actionable insights for real-world deployment in cyber-physical infrastructures.

title: Autonomous vs Direct User Preference in the Control of a Knee Exoskeleton
authors: James Lee, Eduardo Silva, Yousuf Shafiu, Kayson Hergert, Zachary Bucknor-Smartt, Gray C. Thomas
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: A key barrier to the widespread adoption of assistive exoskeletons is limited user agency in their control. Many assistive lower-limb exoskeletons (ALLEs) largely rely on autonomous control algorithms intended to mimic biologically optimal walking torque profiles. However, this biomimetic walking torque may not accurately reflect the user’s true preferences. This work makes three primary contributions: (1) a comparative evaluation of personalized user-generated torque profiles and biomimetic torque profiles, (2) an investigation into user preference between direct user control and autonomous torque delivery, and (3) the development of a novel gait state representation enabling more flexible and robust real-time gait tracking. Real-time gait phase estimation is accomplished using an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF). Output torque is delivered to the user’s knee via a lookup table of either biomimetic or user-generated torque profiles based on the percent of the gait phase estimated via an EKF. During the study, participants walk using direct control, the autonomously controlled biomimetic profile, and the autonomously controlled direct user profile. Finally, the user compares the given choices, selecting their preferred method and torque scheme. This study is conducted with three participants to determine whether the chosen model for the autonomous control system is robust enough for use in a larger trial with more participants.

title: Impact of Memory-Gate Length on Trap Rebalancing and Reliability in 3T2b SONOS Flash
authors: Zhexuan Li, Xuan Li, Yunhao Zhang, Guangji Li, Kun Ren, Dawei Gao
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: The effect of memory-gate length on charge-trapping and reliability characteristics is systematically investigated in a 3T2b SONOS memory structure. Using TCAD simulations combined with electrical measurements, it is revealed that shorter memory gates lead to fewer residual trapped electrons and a higher proportion of injected holes after program/erase (P/E) cycling. This results in a trap rebalancing effect, which mitigates electron-hole mismatch and improves erase efficiency, memory window, and endurance. The results provide physical insight into the relationship between gate-length scaling, trap dynamics, and device reliability, offering design guidance for future high-density embedded charge-trapping memories.

title: Evolution of the Potential Export Region for Building Large-Scale Wind Power Plants in Southern Mongolia
authors: Mangaljalav Chimed, Munkhtuya Erdenebat, Chimed Orshuu, Batgerel Tumurbaatar
publish: 2025 International Symposium on Computer Science and Educational Technology (ISCSET)
abstract: Mongolia's power system is currently underdeveloped and underutilized, with consumer capacity last year limited to around 150 MW to manage peak winter loads. The current and future energy sector policies of the Mongolian government emphasize in-creasing the share of renewable energy, particularly through the installation of high-capacity wind power plants in the Gobi region. Efficient and optimal utilization of large-scale renewable energy resources and their integration into the national energy system have become critical challenges for Mongolia. In this study, we analyzed the wind energy resources in the Southern Gobi region of Mongolia, based on wind resource measurements, followed by mathematical processing and resource evaluation. We determined the potential capacity for wind power plants based on these resources. Additionally, we examined the impact of integrating large-scale wind power plants into the southern region's power grid on parallel operation modes. The wind resource assessment was conducted using Windographer simulation software.

title: Parameter Optimization and Simulation of Induction Motors using Grey Relational Analysis: dq vs. Winding Function Approach
authors: Otgonchimeg Choidorj, Ariunbolor Purvee, Erdenetsetseg Saijaa
publish: 2025 International Symposium on Computer Science and Educational Technology (ISCSET)
abstract: This study presents a comparative analysis of two simulation approaches-the direct-quadrature (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$d q$</tex>) transformation and the winding function method-for modeling a squirrel cage induction motor, with the goal of minimizing the deviation between simulated outputs and the motor's nameplate parameters. Grey Relational Analysis (GRA) was employed to optimize six key input parameters, enhancing the accuracy of the motor's dynamic behavior representation. Simulation models were developed in MATLAB/Simulink, and key performance metrics-rotational speed (RPM), torque, and stator current-were evaluated under both steady-state and transient conditions. The optimized simulations showed a high degree of agreement with the nameplate parameters, achieving <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 8. 5 - 9 9 \%}$</tex> accuracy. Based on these results, frequency spectrum and time-domain analyses further validated the accuracy of both simulation methods against field measurements. The winding function-based simulation proved particularly effective in identifying fault characteristic frequencies for early fault detection, while the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$d q$</tex>-based simulation was more suitable for motor drive development. This paper also presents time-and frequency-domain analyses of the winding function-based model under various motor fault conditions, along with a comparative study of control strategies for squirrel cage and wound rotor induction motors using the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$d q$</tex>-based model.

title: Joint Classification of HSI and LiDAR Data based on Heterogeneous Double-Branch Network
authors: Manman Yao, Haoran Lv, Mingji Yang, Aili Wang, Haibin Wu, Haisong Chen
publish: 2025 International Symposium on Computer Science and Educational Technology (ISCSET)
abstract: The collaborative classification of multi-source remote sensing data is of great significance to improve the accuracy of ground object classification, but the modal differences and distribution inconsistencies between heterogeneous data make it difficult for traditional methods to fully explore the cross-modal correlation features. This paper proposes a multi-modal joint classification approach built on heterogeneous double-branch network. In order to meet the hierarchical expression requirements of LiDAR elevation features, a heterogeneous double-branch convolution network was designed: 1) two sets of parallel multi-scale convolutions were used to aggregate elevation information to enhance the representation of local details and global context; 2) Extracting Hyperspectral Imaging (HSI) spectral-spatial features and LiDAR elevation features, respectively. The two-way Cross-Attention fusion module was further constructed. Experiments on the Trento and Augsburg datasets show that the overall classification accuracy (OA) of the proposed method reaches 99.55 % and 95.11 %, respectively, and the classification performance of sensitive categories such as roads and buildings are significantly improved, which verify the superiority of the proposed framework.

title: MADDPG For Optimal EVCS, RES And BESS Placement For Distribution Network Resilience
authors: E. Kalyana Chakravarthy, A. Lakshmi Devi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: Radial distribution networks face resilience vulnerabilities from the simultaneous expansion of Electric Vehicle Charging Stations (EVCSs) and Renewable Energy Sources (RES), demanding adaptive planning frameworks that collectively address infrastructure hardening, fault withstanding, and service recovery. Existing approaches treat these phases in isolation: classical metaheuristic optimizers handle placement as a static single-objective problem, while single-agent deep reinforcement learning methods lack the cooperative specialization needed to coordinate heterogeneous resources under a unified resilience objective. No existing study incorporates progressive fault-severity training or topology-aware state encoding within a multi-agent placement framework. This paper proposes a Multi-Agent Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (MA-DDPG) framework under Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution (CTDE) for simultaneous siting and sizing of EVCSs, RES comprising photovoltaic systems and wind turbines and Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) in radial distribution networks. Four cooperative specialist agents interact with the network environment through a shared Graph Neural Network (GNN) topology encoder that produces spatially-aware state representations capturing neighborhood-level electrical sensitivity information. A Composite Resilience Reward (CRR) integrating six complementary metrics drives cooperative policy learning across all resilience phases. Validation on IEEE 33-bus and 69-bus test systems demonstrates SAIDI reductions with substantial improvements across all six resilience metrics.

title: Improving Switching and Static Characteristics of 60 V LDMOS: A Study on the Influence of Field Plate, Gate, and Drift Region Design
authors: Boying Meng, Yiting Ye, Yan Pan, Kai Xu
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: The lateral double-diffused MOSFET (LDMOS) is widely used in power management applications, particularly as a power switching device. Enhancing its switching and static characteristics is crucial for minimizing power consumption in integrated circuits. This study focuses on the mechanism analysis of a 60 V LDMOS device featuring a shallow trench isolation (STI) structure. Specifically, the influences of an oxide field plate, the gate, and the N-type drift region on key performance parameters including breakdown voltage, specific on-resistance, and gate charge, are investigated, and their physical mechanisms are elucidated. Finally, an optimized device design is proposed that maintains satisfactory electrical characteristics.

title: Class-Conditioned Synthetic Retinal OCT Dataset Generation Using Diffusion Model
authors: Yanhua Zhao, Megha Deepa Nagarajupally, Christoph Hoog Antink
publish: 2026 IEEE Medical Measurements &amp; Applications (MeMeA)
abstract: Optical coherence tomography (OCT) is a noninvasive imaging modality widely used in ophthalmology, providing high-resolution visualization of retinal microstructures essential for diagnosing various retinal diseases. However, the development of robust AI systems for retinal disease detection is often hindered by limited availability of labeled OCT datasets. In this study, we explore the use of denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) to generate high-quality synthetic OCT images as a strategy to mitigate data scarcity. We propose a classconditioned DDPM framework capable of synthesizing retinal images across four diagnostic categories: choroidal neovascularization (CNV), diabetic macular edema (DME), drusen, and normal retina. The model leverages a U-Net backbone to guide the iterative denoising process, enabling faithful reconstruction of fine-grained retinal structures. The fidelity and diversity of the generated images were quantitatively assessed using the Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), achieving an overall FID of 16.77 across 8,000 generated images. Clinical utility was evaluated through a ResNet-50 classifier trained under four scenarios: realonly, synthetic-only, combined, and traditionally augmented. The combined training scenario achieved the highest classification accuracy of $99.79 \%$ and $\mathbf{F 1}$ score of 0.9979, demonstrating that synthetic augmentation can enhance classification performance without degrading established diagnostic accuracy. These findings highlight the potential of diffusion-based generative models to augment ophthalmic AI systems and support more robust and generalizable diagnostic models in retinal disease research.

title: A Cost-Efficient Reinforcement Learning Framework for Semiconductor Process Recipe Optimization
authors: Lin Fu, Dong Ni, Siwei Fu
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: This study presents a reinforcement learning framework for semiconductor process recipe optimization by formulating the tuning task as a Markov decision process. In a simulated setting, the agent iteratively observes the current optimization state, selects small recipe updates, and improves its policy from the resulting feedback, thereby learning adaptive tuning strategies. We evaluate the framework on standard, widely used benchmark functions designed to reflect representative process behaviors. Across these tests, the learned policy converges more quickly than Bayesian optimization under comparable evaluation budgets and demonstrates better use of prior knowledge, helping reduce the number of costly experimental evaluations needed in practice.

title: Recognizing Occupancy and Activities with PIR Sensors and LSTM-RNN
authors: Muhammad Abid Hussain, Muhammad Arslan, Taymoor Khalid, Heider A. M. Wahsheh, Mohsin Raza Jafri, Wajeeha Tariq Ansari
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: In the realm of monitoring systems, the need for advanced and accurate human detection has become increasingly vital for various applications, ranging from smart healthcare and monitoring to automation and smart environments. Considering its potential to improve the accuracy and efficacy of monitoring systems, human classification within a multi-subject monitoring scenario has attracted a lot of interest. Human Activity Recognition (HAR) has greatly increased in accuracy thanks to the quick development of numerous types of sensors. In this research, we introduce a novel HAR and Multi-Subject classification technique, leveraging state-of-the-art deep learning technique i.e. Long Short-Term Memory - Recurrent Neural Network (LSTM-RNN). The proposed method accurately detects movement direction and activities using fused information from network-connected PIR sensors. Prior to performing feature enhancement on the data, we first gather and examine the PIR sensor’s time-domain signal. We then aggregate the data based on peak time sequence features. The LSTM-RNN is then used to predict the activities and direction of moving subject. Lastly, results are used to validate the effectiveness of the method through experiments. The proposed model accurately classifies the direction of movement of an individual along with different types of physical activities, specifically walking, running, and falling with 99% accuracy. It classifies combinations or the number of individuals present in a test space with an accuracy of 96%.

title: Temperature Control of ESC Under Cold Wafer Chucking Disturbance Using Modern Two Degrees-of-Freedom Controller Design
authors: Jiaxuan Zhang, Yuanzhe Zhao, Zhiyuan Liu, Boyang Mu, Kai Zhang
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Etching in semiconductor manufacturing requires precise temperature control of the electrostatic chuck (ESC) to ensure process reproducibility. The cold-chucking procedure, where a low-temperature wafer is clamped onto a heated ESC, induces temperature fluctuations that degrade control performance. This paper proposes a two degrees-of-freedom control strategy that estimates and compensates for cold-chucking as an external unmeasurable load. A simplified first-order-plus-time-delay (FOPTD) model of the ESC is derived, and a robust observer is designed. Our simulations shows the proposed method effectively suppresses temperature fluctuations, enhances process stability and throughput, and ensures robustness in the presence of parametric uncertainties.

title: IoT-Enabled Smart Medicine Container for Expiration Safety and Refill Alerts Using Weight and RFID/NFC Sensing
authors: Karar H. Hussein, Mohammed J. Alhasan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Medication nonadherence and unsafe home medication practices remain persistent challenges, particularly for patients managing multiple medicines over long periods. In parallel, household surveys show that expired or unnecessary medicines are commonly present in homes, increasing the likelihood of unintentional use when expiry dates are overlooked. This paper proposes a low-cost IoT-enabled medicine container that provides (i) proactive expiration warnings, (ii) remaining-quantity and refill notifications, and (iii) scheduled dose-time reminders with in-app patient acknowledgement based on weight sensing, with optional RFID/NFC or barcode-based registration to reduce user burden. The design adopts an edge–cloud architecture: an embedded node acquires and filters sensor readings, estimates remaining doses, detects consumption events, and synchronizes concise state updates to a cloud service that enforces alert policies and delivers notifications to the patient and (optionally) a caregiver. A practical methodology for dose estimation, depletion prediction, and alert triggering is presented, together with an implementation plan for a proof-of-concept prototype and evaluation protocol. The proposed system aims to unify safety (expiry awareness) and availability (refill readiness) in a single home-use workflow with minimal manual interaction.

title: A Structurally Optimized SiON Passivation for High-Voltage MIM Capacitors
authors: Wenbin Huang, Ran Tao, Senlin Wang, Chih-sheng Keng, Huaxu Zhao, Lida Chen, Yuzhe Su, Luhao Zhou, Dawei Gao
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: To enhance the breakdown voltage of capacitive digital isolators, the reliability of the core metal-insulator-metal (MIM) capacitor was investigated. This work explores incorporating a silicon oxynitride (SiON) film into the passivation stack. Using technology computer-aided design (TCAD) simulations, the impact of SiON film thickness was analyzed, and two device architectures were compared. Results show a modified structure, where a conformal SiON layer encapsulates the top electrode corners, significantly mitigates electric field concentration. This design yields a substantial performance increase in specific thickness ranges, demonstrating a promising path toward more robust high-voltage isolators.

title: Early Detection of Lung Cancer Using Clinical Data and Machine Learning Methods
authors: Bishal Jaysawal, Rahul Raj Sah, Mahamad Sahjad Dewan, Ujjwal Kumar, Aman Kumar, Smita Rani Parija
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Lung cancer remains the main reason for cancer-related deaths. Treatment outcomes and survival chances are significantly improved by early detection. In an attempt to assess the risk of lung cancer, a predictive model using machine learning techniques, which takes advantage of structured demographic and clinical data, including age, smoking status, exhaustion, comorbidities, and pulmonary symptoms, is proposed in this study. Data preparation techniques including feature normalization and missing value imputation were applied to ensure model stability and consistency. Decision Tree, Logistic Regression and Random Forest were some of the classifiers used to test the models based on metrics such as recall, accuracy, F1-score and precision. Based on the prediction metrics, The Random Forest model’s accuracy was the highest at 89%. Results indicate that structured clinical data and machine learning can provide a cost effective, scalable, and practical decision-support tool for identifying individuals at risk of lung cancer at an early stage, particularly in healthcare systems with limited resources.

title: Influences of the Deformable Chuck on Post Bonding Distortion During the Expansion Error Compensation in Wafer-to-Wafer Hybrid Bonding
authors: Wanli Guo, Jiayang Li, Ziqi Wu, Jianing Liang
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: This study investigates the influence of expansion error compensation strategies employing a deformable chuck on post-bonding distortion in hybrid wafer-to-wafer bonding processes, since post-bonding distortion has emerged as a critical concern in next-generation hybrid bonding technologies. Through a combined experimental and simulation-based approach of the bonding dynamics, the research analyzes the chuck's impact on wafer expansion error and distortion residuals. Furthermore, a novel chuck deformation optimization strategy is proposed, achieving concurrent mitigation of expansion errors and nonlinear distortion residuals.

title: Artificial Intelligence-Based Multi-Disease Detection System Using Ensemble Learning Techniques
authors: Rakshith D N, K. Aditya Shastry, Rohit Naik, Sai Kumar C
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) has emerged as a transformative technology in healthcare, enabling automated and accurate disease prediction based on patient symptoms. This study proposes an Artificial Intelligence-based Multi-Disease Detection System that integrates classical machine learning models—Decision Tree, Naive Bayes, and Logistic Regression—with a Deep Neural Network (DNN) using an ensemble voting strategy to improve diagnostic accuracy and reliability. A key contribution of this work is the integration of Sentence-BERT (SBERT) for semantic mapping of natural language symptom descriptions into structured feature representations, enabling intuitive and user-friendly interaction. The system utilizes a symptom-based dataset containing 377 features and 41 disease classes, supporting scalable multi-class prediction. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed ensemble model achieves an accuracy of 86.25%, outperforming individual models while maintaining computational efficiency and interpretability. Additionally, the system is deployed as a real-time web application capable of providing probabilistic predictions with low latency, making it suitable for practical clinical and telehealth environments. The proposed framework contributes a lightweight, interpretable, and deployable AI-based diagnostic support tool that enhances early disease screening, improves accessibility to healthcare decision support, and enables scalable integration into digital health systems. The findings highlight the potential of hybrid ensemble learning combined with semantic symptom processing to advance intelligent, accessible, and real-time clinical decision support solutions.

title: Gastric Cancer Histopathology Classification Using Deep Learning Models with Grad-CAM Visualization
authors: Tripty Singh, Anantha Hothri Inuguri, Dorai Sai Charan Madisetty, Prakash Duraisamy, Afnaan K
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Gastric cancer is one of the most common reasons for cancer-related mortality, and the diagnosis is based on the analysis of histopathological images. But the complexity of the tumor microenvironment is making it difficult to interpret, and this is where the need for reliable computer-assisted tools is felt. Deep learning has also shown promising results in medical imaging analysis, but the application of deep learning in histopathological images is limited by the complexity of tissue morphology and lack of interpretability. This study proposes a deep learning framework for the classification of gastric cancer tissues using a publicly available dataset consisting of approximately 31,000 histopathology images in eight classes.The conventional CNN models, VGG16 and ResNet50, are used for evaluation, and a fusion method is proposed, in which the features are integrated by using both global context features from a Vision Transformer and local structural features from a Graph Neural Network using a cross-attention method. To increase interpretability, the Grad-CAM method is used to highlight the discriminative parts in the images that affect the model’s classification results. From the experimental results, it can be observed that the fusion model provides better consistency in classification results, making it suitable for the analysis of images in the context of computer-assisted gastric cancer pathology using deep learning models and feature interpretability.

title: A Post-Quantum Cryptographic Framework for Securing DDoS Mitigation in IoT-Driven Networks
authors: Shibhani Shanker, Manogar Ellapan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: The rapid proliferation of the Internet of Things (IoT) has brought in severe security threats, especially through Distributed Denial of Service attacks (DDoS), which target resource-constrained devices. Simultaneously, advances in quantum computers threaten current cryptographic defenses and demand the adoption of post-quantum cryptography. Presented herein is a novel framework that leverages lattice-based algorithms, freshly approved by National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), namely CRYSTALS-Kyber and CRYSTALS-Dilithium, and effective DDoS mitigation approaches suited to the IoT environment. Among the key contributions are lightweight authentication with optimized computational overhead and susceptibilities to side-channel attacks, an adaptive traffic filtering mechanism using concepts of software-defined networking, and hybrid implementation techniques that facilitate gradual migration toward quantum-resistant security. Performance evaluation reveals authentication latencies below one second and sufficient throughput during an active attack. This work fills the significant gaps by offering holistic security that balances the demands on resistance to quantum threats and the resource limitations of the IoT, together with immediate defense against DDoS for the post-quantum future.

title: Effect of Surfactants on the Properties of C-, R-, and M-Plane Sapphire
authors: Mengqi Wang, Xinhuan Niu, Bin Hu, Jiakai Zhou, Ziliang Liu, Qing Ma, Shaobo Song
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Chemical mechanical polishing (CMP) serves as a primary technique for producing ultra-smooth sapphire surfaces. Slurry is the key factor affecting the quality of CMP. Among its components, surfactants play a pivotal role in regulating the behaviour at the polishing interface. This study investigates the effects of two amphoteric surfactants, lauryl dimethylamine oxide (OA-12) and lauryl betaine (BS-12), on the surface roughness Sq and material removal rate (MRR) of C-, R-, and M-plane sapphire CMP. Results indicates that both surfactants effectively enhance the MRR and surface quality, with BS-12 demonstrating superior overall performance.

title: Dynamic Network Evolution Modeling with Time-Series Structural Features
authors: Anisha Kumari, Rishabh Shukla, Harsh Rastogi, Anubhav Singh, Hrishant Atri, Aryan Jaiswal, Ranjan Kumar Behera
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Dynamic social networks change as nodes tend to form or break relationships making future link prediction an essential task for recommendation, community analysis, and anomaly detection.Traditional link predictions based on static predictors cannot model changing interactions and lead to incomplete models. To solve this problem, a dynamics link prediction framework that employs temporal structural similarity metrics and supervised machine learning is proposed. Dynamics networks can be considered as sequence snapshots. Temporal similarity metrics such as Temporal Common Neighbors, Temporal Jaccard, Temporal Adamic–Adar and Dynamic Preferential Attachment are calculated for each node pair. Feature vectors obtained from metrics are used to train Decision Tree (DT), Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Machine(SVM), and K-Nearest Neighbor(KNN) algorithms. Experimental results indicate that the most accurate classifier is Decision Tree with 0.9436 accuracy and 0.938 recall. It indicates strong ability.

title: EfficientNetV2-S Based Automated Glaucoma Detection Using Retinal Fundus Images
authors: Bhavna Thakur, Seerat Singla, G Sunil, Sonal Malhotra, Kamlesh Kukreti, Aryan Gupta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Glaucoma stands out as the leading cause of irreversible blindness in patients; hence, early diagnosis of this condition is paramount for effective treatment. This paper proposes an automated glaucoma diagnosis from the retinal fundus image using the EfficientNetV2 model, known for its efficiency and accuracy in image classification tasks. For image classification of patients' data from the SMDG-19 dataset into two classes, namely glaucoma existence (1) and glaucoma absence (0), the proposed model was effective in handling the issue of data imbalance in medical image classification, such as in this study. Since medical image classification has data imbalance, this research applies oversampling methods, weighted loss, and data augmentation, hence effectively addressing the issue of data imbalance in medical image classification. Due to its architecture, the proposed model, EfficientNetV2-S, is effective in recognizing minute changes within the optic nerve head and RNFL layers, stands indispensable in glaucoma diagnosis. This model demonstrated an accuracy rate of 93%, with precision at 0.93 and sensitivity of 0.96, denoting high detection sensitivity of accurate positive outcomes. This model also achieved an F1-score of 0.95, denoting the perfect balance of accuracy and sensitivity in detection of glaucoma within patients. This paper underscores the important contribution of deep learning in the improvement of picture analysis in medical diagnosis.

title: Enhanced Frequency Stability in Low-Inertia Shipboard Microgrid using Virtual Inertia Control
authors: Pratap Chandra Nayak, Rupali Balabantaraya, Amit Kumar Panda, Om Prakash Pradhan, Sunita Pahadasingh, Ramesh Chandra Prusty
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Increasing the incorporation of renewable energy resources into shipboard microgrid (S-μG) is an essential strategy to combat the depletion of fossil fuels and environmental issues that degrade the inertia of S-μG and adversely impact its frequency stability. This decrease in inertia raises the danger of frequency instability and lessens the S-μG's capacity to manage disruptions. In order to enhance the system's inertia and frequency stability, this research presents a virtual inertia (VI) control approach for an S-μG using an energy storage system (ESS). The Starfish Optimization Algorithm (SFOA) is used to optimize the VI controller's parameters. The suggested VI control approach can successfully lower the risk of frequency instability and enhance S-μG frequency stability. The outcomes show that the suggested approach is efficient and capable of giving the S-μG quick and dependable virtual inertia assistance. The suggested VI control strategy outperforms the traditional inertial response for frequency stability in S-μG. During frequency fluctuations, virtual inertia control adds or removes active power from the S-μG. To illustrate the behavior of uncertain load and RES generation, various disturbances are used. The suggested approach improves control over frequency deviation. Additionally, compared to the current approaches, the results show a more reliable and effective system operation with the designed VI controller.

title: Deep Learning-Based Detection of Cluster Bomb Submunitions Using YOLO
authors: Sadek Fakih, Abbas Rammal, Mostafa Rizk
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Unexploded ordnance and cluster munitions pose a significant threat to civilians in conflict-affected regions, necessitating efficient and reliable detection methods. This paper proposes an artificial intelligence–based approach for detecting cluster bomb submunitions using advanced object detection models. The study investigates the performance of recent models of the You Only Look Once (YOLO) family, with a focus on YOLOv8 and a comparison with the recent YOLO11n architecture, for detecting small objects with diverse shapes, and features. A novel dataset comprising 1080 images and 2121 annotations across five submunition classes commonly found in the Middle East is introduced. To address class imbalance, a balanced dataset of 8,500 images is generated using additional samples and data augmentation techniques. Multiple variants of YOLOv8 are trained and evaluated on the original dataset, followed by experiments using YOLOv8n and YOLO11n on the balanced dataset.Experimental results demonstrate strong detection performance, achieving a mean average precision (mAP) of approximately 84% for the YOLOv8m model. Furthermore, dataset balancing significantly improves performance, yielding recall of 0.95 and mAP of 0.97. These findings highlight the effectiveness of YOLO-based models and the importance of balanced data in improving detection accuracy for critical real-world applications.

title: Mixture of Experts Approach for Cross-Lingual Detection of AI-Generated Arabic Text
authors: Kaoutar Zita, Attia Nehar, Abdelkader Khelil, Slimane Bellaouar, Hadda Cherroun
publish: 2026 22nd International Conference on Intelligent Environments (IE)
abstract: This study addresses the gap in cross-lingual detection of AI-generated Arabic text by constructing a balanced cross-lingual dataset that includes both original Arabic texts and Arabic translations of human-written and AI-generated English content. To manage the linguistic variability characteristic of this setting, we propose a Mixture of Experts framework that integrates several pretrained language models through a gating mechanism designed to leverage their complementary capabilities. Experimental results show that the proposed Mixture of Experts approach outperforms individual models, achieving F1-scores of 99.28 % in original Arabic texts and 97.75 % in translated texts, highlighting its effectiveness for robust cross-lingual Arabic AI-generated text detection.

title: A Machine Learning Driven Analysis of Employment Outcomes and Curriculum Relevance in a State University in the Philippines vs. Global Graduate Employability
authors: Liezl Joy L. Quilang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: This study examined the employment outcomes and curriculum relevance of Bachelor of Secondary Education major in Mathematics graduates from a state university in the Philippines using a mixed-methods descriptive-evaluative design integrating tracer study data, employer feedback, and a machine learning framework. Anchored in human capital theory, the study clearly distinguishes employment outcome as the target variable and retains only predictors that are conceptually and temporally independent of that outcome to reduce data leakage. Local tracer variables describe graduates’ educational background, curriculum experience, competency development, employer feedback, and satisfaction levels, while global employability indicators are used as contextual benchmarks aligned by comparable features rather than as direct substitutes for local records. The machine learning design emphasizes hyperparameter tuning, model comparison, robust validation, and feature-attribution analysis to generate policy-relevant insights. Findings continue to show generally positive employment outcomes and strong curriculum relevance for entry into the education sector, while also indicating the need to strengthen research competency, critical thinking, teamwork, creativity, and diligence to better align curricular investments with evolving employability demands. The study concludes that tracer evidence becomes more decision-relevant when descriptive results are integrated with leakage-aware and explainable machine learning analysis.

title: STT-SSCAF: Transformer-Based Infant Pain Estimation with Self-Supervised Learning and Multimodal Fusion
authors: Oussama El Othmani, Sami Naouali, Riadh Ouersighni
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Accurate infant pain assessment is essential in neonatal care but remains challenging due to subtle expressions and limited labeled data. We introduce STT-SSCAF, a novel transformer-based architecture that leverages self-supervised pretraining and cross-attention fusion to estimate pain severity from facial videos. By integrating masked autoencoders, dual-branch transformers for spatio-temporal modeling, and optional multimodal signals, the model employs ordinal regression to predict pain levels (0–3). Evaluated on the iCOPE dataset, STT-SSCAF achieves 84.6% ± 0.7% accuracy and a quadratic weighted kappa (QWK) of 0.82, surpassing baseline models, with near real-time performance on GPU hardware. This work advances neonatal affective computing, offering a robust solution for clinical deployment.

title: Risk-Aware Human-AI Collaboration Framework for High-Stakes Decision Systems
authors: Tejas Pravinbhai Patel, Madhushree Kumari, Milan Parikh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: High-stakes decision systems increasingly incorporate AI, yet existing approaches either over-automate decisions or rely on static escalation rules, leading to accountability gaps. We present a risk-aware collaboration framework that dynamically routes decisions to AI-only, human-only, or hybrid modes based on predicted risk combining uncertainty and impact. Evaluated across three domains (loan approval, medical triage, infrastructure maintenance), the framework achieves 34–47% reduction in critical errors and 42–58% reduction in unnecessary escalations versus baselines, with severity-weighted F1 scores of 0.76–0.89. The system provides complete audit trails and structured explanations for accountability.

title: Designing Cost-Optimal Human-AI Workflows for Retrieval-Augmented Generation: Analytical Framework and Industrial Case Study
authors: Jochen Wulf, Jürg Meierhofer, Manuel Dömer
publish: 2026 IEEE Swiss Conference on Data Science and AI (SDS)
abstract: As Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems are increasingly integrated into high-stakes service operations, organizations struggle to balance the efficiency of automation with the risks of AI hallucination. This paper proposes an analytical cost-benefit framework to quantify the Total Cost per Customer Query (TCQ) across five Human-AI interaction modes: Human-Augmented, Human-in-Control, Human-in-the-Loop, Human-on-the-Loop, and Human-out-of-the-Loop. We derive analytical boundary conditions that identify the break-even points for transitioning between automation levels. In a technical customer service case study in Swiss manufacturing we find that Human-in-the-Loop achieves the lowest TCQ with a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0 \%}$</tex> reduction compared to Human-in-Control or Human-out-of-the-Loop alternatives. Sensitivity analysis identifies AI generation accuracy as the dominant cost driver, followed by retrieval success and evaluator performance. The framework provides managers with a decision-support tool to select cost-optimal automation strategies based on their organization's risk profile and AI system maturity.

title: Speech as a Multimodal Digital Phenotype for Multi-Task LLM-based Mental Health Prediction
authors: Mai Ali, Christopher Lucasius, Tanmay P. Patel, Madison Aitken, Jacob Vorstman, Peter Szatmari, Marco Battaglia, Deepa Kundur
publish: 2025 International Conference on Smart Multimedia (ICSM)
abstract: Speech is a noninvasive digital phenotype that can offer valuable insights into mental health conditions, but it is often treated as a single modality. In contrast, we propose the treatment of patient speech data as a trimodal multimedia data source for depression detection. This study explores the potential of large language model-based architectures for speech-based depression prediction in a multimodal regime that integrates speech-derived text, acoustic landmarks, and vocal biomarkers. Adolescent depression presents a significant challenge and is often comorbid with multiple disorders, such as suicidal ideation and sleep disturbances. This presents an additional opportunity to integrate multi-task learning (MTL) into our study by simultaneously predicting depression, suicidal ideation, and sleep disturbances using the multimodal formulation. We also propose a longitudinal analysis strategy that models temporal changes across multiple clinical interactions, allowing for a comprehensive understanding of the conditions’ progression. Our proposed approach, featuring trimodal, longitudinal MTL is evaluated on the Depression Early Warning dataset. It achieves a balanced accuracy of 70.8%, which is higher than each of the unimodal, single-task, and non-longitudinal methods.
